Dialogue:
Judge: Justice Alito mentioned the Reich case as well, and that's another one where it was a forged court order that prompted the litigant to dismiss a mandamus petition, but that didn't have anything to do with the evidence that was going to be considered in that proceeding.
Judge: So what role does (c)(1) play in your analysis?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So we understand 1512(c) to split up the world of obstructive conduct of an official proceeding into the (c)(1) offense and into (c)(2). (c)(1) covers everything it enumerates. It's the acts of altering, concealing, destroying records, documents, or other objects. And then (c)(2) would only pick up conduct that obstructs an official proceeding in a different way. So there's no duplication or superfluity on our reading. Instead, Congress was taking this universe and dividing it up into the two separate offenses. And I think that's actually a virtue of our reading as compared to Petitioner's because I have not heard him articulate anything that would fall within (c)(1) that wouldn't also come within (c)(2). So, on his reading, (c)(2) really does just swallow (c)(1) whole.
Judge: Well, I mean, in the way you're reading it, (c)(1) -- (c)(2) almost exists in isolation, certainly not affected by (c)(1).
Defendant: 's Lawyer: We don't deny at all that there is a relationship between the two provisions, Justice Thomas, but it's --
Judge: What is that relationship?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: And the relationship is the one Congress specified in the text. It's what follows the word "otherwise." That is the relevant degree of similarity. What both (c)(1) and (c)(2) have in common is that they -- they aim at conduct that obstructs an official proceeding. (c)(1) does so in one way, tampering with records and documents; (c)(2) does so with respect to all other conduct that in a different manner does that. And I think that this has to be the road the Court goes down to look at what Congress actually prescribed with respect to similarity because, in contrast, if you take up Petitioner's invitation to come up with some atextual gloss from (c)(1) to port over into (c)(2), I don't understand what the Court could look at to guide its determination of exactly what the relevant similarity would be.
Judge: General, I'm sure you've had a chance to read our opinion released Friday in the Bissonnette case. It was unanimous. It was very short.
Judge: But it explained how to apply the doctrine of ejusdem generis, and it -- what it said is you had specific terms, a more general catchall, if you will, term at the end, and it said that the general phrase is controlled and defined by reference to the terms that precede it. The "otherwise" phrase is more general, and the terms that precede it are "alters, destroys, mutilates, or conceals a record and document." And applying the doctrine as was set forth in that opinion, the specific terms "alters, destroy, and mutilate" carry forward into (2), and the terms "record, document, or other object" carry -- carry forward into (2) as well. And it seems to me that they, as I said, sort of control and define the -- the more general term.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So, Mr. Chief Justice, I think that the statute --
Judge: And I'm sorry. Just to interrupt --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Oh, yes.
Judge: --so I could put out exactly what -- and -- and the "otherwise" means in other ways. It alters, destroys, and mutilates record, document, or other objects that impede the investigation and otherwise, in other ways, accomplishes the same result.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So I think the problem with that approach with respect to 1512 is that it doesn't look like the typical kind of statutory phrase that consists of a parallel list of nouns or a parallel list of verbs where the Court has applied ejusdem generis or the noscitur canon. You know, these are separate prohibitions that have their own complex, non-parallel internal structure. And I think, actually, the best evidence that it's hard to figure out how you would divine a degree of similarity between them just based on the word "otherwise" is that there are multiple competing interpretations at issue in this case. You know, Justice Alito touched on them, and they're reflected in the competing interpretations between Judge Katsas on the D.C. Circuit and Judge Nichols on the district court.
Judge: Competing interpretations of what, which phrase?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So --and it relates to exactly the -- the question you asked me, which is that Judge Nichols thought that (c)(1) should limit (c)(2), and he looked at it and said, well, the relevant thing about (c)(1) is it deals with records, documents, and other objects, and so that means (c)(2) should be limited only to other acts that impair physical evidence. Meanwhile, Judge Katsas looked at the specific intent requirement in (c)(1), to take action that impairs the availability or use of the evidence, and he divined a broader gloss to put on (c)(2) and said --
Judge: Well, but that's simply saying --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: -- it should be other impairment of all other evidence.
Judge: Well, they're just applying the same doctrine to different aspects of it. And I think you do that as -- as well. What are the common elements? Alters, destroy, and mutilates a record or document. You have the first few, what you're doing, and what you're doing it to. And you -- and you apply both of those in -- as it said in Bissonnette, controlling and defining the term that follows so that it should involve something that's capable of alteration, destruction, and mutilation and with respect to a record or a document. That -- that's how you -- that's why --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So I actually don't even understand --
Judge: -- when you -- when you apply that doctrine, again, as we did on Friday, it -- it responds to some of the concerns that have been raised about how broad (c)(2) is. You can't just tack it on and say look at it as if it's standing alone because it's not.

Defendant: Yes, your honor, and uh, I mean honestly, being a bartender, you know, my income is not fixed so you know, I-I’m always saving for a rainy day. That's-that's why I-I don't want to part with a lot of my money, you know, my savings. I want to keep some of that money for-for the future.
Judge: And your children's future?
Defendant: Uh, well, yes. Yeah, that too, right.
Judge: So that means you shouldn't have a problem paying the additional amount based on your income.
Defendant: Well, what if-what if I lose my job, you know? I mean, yeah, I've got three kids.

Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Respondent's No Fly List claims are moot. He's not on the list. He hasn't been on the list in eight years. And he won't be put back on the list in the future based on the currently available information.
Judge: Well, the Respondent was also concerned about the propriety of the procedures that were used to put him on the list to begin with and challenged that. So how does the declaration that you all used, how does that change the procedures that he thought were violative of his rights?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So two responses. As far as a procedural challenge goes, number one, for the post-deprivation procedures, those have actually already changed since the time he was first on the list. I take the point that maybe he wants some pre-deprivation procedures. Those haven't changed. But I think a prior case pretty squarely forecloses that challenge.
Judge: But how would we know, for example, and how would he know that you have not -- that any circumstances or any procedures are different if he's put on the list in the future when we don't know the procedures or the circumstances that got him on the list?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yeah. So -- so that's the key. He does know why he's on the list, and we've -- we've made this public, which is the standard to be on the No Fly List, in addition to reasonable suspicion that you're a known or suspected terrorist, is also you must pose a threat of conducting or engaging in one of four enumerated acts of terrorism, international terrorism or domestic terrorism or a violent act of terrorism.
Judge: But doesn't that depend on him knowing what might put him back on? Because, if you're assessing whether it is reasonably likely to recur and you don't know what it is that could put you back on, it's a little hard to even make the assessment.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I want to push back on the fact that he needs to know what it is. Even if his claim is that our placement of him at time one was unlawful for whatever reason, procedural or substantive, the question for mootness purposes is whether he could be expected to be placed back on the list at a future time.
Judge: But what -- let me just stop you there. The way this works is not just doing something illegal. It's an email. It's a meeting with someone. It's something that only in combination with all the other "connect the dots" post-9/11 concerns looks more suspicious and gets you on the list.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: You're exactly right. It is always based on a totality of information. It is never -- you know, it's almost never going to be one single thing. And categorically, we have filed declarations in this and certainly in other cases saying it's never on the basis of First Amendment protected activity.
Judge: Are you saying that's unlikely?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: It's a very rare thing. There are very, very, very few U.S. persons on the No Fly List who satisfy that criteria. It's exceedingly rare. And I take the point that he satisfied it in the past and we continue to believe he satisfied it in the past.
Judge: Right. I mean, he was in a prison in UAE for months, right? I mean, that's not a usual circumstance of a U.S. person.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: And for Article III purposes, I think this Court has always been reluctant to rely on past instances in order to --
Judge: But this is not sort of a general Article III issue. Was that a mootness case?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: It was a standing case.
Judge: All right. So I understand here that what we're really talking about is the extent to which the government can rely on voluntary cessation to claim that he no longer has a claim.

Judge: This is the Defendant, Brian Failen. He says he needed a pool cover for his pool because he didn't want his kids falling in and drowning if he took his eye off them for a minute. He had discussions with the Plaintiff about a pool cover but never made a deal with him to get one. He has no idea why the Plaintiff went off and ordered him a custom cover because he never signed off on anything. Besides, he doesn't even need a custom cover because his pool is just a standard pool, and a store-bought cover would have fit.
Judge: He's accused of pooling around. All parties, please raise your right hand.
Judge: Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket: The Plaintiff ordered a custom pool cover for his friend, and the Defendant just up and canceled the deal and says he's not going to eat it. But the Defendant says he never pulled the trigger on the deal. It's the case of the chlorinated cover-up.
Judge: Thank you, Douglas.
Judge: Darren Cook, you are suing Brian Failen for $1,396.41 that you say he owes you for a pool cover that he ordered from you and now refuses to pay. What's going on?
Plaintiff: Back at the beginning of August of last year, 2011, he called me to see if I could call and give him a price on a pool cover.
Judge: How do you two know each other?
Plaintiff: His wife, I believe, works for a good friend of mine. So actually, in the beginning of 2011, I went over to his house and helped him get the pool clear because it was dirty.
Judge: Helped him get it clear because he called you to hire you to do that originally?
Plaintiff: Yeah, they called to see if I could give them some help. So I did that for nothing. I just went over to help him. It took about 15-20 minutes.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: Got the pool clear for him. Then in August, he called. He wanted a safety cover for his pool, which has the springs on it so kids can walk on it, in case anything happens. A safety cover. I measured the pool. I called him up. I went back to the house, measured it again. I gave him a quote on 3x3 spacing, which is narrower straps. But his pool, I told him he saves a couple hundred if he makes it 5x5 spacing.
Judge: What's 5x5 spacing?
Plaintiff: Between the straps on the safety cover. They put them every 3 feet or every 5 feet, depending on which cover you get. If he went 5x5, it would be cheaper. So he said okay, get me one. I gave him the price of the cover. He said, "Go ahead and order."
Judge: What was the price of the cover?
Plaintiff: The price was $1,748.
Judge: What was so custom about this pool? Did it have an odd shape?
Plaintiff: No, it’s just a rectangle with the steps coming off the shallow end.
Judge: So the custom pool cover is just a rectangle?
Plaintiff: Yeah, they're all custom because the steps aren't exactly most steps. Like, say, 8 feet. His was 3 feet by 4 feet, which is an off-size step.
Judge: But what does the cover have to do with the steps? Aren’t you covering the whole top?
Plaintiff: No, you can’t. It’s got to be cut into a rectangle with an extra square coming off for the stairs because that’s how his pool is shaped.
Judge: You ran that by him verbally? Nothing in writing?
Plaintiff: I went to his house twice and talked to him.
Judge: But you didn’t have him sign a proposal?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: So he tells you, "Sure, go ahead."
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And then where do you go to have this made?
Plaintiff: I go to a wholesaler, and they send it out to a company that makes them.
Judge: Okay. When was the first inkling you got that there was going to be a problem?
Plaintiff: September 15th. He called and spoke to my wife. He wanted to know when the cover was coming in.
Judge: Okay. Tell me about the phone call.
Plaintiff: ’s Wife: He asked when the cover would come in. It had not come in yet, so I told him that it should be in shortly, and as soon as it arrived, I would give him a call.
Plaintiff: Two days later, he calls back and says he doesn’t want it anymore.
Judge: What did he say?
Plaintiff: He left a message saying, "I don’t need that cover anymore. Somebody gave me one."
Judge: What’s going on, Mr. Failen?
Defendant: I met him in June through a friend. I called him a few times in July about a pool cover but didn't hear from him until the end of August. When I finally spoke to him, he said he could come over and measure the pool. I told him, "Okay."
Judge: And then?
Defendant: He calls me a week later, tells me about the price difference. I said, "Okay, I’ll take the one with the warranty."
Judge: So you agreed to it?
Defendant: Yes, but I kept looking around because I wasn’t sure if he’d follow through.
Judge: But you told him to go ahead?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Then you found a cheaper one?
Defendant: Yes, so I called him and left a message saying I didn’t need it anymore.
Judge: That’s not how custom orders work. You don’t get to just cancel them when you find a better deal. I find in favor of the Plaintiff for $1,396.41.
Judge: The pool cover belongs to him. Make arrangements with Officer McIntosh to transfer it over. You can't just bail out of a custom order because you found it cheaper. The time to find a cheaper option is before placing the order. Good luck.

Judge: : Katherine Pavnick, you are suing Christopher Aras for $1,168.32 in cell phone bills that he incurred in your name and has refused to pay. Oh, $250 of that is for time and aggravation and $290 of it is the early termination fee. What happened?
Plaintiff: In about January of 2012 he was on a prepaid account with Verizon and he asked me if he can come onto my account with Verizon.
Judge: : How come?
Plaintiff: He was sick of his prepaid, he said he wanted...
Judge: : What kind of people go on a prepaid who are grown? How old are you?
Judge: : Grown men go on a prepaid... you can't think of any answers to that?
Judge: : Think harder. If you have to be on a prepaid, what does that mean?
Plaintiff: He can't afford it.
Judge: : Right, either he can't afford it or he won't afford it. He's got bad credit. How do you get bad credit?
Plaintiff: By not paying your bills.

Defendant: I was paying um, some mostly cash at the store.

Defendant: Verizon Wireless.
Judge: : You were physically going to the store?
Judge: : So if you paid then you'd have a receipt to show that you paid?

Judge: : And where were the bills going to? A guy tells you that he's not going to make you his girlfriend so you're basically a booty call, alright, and then you put a phone in your name for him? Why would you do that? Why do you have such a poor self-image? How old are you?
Plaintiff: I'm 24 now.
Judge: : Okay, so why would you have such a poor self-image that you would do that? You're a perfectly lovely young lady, why would you do that?
Plaintiff: I'll be honest, I was stupid.
Judge: : Was he that charming?
Plaintiff: Yeah, at the time you really liked them.
Judge: : Do you understand that when a woman who likes you more than you like her gets a phone in your name it's to track you?
Defendant: No seriously, I'm very... I'm a lot more technical than that. I don't get tracked.
Judge: : You don't get tracked?
Judge: : You found out he was sleeping with other women?
Plaintiff: Mhm, yeah.
Judge: : How'd you find that out?
Plaintiff: I stumbled across a second Facebook of his.
Judge: : Oh no, she's not a tracker at all. So how did you stumble upon... he's... she's a tracker and you're a two-timer with experience because he has... you give one of them one Facebook and then... Oh tell me how you do it, inquiring minds all around America want to know. How many Facebooks do you have?
Defendant: Depends on how many girls you have... one.
Judge: : One Facebook? Okay, but you say you stumbled upon a second one?
Judge: : And so tell me about the second one.
Plaintiff: It had his information and it had a girl you know posting him back and forth with the other girl "Happy Valentine's Day I love you."
Judge: : Well I don't know to sort of... the fact that he wouldn't call you his girlfriend or spends you know all of his time with you was pretty much evidence that you didn't have a claim to him. I don't know that he lied to you in any regard. I think it's kind of you being a chump, don't you think?
Judge: : Going inside the courtroom, there is no question that you were supposed to pay this because you say "oh I did." That's your defense, not "oh she was... that was a gift" but "oh I did pay some of those" and then when I ask you "okay prove that you paid some of those" you're unable to prove it.
Defendant: : Well I look at it as I like... say I go food shopping, um I get a receipt from the store. Who really keeps those?
Judge: : I do.
Defendant: : I don't.
Judge: : Okay, I don't keep them for food shopping but I keep them if I'm going to owe somebody money.
Defendant: : I didn't think it was going to be a problem here.
Judge: : Okay, well then just prove that you paid them some other way. All you have to do is bring me proof that you paid something.
Defendant: : Well it's kind of hard to prove when you pay cash.
Judge: : No it's not, it's just hard for you because you didn't. And I don't find you that charming so I'm not gonna say "oh that's okay" like she did. That's not what's gonna happen. You have some video you want to show me? Because that's usually happens when somebody has video. Or you do?
Judge: : Who has... you do have video and what is the video of?
Plaintiff: The video is him verbally agreeing to pay me back the money.
Judge: : What did you... when did you guys stop seeing each other?
Plaintiff: In February of 2012.
Judge: : Okay. The video happened about mid-February. My question is why is there still a March bill and an April bill and a May bill and a June bill and a July bill? Like why would there still be bills with Verizon?
Judge: : Why would you not have canceled the phone the day after you stopped seeing each other?
Plaintiff: I didn't have the money.
Judge: : Money to... you didn't have the $290?
Judge: : So instead... yeah but you figured out how to sue for... I mean seriously you couldn't buy... who's that friend? She couldn't loan her $290 to stop the bleeding? Who are you? Come on up, who are you?
[Witness]: I'm just a witness that saw the tape.
Judge: : Oh let's see the tape.
[Video Audio]: You need to... you owe my friend a lot of money for helping you out. My uncle's a cop. I will get you arrested if you don't pay her back for what she done for you. I'm telling me that it's mutual... mutual agreement right?
[Voice on Video]: Proof right here, mutual agreement Chris?
[Defendant on Video]: Yes, mutual agreement.
[Voice on Video]: You will pay my friend back everything?
Judge: : Okay, you can go inside now. They can stop your video after that.
[Unknown Voice]: I love you!
Judge: : Oh that wasn't me.
[Unknown Voice]: Oh who did that?
Judge: : I love whoever did that. That's a friend. Where were you when that got taped?
Plaintiff: In front of my house.
Judge: : So why are you such a jerk? Why don't you pay her?
Defendant: : Well I was... I'd be willing to pay her what I owe her what I gave you the copy of...
Judge: : What do you mean you gave me the copy?
Defendant: : Of this is one page of a four page...
Judge: : Where's the rest of it?
Defendant: : It was just in detail of...
Judge: : Yeah I want to see the in detail. Give me the four pages.
Defendant: : But this is what you... let me try to correlate to what she has and see if...
Judge: : Okay the very last bill that you get says that the amount is $866.74 right? So why are you suing for $1,168.32?
Plaintiff: Um, part of that was the early termination fee...
Judge: : No but that... the very last bill that you get has the early termination fee on it doesn't it? Or no?
Plaintiff: Yes it does.
Judge: : So if the very last bill you got is $866.74, why are you suing for $1,168.32?
Plaintiff: Time and aggravation, stress.
Judge: : You know what? Sometimes life lessons are a little bit expensive. You should have learned a long time ago to value yourself a little bit more than you were valuing yourself here. You did that for one of two reasons - one is pathetic and the other one is kind of slimy. Either you just want to buy his love which never works, or you want to keep track on him which is kind of impressive but still slimy. Those are the only two reasons why women get men phones and it always astounds me that men don't understand that. They think they're pulling one over on you and on half those women they're not just pulling one over - the women are smarter than you think. Okay? But and they come and cry to me later. Alright? You are one of the criers.
[Judge continues]: I would... what I would like to do is have a bright line new rule if I was Queen that if you are so... if it was... if you were my child I'd say suck it up so that it never happens again but I cannot do that because my hands are tied. I've got to sit here and right the wrong that... to me you'd learn more if I just let it go and if you were my daughter that's what I'd do. I'd want to see you working overtime to pay that bill because I want to know that never again in your life will you be a py... unfortunately I'm the judge on your case and not your mother so when you're the judge on the case you got to actually figure out what's owed and what's owed even considering all the adjustments which were most of what were paid in the year that you had that... almost a year that you had that phone uh leaves us with a balance of $866.74 that you owe what you're going to pay is every penny that she's out which is $866.74 plus your filing fee that is my judgment. You don't get time and aggravation consider that your lesson.
Manipulation Techniques: gaslighting, deflection, emotional appeal, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge: So is there any distinction between your position and the Solicitor General's position, and, if so, can you just zero in on it?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: There's no difference in this case.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: There's no difference as to what the rule that you should apply in this case is, which is any offense-related conduct beyond the elements of the crime is subject to Apprendi and not Almendarez-Torres. The only differences that I can discern in the briefing between our position and the Solicitor General's are a few borderline in-between questions about how you apply that test to particular facts.
So there's the offense— the date of the offense, I think, is something the Solicitor General suggests might be within the prior conviction exception.
We don't think it is because the date of the offense is not an element of the crime. It's not something a prior jury would have had to find.
Judge: Does that suggest that the future work of this Court and other courts is going to be to have to identify which facts go to the judge and which go to the jury? I mean, are we at that level? It seems at least the other side has a sort of simpler conception of this, which is recidivism— put it in the bucket of Almendarez-Torres.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, it might be simpler to say any fact about a prior conviction— using a gun, vulnerable victim, whatever you could imagine— would be called within recidivism.
I just think that's so at odds with the Court's Apprendi jurisprudence that that option is just not on the table as a matter of stare decisis.
Judge: Well, let me ask you another question about that option—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Yeah.
Judge: —which is, it seems very complex. This is going back to a previous line of questions.
I totally understand your point, I understand the precedents, but we do have this Almendarez-Torres carveout, and part of this case is understanding its scope and whether or not this kind of thing does fit in it as a matter of precedent or should fit in it, given all of the various ways in which this could go.
And one concern I have is that I think when we're talking about two different sets of facts with respect to the jury, there is added complexity.
What I mean by that is we have the facts that relate to the charged crime— today's charged crime, in this case, it's the 924(g)— but we also have facts that relate to past crimes that this defendant was convicted of committing.
And I guess I'm just trying to understand how today's jury adjudicates past crime facts.
So are they limited to the record that was presented to the original jury on those facts? Can new evidence come in related to crimes that happened 20 years ago as we try to figure out whether they happened on a single occasion, or how does this work?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: So, remember, if you bifurcate, the jury's not doing the two things at the same time.
They're doing the 922(g).
Judge: Yes.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: And then they’re having a separate proceeding.
Judge: Right.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: In that separate proceeding, I do think other evidence could come in beyond the record that was established in the initial conviction, because the way I think Congress drafted this— “was committed on separate occasions” is an open-ended fact-finding.
Judge: So how do we keep this from being just like mini retrials of the whole— are you saying we have to have the evidence with respect—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, remember—
Judge: Yeah.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: You're having that inquiry regardless. It's just whether or not the judge or the jury is going to make the finding.

Judge: All right.
So here's the question here.
We -- the question, I guess, in response to that answer is: Why wouldn't it be reasonable for a jury to interpret the submission of the invoice to be the kind of specific question that would -- that would require him to provide an answer? I mean, we don't have a particular question.
We have his interpretation of the question and then answering it in a certain way, which you say doesn't make it false.
But, in the context of what a reasonable jury could have determined, I don't understand why -- what your argument is to why a jury couldn't have interpreted what happened here to be calling for a specific response to the question how much do you owe.
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, I think it's -- it -- it -- it's difficult to conceive of an assertion in an invoice as being a specific -- specific numerical --
Judge: Difficult, but impossible? The question is: Could a reasonable jury have interpreted it that way?
Lawyer of Defendant: I -- I don't think so, Your Honor, and the reason is, among others, that there wasn't a -- there wasn't a question posed at all.
The witnesses testified that it had -- that he was -- or the evidence showed that he was talking about his personal note, not the total amount that he owed.
And the government conceded that what he said was, and I quote, "literally true" --
Judge: All right.
Lawyer of Defendant: -- "but not the whole story."
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
Plaintiff: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Section 1014 prohibits any false statement.
And like any other collection of words, a statement is false if it conveys an untrue message to the listener in context, even if the precise words used, considered in a vacuum, could possibly carry another meaning.
So, here, when, in response to receiving an invoice telling Defendant that he owed the FDIC $269,000, Defendant then told the FDIC's agents that he was shocked by the letter, had no idea where the 269 number comes from, and had borrowed $110,000, he made a false statement because he clearly conveyed the message that he did not owe the higher amount.
And 12 members of the jury in this case, who were not given a specialized definition of what "false" means and, therefore, must have applied the concept as ordinarily understood, agreed.
Indeed, on page 7 of his reply brief, Defendant himself agrees that context obviously matters in determining whether a statement is false.
I understood my friend to reiterate that position again today.
So now it appears we're just debating what context the jury may consider as a matter of law.
And to the extent Defendant is arguing that you can only take account of the immediately preceding question, we urge the Court to reject any such rule.
The jury should assess a speaker's meaning the same way the original listener would have: in light of other parts of the conversation and other circumstances that naturally bear on meaning.
Our position in this case is not that "false" encompasses anything that might be characterized as misleading or any failure to disclose pertinent information.
It is that a statement is untrue if it states only a portion of the truth on the subject it addresses in a context where the statement would be taken as both accurate and complete.
If, like Defendant, the speaker knowingly conveys that untrue message and does it with a specific intent to influence the FDIC to not fully collect on its debt, that violates the statute. I welcome the Court's questions.
Judge: So it doesn't really matter in this case whether there is a difference between "false" and "misleading?"
Plaintiff: Our position in this case is that Defendant's statements were false.
That's how the jury was instructed. Our position is just that you assess the falsity of something, you know, the inaccuracy of it, by looking to context and what -- whether a false message was imparted.
There's been a lot of talk, though, today about what kind of rule the Seventh Circuit applied in this case, and I -- I think the crux of the Seventh Circuit's analysis completely aligns with what I just said.
I think you can see this in particular at pages 10a and 13a of the petition appendix.
On 10a, the Court said: Even if he never used the precise words, the implication of Defendant's statements was that he owed Washington Federal no more than $110,000, something that was untrue.
And then, on 13a, the court talks about the mistake -- unmistakable impression left by his statements and how the jury found in its verdict that he conveyed the message that he falsely stated that he only owed $110,000, and any higher amount was incorrect.
Judge: So -- but, in -- in general, do you think there's any difference between the statutes that say "false statement" and the statutes that say "false and misleading?" Because it sounds to me that your -- would argue that when it says "false," that includes misleading statements in context.
So is there any difference?
Plaintiff: We are not taking the position that the word "misleading" does no work in statutes in which it appears.
We are -- we think there is some overlap between these concepts, as I understood my friend to agree.
But, when we say something has to be false in context, we mean it has to state -- the statement itself has to state a -- a false message, it has to convey a false meaning directly, not lead the listener down a path perhaps to a foreseeable conclusion that additional information might have obviated.
But, here, when Defendant says "I borrowed $110,000" in response to what was essentially a question from the FDIC saying, did you -- do you owe $269,000, that is directly conveying through his statement that he only owed that amount.

Judge: I will say that the courts of appeals after Badgerow have split on the question as to whether, if the -- if a case remains open, there is a basis to vacate an award.
With the Seventh Circuit and the Third Circuit saying you do not need an independent basis and the Fourth Circuit coming out the other way, that may be the next case.
Plaintiff: Lawyer:
Okay.
But I guess regardless, is what I'm saying, even if I disagree with you or, you know, however hard or easy that question might be, your real contention here is that this is a different jurisdictional question and that the Badgerow question can be decided at some place down the road.
Defendant: Lawyer:
That's exactly right.
And in terms of -- of the question presented, it -- the text is clear and the California courts are clear that proceedings and judgments are to be -- proceedings are to be interpreted broadly. Judgments at the time the rules were enacted included voluntary dismissals.
The California Supreme Court interpreted the same words to include voluntary dismissals, rejected the argument that voluntary dismissals could not -- could not be reopened because it was voluntary.
The -- the point is --
Judge: Mr. Levy, on -- on that, your reading hinges on "proceeding," places a lot of weight there.
I wonder, though, what wouldn't be a proceeding on your theory? I know what an order is, I know what a judgment is.
Defendant: Lawyer:
Right.
Judge: But is a proceeding every docket entry? And if it is, then why do we have order and judgment? Have we created a -- a problem where they're rendered meaningless?
Defendant: Lawyer:
Well, I think the -- the way the statute was written was to go from narrower to broader because every judgment --
Judge: Without doubt, yeah.
Defendant: Lawyer:
-- every judgment is also an order, and the way the courts interpreted "proceeding" would -- would include orders and -- and judgments --
Judge: Yeah, does it swallow the whole thing, though, and does that create a problem? And what wouldn't be a proceeding?
Defendant: Lawyer:
Well, I think it's a belts-and-suspenders approach to making sure that there was broad authority to --
Judge: So, yes, it does swallow it, but we should overlook that?
Defendant: Lawyer:
Well, it's a virtue, so I don't know that I would --
Judge: It's a virtue, not a vice.
Okay.
All right.
All right.
Got it.
I got it, I think.
And then Microsoft, how do you deal with that, where this Court said that a dismissal with prejudice wasn't a final decision for purposes of 1291?
Defendant: Lawyer:
Right.
So I think this goes to an argument my friends make, which is to try to import the concept of finality from appeals to --
Judge: 60(b).
Defendant: Lawyer:
-- 60(b).
And I think a couple of points in response, and we make them in our brief.
The first one is that the -- the -- the rule-makers used different words.
They didn't use final decisions, final decisions subject to appeal. They made very clear initially that it covered whatever California covered, which is everything. And then the word "final" was added later to make clear that the courts' authority over interlocutory matters was not constrained.
So that history and the way the -- where the words come from impart a very different meaning.
So that's number one.
Number two --
Judge: Number one's good enough for me.
Defendant: Lawyer:
Okay.
Judge: Judge: Matheson's dissent and his boomerang theory of finality --
Defendant: Lawyer:
Yeah.
Judge: -- which you seem to embrace, so I -- I -- I don't mean it pejoratively, but that things can become final, you didn't make that argument here.
You're asking us, as I understand it, to hold that there was always a final proceeding.
It didn't -- it didn't mature into finality due to later events.
Is that right?
Defendant: Lawyer:
So ours wins under both standards.
And we took -- we -- we -- we endorsed, I think, something closer to Yesh from the Fifth Circuit --
Judge: Yeah.
Defendant: Lawyer:
-- which looks at finality when made.
But we would win under Judge Matheson --
Judge: You would take it if you had to.
Got it.
Okay.
Defendant: Lawyer:
And -- and, as I mentioned, if finality turns on the limitation period, in this case, when the dismissal was initially made, the -- the limitation period's already applied.
So even that standard would be met.
Judge: Thank you, Mr. Levy.
Plaintiff: Lawyer:
Can you walk us through the enactment history and, in particular, the -- the significance of the addition of "final"?
Defendant: Lawyer:
So, initially, as I said, the -- in 1937, the statute -- the -- the rule, I'm sorry, covered judgments, orders, or proceedings.
The advisory committee notes made clear that's taken from the California decisions, and Professor Moore has said in various places at the time that the California decisions are authoritative.
Between 1937 and 1946, some courts were reading Rule 60(b) to apply to interlocutory matters so that if a -- if a -- a decision were made or an order taken along the way, that --
Plaintiff: Lawyer:
In an open, pending case?
Defendant: Lawyer:
In an open, pending case. That the movant would have to show a mistake, fraud, or the like under Rule 60(b), and, otherwise, the court would be without authority.
And Professor Moore, in -- in the article that is referenced in the advisory committee notes, says that's wrong because, as this Court had said in 1922 in the John Simmons Company case, courts retain plenary inherent authority to revisit interlocutory matters and that's how it should be.
And so, for that reason, Professor Moore recommended, and that was ultimately taken up, that the word "final" be added to Rule 60(b) not to limit Rule 60(b) but, rather, to make sure that inherent -- that interlocutory matters would always be subject to reopening, unconstrained by Rule 60(b).

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff cashed a money order for her godsister, and it was bogus. Now she’s stuck. But the defendant says she had no idea the money orders were bogus. It’s the case of money not made to order.
Judge: Thank you. Chantel Brown, you are suing Katia. You’ve asked us not to mention your last name for $2,412.
Plaintiff: Yes, Your Honor.
Judge: $1,935 of it for money orders that she had you deposit in your account that turned out to be fraudulent, $400 for pain and suffering, $100 for some loan that has nothing to do with this, and bank fees. All right, tell me what’s going on.
Plaintiff: Well, I’ve known Katia for most of my life, since I was about 11 or 12 years old. Her son is my godbrother, so I consider her family.
Judge: Her son is your godbrother? What does that mean?
Plaintiff: It means that my parents are his godparents.
Judge: Okay. Continue.
Plaintiff: She called me at the end of February, I believe, about a money order. She said she was having problems cashing it because of her ID or something. She called me distressed. She was worried about paying bills and helping her mom out, things like that. So, as a loving friend and family member, I helped her out. She asked if I could lend her the money and then deposit the money order in my account, which I did.
Judge: How much did you give her?
Plaintiff: $1,900.
Judge: That was the amount of the money order?
Plaintiff: Yes, Your Honor. To me, that was the same as cash. I didn’t think it was fraudulent. I didn’t think it was anything shady.
Judge: Who were the money orders from? What kind of money orders were they?
Plaintiff: I’m not sure exactly. She said it was from a company that she worked for.
Judge: But you didn’t photocopy them before you deposited them into your account?
Plaintiff: No, Your Honor.
Judge: Did the bank send you a copy when they reversed it?
Plaintiff: Yes, I have it here.
Judge: May I see that?
Plaintiff: Sure.
Judge: Where did these money orders come from?
Defendant: I’m a mystery shopper.
Judge: You’re a what?
Defendant: A mystery shopper.
Judge: What is a mystery shopper?
Defendant: You’re paid to go into certain businesses and critique them—customer service, cleanliness, how long it took them to wait on you, things like that.
Judge: That sounds interesting. How much do you get paid for that?
Defendant: It depends. In this case, I did about four right around the holiday season of 2011.
Judge: You did about four what?
Defendant: Four different stores I visited as a mystery shopper.
Judge: And they were going to pay you how much for that?
Defendant: $1,900.
Judge: To go to the four stores and do your Christmas shopping?
Defendant: Well, it was divided among the four stores. I got my payment for being a mystery shopper, and they sent money for me to purchase everyday items.
Judge: Did they do that?
Defendant: Yes. That’s why when they gave me the money orders, I didn’t suspect anything. I had been with them for two years and never had a problem before. But when Chantel called to tell me that the checks were fraudulent, I called the company. I only got voicemail. No return calls. No letter. Nothing.
Judge: And then what happened?
Defendant: A month later, they told me the money orders were not fraudulent, but then they went out of business in California.
Judge: So, you were left hanging.
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: But how are you both victims when she’s out $1,900 and you’re out nothing?
Defendant: Because I depended on this company. I trusted them.
Judge: But what are you out?
Defendant: My time and work during the holiday season.
Judge: That’s not the same. You didn’t lose money. She did. Did you pay her anything?
Defendant: Yes. I gave her $600.
Plaintiff: That’s not true.
Judge: Did she receive anything?
Plaintiff: Nothing. She called me the day after and asked for $100 to help her mom out. I talked to my husband about it.
Judge: Husband, what do you know about this?
Plaintiff: ’s Husband: I know my wife asked me for $100 to help her godsister. She mentioned the $1,900 money order.
Judge: Did she mention getting $600?
Plaintiff: ’s Husband: No, she did not.
Judge: Did you get documentation for the $100?
Plaintiff: No, Your Honor.
Judge: Show me the Facebook messages.
Plaintiff: She tried to claim she only owed me $1,000. She even admitted she could start paying me back every two weeks.
Judge: Did she ever say she paid you $600?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: Do you have proof of paying her $600?
Defendant: No.
Judge: That’s what I thought. If she cashed your money order and it turned out to be bad, that’s your responsibility. You owe her the full $1,935 plus bank fees. I rule in favor of the plaintiff.

Defendant: Well... it was a one-night stand. I haven't... you know, I haven't heard from her in months. You know, and then she comes telling me that, you know, she's pregnant and she's having a baby. Then she says she wanted to be my baby and all that type of stuff. I told her, 'Look, I love my wife, I love my family, my kids. I just want to move on. Get this thing behind me.'
Judge: So... Let me be clear, you say this only happened, this one night?
Defendant: Just one night.
Judge: You never saw her again?
Defendant: Never saw her again.
Judge: That's wrong, Your Honor. That's wrong.
Defendant: Well... we did sleep around a couple of times.
Judge: So, it was more than one night?
Defendant: Yes. She's just looking for some money. She's looking for a cash-out.

Judge: How old is the child?
Plaintiff: Two years old.
Judge: Two years old, and you don’t visit with the baby?
Defendant: No, I mean, I have a night job, and so I sleep during the day, so it’s really hard to get up and see the child.
Judge: So because you have a night job, you cannot spend time with your baby? That’s what you’re saying today?
Defendant: Yes, Your Honor.

Judge: Thank you, Douglas. Christopher, how do I pronounce Raisin?
Plaintiff: Raisen.
Judge: You are suing Tariq McGee for $3,885. You say he owes you because you rented your car to him. Tell me about it.
Plaintiff: Basically, me and Tariq have been friends for some years. He was in a jam. I had two vehicles at the time, so I agreed to give him one of my vehicles if he agreed to pay the car note.
Judge: Okay, so you agreed to rent him your vehicle for how much a month?
Plaintiff: The car note, which was $400 a month.
Judge: $400 a month. And when did you agree to do that?
Plaintiff: I don’t remember the specific date and month, but it was sometime—
Judge: When did you start renting the car?
Plaintiff: I want to say April of 2016.
Defendant: Yes, that sounds about right.
Judge: So what is it you’re suing for?
Plaintiff: I just wanted the ticket payments because that’s what’s affecting me today.
Judge: Okay. How many tickets did you get on this car? First of all, you’re paying $400 a month, just for the car?
Plaintiff: I was paying $900, up to $900 a month.
Judge: How? How long were you paying that?
Plaintiff: That was for the summer of—
Judge: Wait, this is a 2010 Volkswagen Jetta?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And you’re paying $900 a month?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: How is this a good deal for you?
Plaintiff: Bad credit. I didn’t have— I have terrible credit.
Judge: Wow. Alright. So according to you, you paid $900 a month for how many months?
Plaintiff: Three.
Judge: And then what?
Plaintiff: The first boot— the car got three boots in total. The first boot was not my fault. The second two boots were. And I did get—
Judge: How was the first boot not your fault?
Plaintiff: Because they weren’t my tickets.
Judge: Did you pay off all those tickets?
Plaintiff: I paid them off.
Judge: Okay. And then what happens?
Plaintiff: The second boot, I paid off. I had some tickets. I didn’t pay them in time. It wasn’t as many. I paid them off. Third boot—
Judge: So the boot comes off only when you pay everything you owe, right?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: So the third boot that you pay off is when?
Plaintiff: I don’t exactly— it was in 2017.
Judge: Do you know when the boot was?
Defendant: I believe it was February.
Judge: So I know that at some point after that boot, any tickets that happen are going to be yours. And in fact, according to you, you had the car between what dates and what dates?
Defendant: April 2016 to when?
Plaintiff: To September 2017.
Defendant: Yeah, September 2017.
Judge: When the car got— was finally repossessed. Now, the car gets repossessed. And why is it repossessed?
Plaintiff: Because the car note wasn’t paid on time.
Judge: How many times had he not paid the car note on time?
Plaintiff: It was a total of three months.
Judge: And according to you, you were paying him on time and he just wasn’t giving the money to the bank?
Defendant: I didn’t know. When the car got repossessed, I didn’t know I was behind. I was led to believe that everything was okay. We only had to worry about the insurance.
Judge: I’m going to need you to prove to me that you were current. Can you do that?
Defendant: The last transaction we made was in person. I have nothing on paper.
Judge: According to you, the repo fee was $740. Did you get charged that?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am. I paid the tow company $740.
Judge: And do you have the car now?
Plaintiff: No, I voluntarily surrendered it to the bank. But I didn’t want the repo on my credit, so—
Judge: Okay. So you think he should pay the repo fee because—
Plaintiff: I have proof here. This is him admitting that he didn’t pay the bill and lost his job.
Judge: But I don’t understand why you’re allowing him to ruin your credit. Why are you allowing— how do you let it get months and months behind?
Plaintiff: I was monitoring them, but the thing was, he didn’t make all the payments directly to the bank. Some of them came to me.
Judge: No. All of them should come to you, and then you would know every single month whether he made the payment. Then you should be responsible for making sure that the payment gets to the bank because it’s your credit on the line. Do you understand?
Plaintiff: Absolutely.
[The case continues with the discussion of evidence and judgment, leading to a final ruling by the judge awarding $2,938 to the plaintiff.]
Judge: Pay the man.

Judge: We will hear argument this morning in Case 22-807, Alexander versus the South Carolina State Conference of the NAACP.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: District 1 is not a racial gerrymander. Rather, the General Assembly largely preserved District 1 from the constitutional benchmark plan and made changes based on traditional criteria and politics. The panel acknowledged that the General Assembly pursued a political goal of increasing District 1's Republican vote share. It achieved that goal by moving Republicans into the district and Democrats out of the district. All of the direct evidence confirms that it used political data, not racial data, to identify Republicans and Democrats. The panel declared District 1 a racial gerrymander only by departing from this Court's precedents and adopting sua sponte an erroneous racial target theory.
Judge: Mr. Gore, we review this for clear error. And the district court credited the plaintiffs' expert and found your experts non-credible. So how does that meet the clear error standard?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: The Court will proceed to clear error if it rejects our legal arguments, but let me turn to Dr. Ragusa first. All three of Dr. Ragusa's opinions raised in this appeal contradict his own data and conclusions in his initial report, which actually demonstrated that race did not predominate in the enacted plan's changes to District 1. His own data showed that politics was a stronger predictor than race of whether a VTD was moved out of District 1. He also concluded that there was no statistically significant correlation between race and whether VTDs were moved into District 1.
Judge: Well, I thought -- I thought he said that as far as geographic contiguity, that the -- the size of the different districts was an adequate proxy for that.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: He did say that traditional principles were embedded in his analysis, but whatever he meant by that, he did also admit on cross-examination that he didn't test or control for those principles and whether they explained the decisions the General Assembly actually made. That's the same error that the experts made in Allen that this Court set aside just last term. His failure to consider the location of VTDs and voters within the district is the same error that was committed by the expert in Cromartie II, where this Court reversed a finding of racial gerrymandering under the clear error standard.
Judge: Did your expert present an alternative study which did control for geography and reached a different result?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: He did not try to mirror Dr. Ragusa's study --
Judge: Because that would have been the easiest way to undermine the theory. I mean, as I understand it, this was hardly touched upon by -- by -- by the state below. And, certainly, the state did not do what would seem to be the normal thing if you were really concerned about this, which is to say: Look at our study. We controlled for geography. The results are entirely different.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: We did raise objections to Dr. Ragusa's methodology, and as I was explaining, it is a flawed methodology and not reliable. Moreover, the state presented direct testimony from the map drawer to explain which VTDs were chosen and why. That direct evidence showed, like all the other direct evidence, that decisions were made based on politics and traditional principles and not using race at all.
Judge: I think you end up in a very poor starting point under clear error arguing the substance of believability of one expert over another, because credibility findings under clear error standard must be deferred to to the district court. I understand your points about -- your point about Dr. Ragusa, but I just point out that other experts before the court and he himself said that geography was very much embedded as part of the structure of his analysis. You may disagree with that. It's going to be very hard for you to show that no fact finder could credit that understanding of his testimony. But I think what I'm really troubled by is, going back to Justice Thomas's question, what's the legal error and what's the clear error? Just tick them off for me.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: There are several legal errors, Justice Sotomayor.
Judge: Not facts. I want legal errors or clear errors beyond -- under our standard.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: The first legal error is a failure to enforce the alternative map requirement.
Judge: Okay. I'm going to butt in. And I'm sorry, Justice Sotomayor.
Judge: The alternative map requirement, I mean, doesn't exist. You know, sometimes this Court, I think, holds things, and then I go back to the opinion and I think: Well, maybe we weren't as clear as we might have been. Not here. I'm just going to read from -- from Cooper: A plaintiff's task is simply to persuade the trial court, without any special evidentiary prerequisite, that race, not politics, was the predominant consideration. In no area of our equal protection law have we forced plaintiffs to submit one particular form of proof to prevail, nor would it make sense to do so here. An alternative map is merely an evidentiary tool. Neither its presence nor its absence can itself resolve a racial gerrymandering claim. I don't know how to more clearly say that there is no alternative map requirement in these kinds of cases.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Cooper was directed -- was addressing a case where there was direct evidence of racial predominance. It also said on page 322 in the majority opinion: In a case like Cromartie II -- that is, one in which the plaintiffs had meager direct evidence of a racial gerrymander and needed to rely on evidence of forgone alternatives -- only maps of that kind could carry the day.
Judge: All we were saying there, Mr. Gore, is that in a case with no other evidence, you needed some evidence. So that is not this case. Cromartie II was making a very case-specific point, look at this case, there's none -- none of this kind of evidence, there's none of that kind of evidence, there's none of the other kind of evidence. So, my gosh, in that case, you needed a map. But this is case by case, all we were saying is that when you have no other evidence, you better present a map. But that's not to say that there's anything like an alternative map requirement. If you make your case some other way, that's good enough. And, here, the Court found, again, on a clear error standard, that the plaintiffs made their case some other way.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: But even if that's the correct reading of Cooper, Justice Kagan, there were still other legal errors in how the panel conducted its analysis.
Judge: Well, Mr. Gore --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: One --
Judge: -- I thought your argument was that at least as a practical matter, in a case in which there is no direct evidence or virtually no direct evidence, there is no way in which a plaintiff can disentangle race and politics, except by providing an alternative map. I thought that was your legal argument.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: That is.
Judge: And that's exactly what Cooper says is not the case.
Judge: Well, one may read Cooper a different way. Cooper was a case in which there was a lot of direct evidence, was there not?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: Well, let me ask you, how could there be direct evidence really in this kind of case? So this is what I'm a little concerned about because, to the extent that this distinction's turning on whether or not there is direct evidence, I wonder if it is reasonable to require such evidence or -- or say that such evidence would exist in a situation that is not a majority/majority -- a majority/minority district scenario. You can see how there would be direct evidence when the state's goal is to try to, in its view, comply with the VRA, they're trying to make a majority/minority district, so we're going to have some evidence of people saying that. But, in a situation like this, where that is not the case, where the state is saying instead, we are trying to, you know, achieve a partisan tilt, I guess I don't understand -- and, excuse me, we've also said that its, you know, intent to use race is a very hard thing to prove just on its own. Are you asking that we have the smoking gun in a situation like this?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Not at all, Justice Jackson. As you pointed out, of course, in majority/minority district contexts, there's often direct evidence of a use of race and even of race predominating. You could also have that in another context if the map-drawer or some key legislator admitted to using race as a proxy for politics because they didn't have adequate election data or --
Judge: But are you requiring that? Could we ever -- could we ever make this showing on circumstantial evidence alone? There were some amicus briefs related to computer drawings and that sort of thing that they thought would be particularly helpful in this context.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: The -- the alternative map itself would perform that requirement because, if race predominated over politics, then any alternative map can be drawn that preserves the political outcome the General Assembly was seeking while removing the alleged racial effect.
Judge: Putting that aside, there were alternative maps here that showed that if race wasn't used, the map would not look like this. So it didn't show what you're saying. But we go back, let's assume, let's move back past the map because I think Cooper was petulantly clear that you don't need a smoking gun, and if you don't need a smoking gun, you don't need direct evidence. What are the other legal errors?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Another legal error was the panel's misconstruction of the Sell -- Shelby County decision. It also failed to disentangle race and politics, as this Court has directed it to do. It ignored volumes of direct evidence on the politics-versus-race question. It didn't even --
Judge: It didn't --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: -- discuss that evidence --
Judge: -- it -- it --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: -- in its opinion.
Judge: -- it rejected -- the person who drew it was Mr. Roberts? Mr. -- and it disclaimed his credibility. So whatever the legislature said in terms of their intent is irrelevant. It's what he did, and the Court did not believe that he didn't use race. It said so.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: The Court did not accept his version of events but didn't make a credibility finding based on his demeanor or testimony at trial. It simply credited other evidence. But, in conducting the sensitive inquiry that Cooper requires, the Court was required to look at all the evidence, direct and circumstantial, of intent, and it simply didn't do that here. Senator Campsen testified --
Judge: That's the legal error, is that they didn't correctly weigh the evidence?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: They didn't correctly conduct the inquiry.
Judge: Because that sounds like a factual error to me. I mean, your brief basically, you know, says we have legal errors, and then it says, well, the evidence didn't show. Those are factual errors. That's subject to the clear error standard, going back to Justice Thomas's question.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hand. Welcome back to The People’s Court. The next case on the docket: the plaintiff says the defendant’s German Shepherd attacked her Viszla badly in a dog park. The defendant says it was just a scratch, so chill. It's the case of German attack. Thank you, Douglas.
Judge: Anna Chernovich, you are suing Roy Bamford, correct, for $2180.89 in vet bills that you say resulted from his dog. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: On December 23rd, at three o’clock in the afternoon, my daughter and I took the dog to the dog park.
Judge: What kind of dog do you have?
Plaintiff: It’s a Viszla. It’s a hunting dog.
Judge: What does that look like? Do you have a picture?
Plaintiff: Yes. You can just give her all of them. There’s a headshot somewhere in there.
Judge: So go on.
Plaintiff: We approached the orchard, which is a dog area where dogs are allowed off-leash. There were two dogs and two individuals present. My dog recognized one of the dogs and started to run over.
Judge: Is that the defendant’s dog?
Plaintiff: No. His dog, Bentley, approached. Our dog had played with Bentley maybe two or three times in the past.
Judge: And his dog played well with your dog before?
Plaintiff: Yes, so I was not concerned when I arrived at the dog park. There are usually a lot of dogs there. Bentley, however, approached my dog, growled, and my dog ran away. His dog proceeded to follow and within 10 seconds bit my dog. There was no fight. My dog did not cry out. I just noticed that out of the corner of my eye, something was wrong. He was running over to me, and he had this big gash on his side.
Judge: So you could see the gash?
Plaintiff: Oh yeah.
Judge: And your dog didn’t yelp or anything?
Plaintiff: I think it was out of shock. The skin was sort of like a triangular laceration and was sticking up. He started to just lick it immediately.
Judge: Do you have pictures of the wound?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: What did you do next?
Plaintiff: Right away, I was like, “Oh my God, this is a bad situation. We need to get him to the vet immediately.”
Judge: Well, like a thinking person would think that too, you know.
Plaintiff: Yes, so I went into Mommy Mode. Mr. Bamford and the other gentleman took a look at the dog, and I said, “Oh my God, I have to go right to the vet.” Mr. Bamford said, “It’s nothing but a tooth snag.”

Defendant: I was at the dog park for maybe 15-20 minutes. Her dog entered from the side, started to run with them, changed course, and they collided. I turned around and saw a white spot on the dog. The dog has no hair, so in the collision, the tooth must have gone in, but it wasn’t a bite.

Judge: Did her dog yelp?
Defendant: There was no growling or barking. They were running, playing like they always do, and all of a sudden, we heard a yelp. They both split up, and I saw the white mark. I walked over, and two gentlemen were there. When she left, I asked if she wanted my phone number.
Judge: Why would you offer your phone number if it was just a collision?
Defendant: Because I’m a dog person, and I felt obligated.
Judge: Okay, so what ended up happening with the dog?
Plaintiff: We took him to the vet. He had surgery that afternoon. They cleaned the wound, stitched it up, and put a drain in. The next morning, he was feverish. My daughter would sit with him, cuddle with him, and put blankets on him.
Judge: And then what happened?
Plaintiff: By December 26th, he had to go back. The vet suspected an infection. It turned out he contracted streptococcus in his wound.
Judge: What did they do then?
Plaintiff: They gave him a different dose of antibiotics.
Judge: And did he recover?
Plaintiff: He developed three sacs of fluids. He had to be brought back constantly. Eventually, he leaned against furniture, knocking over a speaker and breaking his toe.
Judge: And you’re suing him for that because…?
Plaintiff: Because if he hadn’t been in pain from the bite, he wouldn’t have leaned against the furniture.
Judge: And if you had secured the speaker, he also wouldn’t have had a broken foot.
Defendant: Exactly.
Judge: Mr. Bamford, did you ever offer to pay anything?
Defendant: Yes, I sent a check for $1,000. She refused it.
Judge: In your state, there’s a strict liability rule. If your dog causes damage, you’re responsible. However, the broken toe is too attenuated. I find in favor of the plaintiff for $1426.89. Case closed.

Judge: Thank you, Douglas. Ramon Chadar? Yes, and Kti Sinini. Cark Cark. Okay. You are suing Michelle and Ton Saunders, okay? You’re the mom? Yes. Alright, for $1,000 for stolen property because, according to you, he stole your scooter, your helmet, and caused you to pay some money in changing locks because your scooter keys were involved. Tell me what’s going on.
Plaintiff: Well, your honor, on June 8th, me and my friend went to the pawn shop, and I decided to stand outside. When I was standing outside, the defendant was with a girl named Jennifer. Is she in the car? Yes. Is he in the car? Yes. Okay, and you have driven to the pawn shop on your scooter? Yes. What kind of scooter? It was a tow, tow. It was a 49cc. Did you have any pictures of it? No. Okay, so go on.
Plaintiff: Well, when I was talking to her, the defendant hopped on my scooter and drove off and took off. Took off, stole your scooter right in front of you with someone who can identify him?

Plaintiff: Go on.
Plaintiff: Do you know Jennifer?

Plaintiff: Who’s Jennifer?
Plaintiff: His friend. Girlfriend or friend?
Plaintiff: Friend.
Plaintiff: So at that point, I felt my attention was taken away from the scooter for a reason.
Plaintiff: Yeah, you got had.
Plaintiff: So then what happens?
Plaintiff: You call the police?
Plaintiff: Call the police and then what?
Plaintiff: The police couldn’t really do much.

Plaintiff: Oh, because you didn’t know who the guy was.
Plaintiff: Yeah, until—

Plaintiff: Okay, and I go and tell the cops, and by that time, the defendant was already in jail.

Plaintiff: Because he was already in jail.
Judge: So what then?
Plaintiff: He can be in jail on two things. Why wouldn’t there—like, why didn’t the police follow through? What did they say to you?
Plaintiff: ’s Dad: Your honor, the detective says we cannot do anything anyway because he’s a juvenile.
Judge: What does that have to do with it? Obviously, things can be done to a juvenile. They’re just done in juvenile court. You still charge them with a crime, and then a judge decides a punishment. There’s juvenile hall. There’s—
Plaintiff: ’s Dad: Okay, alright, alright, alright, wait, just stop. Just stop. Stop, stop, stop. What’s going on, you H?
Defendant: Um, the day he said, um, I think he said June 8th, we was with my friend Jenny. He had come to the plaza. We was in talking about he’s selling a scooter. I’m like, how much you selling it for? He like, I want like a thousand. I’ll take eight. I’m like, word. I probably get like 650-700. He like, you know how to ride? I’m like, yeah. He gave me his helmet and the keys and was like, yo, where you going? I’m like, I’m going down to the gas station. Come right back up the street. I gets on, I goes. I’m gone for like 10 minutes. I came back to the plaza. He was gone. I don’t know him.
Judge: Yeah, you know how people are constantly just leaving the plaza after 10 minutes?
Defendant: I guess my scooter’s not coming back. I’m just going to invest in a 10-minute—not a second more than 10 minutes. Mama, you listening to him?
Plaintiff: Mm-hm. You know this is a load of garbage, right?
Defendant: Yeah.
Judge: So are parents largely responsible when a kid becomes a criminal? You got a kid here, so I’m going to ask you.
Judge: Yes, they’re completely responsible because from a young age, you have to mold them into the kind of adult that they should be.
Judge: What do you think? Are parents responsible if a kid becomes a criminal?

Judge: Okay, going inside the courtroom.
Plaintiff: ’s Dad: He has two scooters, so he wasn’t still my scooter. How’d you get the other two?

Judge: Yeah, but like he said he came back 10 minutes later. He was going, what was he in for? Now fighting, not stealing. He never got arrested for nothing stealing.
Defendant: So you come back in 10 minutes and the owner of a scooter has forfeited the scooter because you—‘cause he couldn’t wait 10 minutes for a scooter?
Plaintiff: How’d you get home?
Defendant: My house is directly across the street.
Plaintiff: Okay, so he walks home rather than wait for his scooter, and then what?

Defendant: It’s gone.

Defendant: No, it’s not obvious to me, because I don’t live in this fairy tale land. So I don’t know what you’re going to say next. Where did the scooter go?
Defendant: I’m not sure.
Judge: Well, was it g? The same day, a week later?

Judge: Tony Ash Maui, you are suing Oliver for $5,000 in damages to a side lamp, lamp post, wall, front stairs, railing, bricks, and foundation after what appears to have been a wild party. Is that correct?
Plaintiff: Yes.

Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Were you aware of the party happening?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: You’re the son?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: Yes.
Judge: Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: ’s Son: I had a few friends over on New Year’s Eve. We were watching the ball drop and just hanging out.
Judge: How many friends?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: About ten.
Judge: Were they friends and girlfriends?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: Yes. Then he and his buddies came over.
Judge: Were they invited?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: No.
Judge: So they came in uninvited?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: Yes, and they were very intoxicated. I talked to him.
Judge: You know him?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: Yes, from high school wrestling.
Judge: Were you friends?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: Yes.
Judge: So you see him inside your house, and then what?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: He came with three others.
Judge: Did you know them?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: I knew of them but didn’t really know them.
Judge: Did you ask them to leave?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: I confronted them and told them they were intoxicated. I didn’t want any trouble because one of them had issues with one of my friends who was there.
Judge: And then?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: One of them, Brendan, hit my friend in the face.
Judge: Who is Brendan?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: One of Oliver’s friends.
Judge: What happened next?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: The other three, including Oliver, jumped in and started fighting.
Judge: Fighting whom?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: Everyone in the mix—my friends.
Judge: How many people from your side got involved?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: About five.
Judge: And on Oliver’s side?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: Four.
Judge: And this was all happening in your kitchen?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: Yes, right by my stairs.
Judge: Where were you, Dad?
Plaintiff: I was at another New Year’s party and didn’t know anything until the next morning.
Judge: Did you know he was having people over?
Plaintiff: Yes, he told me, and it sounded fine.
Judge: When did you get the call?
Plaintiff: At 10:30 the next morning.
Judge: From whom?
Plaintiff: My son.
Judge: What did he say?
Plaintiff: He said he was at Home Depot picking up supplies for minor repairs.
Judge: How many stitches did you get on your head?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: Seven.
Judge: When did that come into the conversation?
Plaintiff: When I got home, he told me he might need to go to the ER. I looked at his head, and he was covered in blood. So I took him to urgent care right away.
Judge: When did you call the police?
Plaintiff: A girl at the party called the cops during the fight.
Judge: Did the police arrive before or after the fight ended?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: They arrived as we were getting them out of the house.
Judge: Did they arrest anyone?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: No, they just removed them.
Judge: What happened after the police left?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: Someone saw them outside again, grabbing the railing and breaking it.
Judge: Did you record them breaking it?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: No.
Judge: Oliver, what happened?
Defendant: I was invited.
Judge: Can you prove that?
Defendant: Yes, I have a text message.
Judge: Let me see.
Defendant: On December 29th, I asked him what he was doing. On December 31st, he replied, "Only like 20 peeps here. More than welcome."
Judge: But you originally said they weren’t invited.
Plaintiff: ’s Son: I didn’t invite his other friends.
Judge: What about this text message where you mention a racial slur?
Defendant: That wasn’t meant to be read.
Judge: I read what you don’t want me to read because that tells me what really happened. You apologized and offered to help fix things. So you felt bad?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: But you and your friends came back after the police left?
Defendant: No, I only came back to get my phone.
Judge: So the police escorted you off, and you returned?
Defendant: Yes, just to get my phone.
Judge: So when did the railing get broken?
Defendant: The first time we left, we got pushed off the railing.
Judge: You are contradicting yourself. Did you come back to cause trouble?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Why shouldn’t you have to pay?
Defendant: A fight broke out. It wasn’t all my fault.
Judge: But you acknowledge damages happened?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Let’s look at the damage. (Shows photos) What happened to the door?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: A body was slammed into it.
Judge: And the railing?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: It flipped over.
Judge: And the table?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: That’s the one that hit me on the head.
Judge: You got estimates?
Plaintiff: Yes. The defendant and his stepdad initially said they’d help fix it, but nothing happened for weeks.
Judge: Why didn’t you fix it?
Defendant: I couldn’t get the others to pitch in.
Judge: What’s your plan now?
Plaintiff: I’m suing all four of them separately.
Judge: Why separately?
Plaintiff: It’s easier in small claims court, and I need money to fix my house.
Judge: Your estimates are excessive. The pathway doesn’t need to be redone. I’m going to order the defendant to pay $2,000. That’s my verdict.
Manipulation Techniques: gaslighting, evasion, framing the narrative, deflection, minimization

Judge: Your position doesn't depend on this, but I think it's a little rough to tell a tax advisor, oh, figure out whether the Ninth and Eleventh Circuit opinions are thinly reasoned and don't follow them when they're—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So I don't—I don't mean to disparage those decisions in any way, but I think that if Your Honor would look at them, I don't think you would need to be a tax specialist to think that they are not a fulsome analysis of this issue.
There's a few sentences, and they don't engage at all with the arguments that—
Judge: Right, but the—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: —the IRS had been making.
Judge: Right. That's fair. But, normally, you'd rely on the bottom line, I think, if you were in that business, two—two courts of appeals, but—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, just to clarify that—
Judge: But you have a whole bunch of academics who for years have been writing about this.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: There are many academics writing about it, including one of the amicus here, Professor Chodorow.
There's also many, many other articles that have come out since those cases explaining why they're wrong, including those by Delaney, Burke, and Bogdanski, and other professors.
But I think just to go to sort of the heart of your question, the Tax Court, you know, is free—it doesn't have to follow the Ninth and Eleventh Circuit decisions.
So, if you are coming up in a circuit that is not the Ninth or Eleventh, there is no reason for you to think that those Ninth and Eleventh Circuit precedents are governing.
And I do expect that tax advisors would know that.
Judge: Mm-hmm.
Judge: But if I could just sort of put this in most simple—you know, it's a little bit hard for me to get this through my head, but your basic pitch is this is not any old liability.
This is a redemption obligation.
A redemption obligation is supposed to split the pie, so you come away with a smaller pie.
That's because that's what redemption obligations do.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes, that's correct. That's our basic pitch about a redemption obligation.
I would just add that the other part of our pitch is that the hole in their case is that they are trying to value Michael's shares after Crown already redeemed them.
You can't do that.
The price paid out for Michael's shares is value that goes to Michael's shares.
If you subtract that as a $3 million liability before trying to value Michael's shares, you will never get the correct answer.
Judge: Thank you, further? Anyone, anything further? No? Thank you.

Defendant: We have one girl, and she lives with me.
