Dialogue:
Judge: Carlos Hernandez, you are suing Thomas Shyer, yes, and Linda Hornay, you have power of attorney for Linda, for $2,350 that you say you are out as a result of an RV that they sold you. You have a counterclaim against them for $2,993 in fees that you say it’s going to cost you because he never registered it in his name and it got towed and stored.
Alright, let's start from the beginning. Mr. Hernandez, what happened?
Plaintiff: So, I was looking up on Craigslist for vehicles, for motorhomes, and I came across this one.
Judge: And why were you looking for motorhomes? You seem very young. How old are you?
Plaintiff: 28.
Judge: Okay, you look a lot younger. And you were in the process of moving out on your own and so I wanted to start small.
Judge: Starting small is getting an apartment, not owning a motorhome. But go ahead.
Plaintiff: Well, I was going to move it to a lot and then have a space where it's rented out.
Judge: So you see an ad and you like it and you buy it. Do you have the paperwork from the sale of the RV?
Plaintiff: He only gave me a bill of sale. He said on the ad that he had the pink slip and registration.
Judge: You sold him the RV for how much?
Defendant: $2,000.
Plaintiff: He says that it broke down on the way, the first day he had it, and that it died. When I followed him, we put $10 worth of gas in it.
Defendant: $10? That’s going to take your block. It took him 3 miles and he ran out of gas. I went there the next night. We moved the motorhome over by his mother’s.
Judge: How did you get it there?
Defendant: I believe I pushed it with my Land Cruiser.
Judge: Seriously?
Defendant: Yes.
Plaintiff: I paid for a tow.
Judge: And where did you tow it?
Defendant: I towed it over onto Vermont and down the street from my mother’s house, just to get it close by and off the freeway.
Judge: But you just dumped it on the road? You just left it there?
Defendant: No, I parked it there.
Judge: So, you parked it in the main street of a business area?
Defendant: It was in a non-residential area, a main street over by a bridge.
Judge: Didn’t think any of the business people were going to complain?
Defendant: Apparently, there was a problem.
Judge: How long was it there?
Defendant: I was there for 3 days.
Judge: What was your plan about where you were going to hide a motorhome until you got your act together?
Defendant: Well, it was there for 3 days.
Judge: So now the police come and they knock on your door. What do they say?
Defendant: They asked me if I had identification or a title or anything, and I wasn’t able to show any. I didn’t have the title. I had all this paperwork that he had given me, but it was mostly just maintenance records.
Judge: What did they arrest you for?
Defendant: Suspicion of a stolen vehicle, because it looked like I had stolen it.
Judge: How long were you in jail?
Defendant: 3 days.
Judge: And then what happened?
Defendant: They released me.
Judge: Did they throw out the case?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: When did they throw it out?
Defendant: A few days after I got arrested.
Judge: And then?
Defendant: They were able to reach him, and he said, "Yes, I sold it to him."
Judge: So, you get out of jail, did you call him?
Defendant: Yes, I called him, and I told him my situation.
Judge: What did he say?
Defendant: He said, "Oh, I forgot. You should have reminded me last time. Okay, we’ll do that."
Judge: And what happened then?
Defendant: He had to come to us, and we would take him to the DMV. He had all the paperwork, but the only thing that I had was the certificate of non-operation.
Judge: What does that mean?
Defendant: It means you cannot operate the vehicle on the road while you have a certificate of non-op.
Judge: So how are you going to get it there?
Defendant: One of the guys who worked for me at my house set up sewage, water, and electricity. I arranged with Carlos to go ahead and have AAA move the motorhome.
Judge: What happens then?
Defendant: It went into tow. I contacted the towing company, and they wanted to charge us $1,400 to get it out, but we couldn’t get it out unless we had the right paperwork from the DMV.
Judge: What did the DMV say?
Defendant: They said we would have to pay $22,900 in back fees because we removed it from the premises without filing the non-op.
Judge: What did Wendy from the tow yard say?
Defendant: Wendy told me she would let us take it out for $600.
Judge: Has it been auctioned?
Defendant: Yes, it’s been auctioned off.
Judge: Did you know that?
Defendant: Yes, they auctioned it off on the 23rd.
Judge: But the people haven’t come to pick it up?
Defendant: No, not yet.
Judge: And now Wendy said if you come up with $800 by Friday, you can get it back?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Okay, here's what we’re going to do. My ruling is that if you can come up with $800 by close of business on Friday, you can buy the RV back from the storage facility. If you don’t, I will find that the defendant has abandoned all title and rights to it, and anyone can go pick it up, including anyone in the audience. That’s my verdict.

Judge: This is the plaintiff, Thees Gashin. She says the defendant pushed her way into living in her home, brought her boyfriend in to live with her too, then, to top things off, stole her snowblower. She thought the defendant was her friend, but it turns out she's nothing but a user, and she's here suing her for the $1,500 it's going to cost her to buy a new snowblower.
Judge: This is the defendant, Nini Singh. She says she purchased a used Honda snowblower from the plaintiff, paid her 500 bucks in cash, and has a receipt to prove it. The plaintiff blames her for being fired from the job they worked at. She's so happy now they're no longer friends.
Judge: She's accused of blowing a friendship.
Bailiff: All parties, please raise your right hands.
Judge: Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff says the defendant strong-armed her way into living with the plaintiff at her house, then had the nerve to steal her snowblower. The defendant says the plaintiff sold it. It's the case of "You Dirty Snowblower."
Judge: Thank you, Douglas. Thees Ganesan?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: You are suing Nalani Singh for $1,500, the cost of a snowblower that, according to you, she has kept from you. Tell me what's going on.
Plaintiff: It's not going to be the cost of the snowblower, but I'm asking for compensation to get one in place. Uh, I purchased a snowblower in 2015.
Judge: A used one?
Plaintiff: A used one, okay. I got it for, I paid only $600, but it was an antique.
Judge: Antique? Meaning how old was it?
Plaintiff: A 2008 one, but the person who was selling it didn’t use it. He was going through a divorce and wanted to get rid of it immediately before his wife could get the money.
Judge: Right, okay. So go on.
Plaintiff: I thought she was my friend.
Judge: How did you two know each other?
Plaintiff: Through work. She was working in an independent handicap place, and she told me that I could come and work there. She got me the job. But I thought she was really getting me a job, but she was actually using me to cover her time off.
Judge: Okay. You had said in your complaint that she was married and wanted to rent a room in your home, and you thought she was joking. Tell me about that.
Plaintiff: While she was talking, she would say that she wanted to rent a room, she wanted a room, she wanted a room, because she knew that I have a house and I'm a widow.
Judge: What did she want a room for?
Plaintiff: She was saying that she has a boyfriend and wanted to bring her boyfriend.
Judge: Is she married?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: So she has a house with a husband that she's living in, and she wanted an apartment, according to you, so she could see her boyfriend?
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: And then you—you said yes?
Plaintiff: No, I did not say yes at all. But she is a person who, if she wants something, she gets it.
Judge: I'm sorry, so did you rent her a room?
Plaintiff: I did not rent the room.
Judge: Did she give you money for the room?
Plaintiff: She gave me $100 for utilities.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: She ended up coming, took everything out of the room, and put her stuff there. She would just come and go.
Judge: So she just pushed her way into your home?
Plaintiff: She also brought her boyfriend there. I was not happy about it.
Judge: Why didn’t you call the police and say there's an intruder?
Plaintiff: At that time, I was still...
Judge: Alright. So, the snowblower—according to you, what happened with the snowblower?
Plaintiff: She saw the snowblower there, and she wanted to take the snowblower to use it, because, according to her, the person who was plowing her snow went to jail and they couldn’t get somebody to do it.
Judge: Did you sell her the snowblower?
Plaintiff: No, I did not.
Judge: Did you—?
Plaintiff: She told me that she—she borrowed the snowblower.
Judge: Okay. And were you charging her for borrowing the snowblower?
Plaintiff: She told me that, to use it, she will give me something.
Judge: What did she give you?
Plaintiff: $250.
Judge: While you were working?
Plaintiff: She came, and she said, "Sign this receipt, sign this receipt."
Judge: Why did you sign?
Plaintiff: I did sign it because she was interfering at work. I said, "Get out." That's what I do. They come to me, they say, "Sign this, sign this," and I say, "Why are you coming to my workplace? I will see you later."
Judge: So you signed something. What was it?
Plaintiff: Something that you signed—what did it say?
Plaintiff: To get her out of that place, I signed it.
Judge: Okay. Let me see it.
Defendant: Oh, you have a carbon copy of it.
Judge: Okay. So the one that you signed was on top of that. She took that one and gave you the carbon.
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: So then what happens?
Plaintiff: She hasn't returned it.
Judge: And so you want her to buy you a $1,500 one?
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: No, not that. Well, that's what you're doing. You're suing for $1,500.
Plaintiff: Yeah, because I can't get the same kind now. And I asked her to return it.
Judge: What's going on with the snowblower? First of all, would you like to respond about the love nest she says you had in her apartment?
Defendant: Yes, I have known her for almost 10 years. We were friends. I thought—she’s older than me, I trusted her like a mother. I used to tell her all my problems, because my marriage is in problems.
Judge: Did you get a room in her house?
Defendant: No, I did not.
Judge: Okay. So, did you pay $100 in utilities?
Defendant: No, I did not.
Judge: Okay. So tell me about the snowblower.
Defendant: I bought it from her.
Judge: When did you buy it from her?
Defendant: November 11, 2016.
Judge: Let me see what you have.
(Defendant hands over receipt)
(The judge examines signatures, finds inconsistencies, and accuses the defendant of forgery.)
Judge: Miss Singh, you know what I think? I think you forged this. I'm going to order you to recompense the plaintiff $600—the amount that she paid. You don’t get a new one, you get the value of the thing that was taken from you.
(Plaintiff nods in agreement.)
Judge: Verdict for the plaintiff. I'm done with both of you.
Manipulation Techniques: gaslighting, persuasion, deflection, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge: Thank you, Douglas. Tiffany Archer, you are suing your—what is he of yours?
Plaintiff: He was an acquaintance with benefits.
Judge: Your former something, right?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Jarell Bryant, for $4,050 that you say he owes you in loans, a cable bill, a Bluetooth speaker he broke, and $2,000 for intentional tort.
Judge: All right. And you are counterclaiming her for pain and suffering for bed bugs. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: Okay. Um, we started dating in October. We met before then. He was working for a cab service. His work started to slow down, and then he expressed interest in working for Uber.
Plaintiff: Now, there are things that I acknowledge that are gifts. There are things that I purchased for him. There are things that I’ve given to him. I’m suing for the $1,650 because one portion was for a vehicle so that he could obtain work with the company. And the extra $150—
Judge: He didn’t even have a car, but he wanted to be an Uber driver? Don’t you need a car first?
Plaintiff: Right.
Judge: So you were buying him the car so he could be an Uber driver?
Plaintiff: Right.
Judge: So if I called Uber and he answered, I was going to be riding around in a $1,000 car?
Plaintiff: No, actually, I gave him $1,500 for the car.
Judge: That makes me feel so much better.
Plaintiff: No, I gave him—
Judge: Did you buy a car with $1,500 that you could use for Uber?
Defendant: Um, I bought a car, but I didn’t use it for Uber.
Judge: What car did you buy?
Defendant: Uh, I bought an Impala.
Judge: What year?
Defendant: 2007.
Judge: Did you tell her that you wanted to borrow money so you could buy a car?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Where did this Uber stuff come from?
Defendant: Um, well, I was driving cabs, and, you know, Uber’s a hot thing, so I was interested in becoming an Uber driver, but you know, I—I drive a gypsy cab.
Judge: And did she loan you the money for the car?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Did she give you the money for the car?
Defendant: Why would she give me that money?
Judge: Um, you tell me.
Defendant: We were an item.
Judge: So what? I don’t—nobody rains money on me. I’ve been an item with a lot of people. They don’t rain money on me like that. I’m cute!
Judge: Did you have him sign a promissory note?
Plaintiff: I did not.
Judge: What were you doing that you were able to give away money to your boyfriend—so much?
Plaintiff: I work as a home health aide. I do live-in, and I work four days at a time. So I go in on Thursday and come off on Monday.
Plaintiff: But prior to this, the situation I was in, um, my home was in foreclosure, and we were paid out. And I had already saved money up, so I was okay.
Judge: So you couldn’t wait to get rid of it.
Plaintiff: It’s just that I—I help people if I’m okay.
Judge: And how often do you get burned?
Plaintiff: A lot.
Judge: Okay. I have a philosophy that you should only pay for someone else if you don’t mind seeing it go away. Because you will end up with a lot of broken friendships.
Judge: So if you want to help somebody out in their time of need, why don’t you just get it in writing?
Judge: So when is the first time that there’s any discussion between the two of you that you want him to pay you back?
Plaintiff: Well, when I gave it to him, I told him right off as soon as I handed him the money.
Plaintiff: As soon as he got the money—because the text comes to my phone—I wired it from Western Union because I was at work at the time.
Judge: Yeah, you’re burning Western Union’s wires. How many times did you wire money?
Plaintiff: I would say five, six, seven. Around about five times, probably, in the process of me—
Judge: Did she tell you when she gave you the money that you would have to pay it back?
Defendant: No.
Judge: And that’s what we guard against by having a promissory note. Or even a text that says it.
Judge: If you’re too embarrassed—if you think, "Oh, I don’t want to seem like I don’t trust him by making him sign a promissory note"—I don’t know why, but if that’s what’s in your head, then just send a text saying, "I’m so glad I was able to help you. When do you think you can pay me back?"
Judge: Oh, don’t worry, I’ll be able to pay you back in a few months.
Judge: Done. That’s a contract. There’s no dispute about whether it’s a loan or a gift, right?
Judge: If you’re suing for money you gave him for a car, money you gave him for insurance, money you gave him for a cable bill that you opened up in your name—
Judge: You think you’re going to—You’re moving in? What do you think?      Plaintiff: No, the thing is, because he's in a niche, and he gets his rent paid through public assistance. Because I'm standing here, a lot of people that stay with you, they don't like to pull their weight. So I said, while I'm staying, at least let me—
Judge: But you're not even supposed to be staying there though, right?
Plaintiff: Well, I wasn't, but I wasn't planning on living there. But I just—
Judge: But you just put a bill in your name there. And when you put a bill in your name there, you had to pay $100. Why?
Plaintiff: That's actually the cable bill.
Judge: No, the cable bill was $139. But the bank statements that I got, it doesn't show the other half where I paid. But why does he have to pay you back the $100?
Plaintiff: Oh, because when I left, we made an agreement that, being that I was not going to stay there, he was going to continue to pay the bill. He has others entertaining—family and friends—cable, right? And I'm not staying there.
Judge: Why don't you just cancel the cable? Why didn't you just do that? Why would you trust a guy to pay the bill? Did he pay the bill?
Plaintiff: He did, for the first—And it's just that the last bill he didn't pay.
Judge: But he owes you $100 on the cable?
Defendant: No.
Judge: How not?
Defendant: I don’t—
Judge: Show me the proof of everything you paid on the cable.
Defendant: I don’t have any proof.
Judge: Okay, well then, you owe her $100 on the cable if you don't have any proof.
Judge: Broken Bluetooth speaker—What happened there?
Defendant: Um, she left a speaker. I don’t remember her leaving a speaker. She came back, and it was broke. I had a bunch of kids in my house.
Judge: And then what did you do with the speaker?
Defendant: I threw it away. It was broke.
Judge: Yeah, that speaker didn’t break itself. You owe her for the speaker. Another $100. I don’t like that at all.
Judge: Intentional tort—What do you mean when you say intentional tort? Somebody did a little reading on the internet.
Plaintiff: Intentional tort, yes. Because, um, I feel that he misrepresented himself when he asked for the car. I thought he was actually going to get a job, try to better himself, make better of his situation so he could take better care of himself and pay me back. So this is why I loaned him the money for the car.
Judge: So you want—So the intentional tort is you chose unwisely?
Plaintiff: Well, I feel like he misrepresented himself.
Judge: All right. So now let me ask you about your lawsuit.
Defendant: Oh, okay, yeah.
Judge: Bed bugs and pain and suffering—Talk to me.
Defendant: All right, so Tiffany had moved into an apartment, and they had, um, I guess a pre-existing bed bug situation. And you know, she was staying at my house Monday through Wednesday towards the middle—Towards the beginning—And you know, I mean, it felt a little funny, a little itchy and stuff, but I can say by the time she left, I had a bed bug situation. You know, I had to call housing, get them to deal with it.
Judge: Can I see proof of that?
Defendant: Yeah.
Judge: Did you have a bed bug situation in the house that you were in?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: I'm trying to imagine. So I have a bed bug situation—Can I come stay with you? Let me take my—I'll just bring my sheets and my luggage and everything else a bed bug can hitch a ride on. Let’s do that. That sounds like a—like a capital idea!
Defendant: Says we exterminating for gnats and water bugs and mice.
Judge: Whoa! You're living in a—No. You see, they came for roaches and rats. They came to spray for everything. When you tell them you have a problem with bugs, they come and handle everything.
Judge: Okay, target organism, though, is what you complained about. And that was roaches.
Defendant: Right.
Judge: And that was in March. Anyway, where—Where am I finding a bed bug problem that she brought into your place?
Defendant: This is July of 2018.
Judge: When were you there?
Plaintiff: December of 2017.
Judge: Yes. Right. This is July of 2018. This is the other day. So where’s your proof that she caused a bed bug problem that would entitle you to $3,000?
Judge: I mean, listen, bed bug lawsuits are awful because it is really hard to determine who brought in the bed bug. Nobody knows but the bed bug, and the bed bug ain't talking.
Judge: So it's very hard to—But assuming, okay, that she—I mean, yours is a better case than most because she had bed bugs and then went to your house to stay, which was—No offense—On your part. But okay.
Judge: Except here’s the problem I'm having. I'm not finding you complaining to the housing authority to spray, and it's free. When you complain to them to spray, they just do it. You don’t have to pay for it, nothing. Right?
Defendant: Right.
Judge: I don’t see you complaining about it until four months after she moves out.
Judge: Welcome back to The People’s Court. Harvey Levin here. So, um, if you loan a friend money, do you expect payment back, or do you just think, "Whatever, if he pays back, great. If not—"?
[Public Opinions and Responses]
Judge: Technically, she never originally moved in because, like I told her, I wasn't ready for no relationship. But she gets out of work, she comes straight to my house Monday morning, and she’ll stay till Wednesday.
Judge: Okay. When did you two break up?
Defendant: Huh?
Judge: When did you two stop making love to each other, or having sex, or whatever you guys want to call it? I don’t know what to call it.
Defendant: I don't know.
Judge: I don’t find a complaint until March 19th.
Plaintiff: If I may say—
Judge: And they sprayed for everything. The target on March 19th—They may have sprayed for bed bugs, but the target on March 19th was roaches.
Defendant: I told them I had bugs. They said they were going to come handle the bugs.
Judge: You know what? If you had bed bugs, you'd know. You claim you had to throw out furniture. Do you have any proof you had to throw out furniture?
Defendant: Um, no. I didn’t bring it.
Judge: No, because she has pictures of you on the furniture you say you threw out.
Defendant: Yeah, but that was, like—How many months ago?
Plaintiff: November.
Judge: I don’t know. When was that?
Plaintiff: January 21st.
Judge: January.
Defendant: It takes a while for the bugs to grow, you know? They come little, and then they take time to sprout out.
Judge: I had them, so I would know. It is a disaster.   Defendant: Yeah, it was bad.
Judge: But what I'm saying is, when you claim you threw away furniture, I want to see some proof that you actually threw away furniture.
Defendant: Well, I don’t have any.
Judge: You don’t have any. So I’m looking at a lawsuit for $3,000 for pain and suffering and damages, and you don't have any proof that you actually threw out furniture. You also waited months before complaining to housing about it.
Judge: So you want me to make the leap that she brought the bed bugs, but your own timeline is inconsistent.
Defendant: Well, I didn't really think about it like that. I just know I had them, and I didn't have them before she started coming around.
Judge: Okay. But bed bugs don’t just immediately show up and take over in a few days. They take time to spread. You didn't complain until months later. So, I’m denying your claim for $3,000.
Judge: Now, back to your case, ma’am.
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: You’re suing for the car money, the insurance money, and the cable bill. I’m awarding you the cable bill portion—$100—since he failed to show proof of payment.
Judge: As for the car money, you’re calling it a loan. He’s calling it a gift. Do you have anything in writing?
Plaintiff: No, but we had a verbal agreement.
Judge: A verbal agreement is only as good as the proof you have to back it up. You don’t have text messages? Emails? Anything that states he was supposed to pay you back?
Plaintiff: No, I just trusted him.
Judge: That was your mistake. Without proof that it was a loan and not a gift, I can’t force him to pay you back. I’m dismissing that portion of your case.
Plaintiff: But—
Judge: No, I understand. It’s unfair. It’s frustrating. But legally, I can’t enforce an agreement I don’t have proof of.
Judge: So here’s my final ruling—Plaintiff is awarded $100 for the cable bill and $100 for the broken speaker. Defendant’s counterclaim is dismissed.
Judge: Judgment for the plaintiff in the amount of $200.

Judge: Again, I'm talking about the --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: But I don't think that the Court could draw the same conclusion here.
Judge: -- I'm not talking about the compelling interest or any of that.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Right.
Judge: I'm talking about the tailoring. And -- and you're saying we have no alternative but to stop this speech altogether. We can't -- we can't rely on disclosure. But you say that wouldn't apply to Politico or to the Oxford University Press because?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: In the circumstance where you have a newspaper that is understood to reflect its publisher's views, then you might think that disclosure would be a more adequate remedy there because it's not just holding itself out as a forum for speech between other people. I think social media platforms do raise distinct interests in this regard because what people think when they're engaging with TikTok is that it's organically feeding them videos based on the recommendation engine. And if actually China is behind the scenes engaging in this kind of covert operation, it does present a distinct national security risk. Of course, the other big difference with a newspaper is it's not likely to be collecting sensitive personal information about 170 million-plus people and then having the capacity to send that back to a foreign adversary.
Judge: General Prelogar, can I --
Judge: Counsel --
Judge: Oh.
Judge: Go ahead.
Judge: -- I was just going to say, did I understand you to say a few minutes ago that one problem that Byte -- is that ByteDance might be, through TikTok, trying to get Americans to argue with each other?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: That it might be just trying to foment disruption or --
Judge: If they do, I say they're winning.

Defendant: 's Lawyer: That might very well be true, Mr. Chief Justice, and I think the point I'm trying to make is that China is a foreign adversary nation that looks for every opportunity it has to weaken the United States and to try to threaten our national security. And if it has control over this key communications channel, it's hard to predict ex ante exactly how it's going to use that as a tool to harm our interests. But we know it's going to try first and foremost by seeking to get the data of these American users, which would be of a piece of all of the activity the PRC has already undertaken to breach our laws, hack OPM, for example, and exfiltrate the background files and security clearances of 20 million government employees, the breach of Equifax to get sensitive financial data, Anthem to get sensitive healthcare data. We know that the PRC has a voracious appetite to get its hands on as much information about Americans as possible, and that creates a potent weapon here because the PRC could command that ByteDance comply with any request it gives to obtain that data that's in the hands of the U.S. subsidiary.
Judge: Thank you.
Judge: Suppose --
Judge: General Prelogar -- go ahead.
Judge: Suppose that TikTok had no connection whatsoever with any foreign government. It was owned instead by an immensely, immensely rich multinational corporation, and Congress concluded that this multinational corporation really has it in for the United States and is going to use this extremely popular platform to do everything it can to undermine the United States in all the ways in which you think that TikTok may -- may pursue at the direction of the PRC. Would this --would that be the same case?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I think there would be a first-order question of whether the multinational corporation itself has First Amendment rights.
Judge: All right. It's a -- it's an American corporation.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So, if it were an American corporation, I think that --and Congress disagreed with the viewpoints or content the corporation would display, obviously, that's a direct regulation of protected speech, and it would trigger strict scrutiny. I think that's different in kind from what Congress was worried about here, which was not regulating speech as such but instead regulating foreign adversary control and --
Judge: So your whole -- your argument depends on the fact that what is at bottom here is the -- the People's Republic of China using TikTok. That's what your argument depends on. If this were an American corporation, it would be an entirely different thing.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Exactly. And the reason we know this statute is different is because all of the same speech that's happening on TikTok could happen post-divestiture. The Act doesn't regulate that at all. So it's not saying you can't have pro-China speech, you can't have anti-American speech. It's not regulating the algorithm. TikTok, if it were able to do so, could use precisely the same algorithm to display the same content by the same users. All the Act is doing is trying to surgically remove the ability of a foreign adversary nation to get our data and to be able to exercise control over the platform.
Judge: General Prelogar -- oh, sorry.
Judge: I'm sorry.
Judge: I just wanted you to respond to Mr. Fisher's argument about the rights of Americans to receive information, say, from the PRC or anyone else and that even if ByteDance did not itself have First Amendment rights, that Americans would have a First Amendment right to --to receive that information in the Lamont sense.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes. So I think that Lamont reflected a principle that there can be a right of American listeners to receive information. And if Congress is directly regulating that based on disagreement with the speech that's being sent into this country, that's obviously going to trigger heightened scrutiny under the First Amendment. But, here, I think the users have to be asserting a different type of interest because what Congress was safeguarding against was not the ability of TikTok to continue to operate or the users to post content. It was focused only on foreign adversary control. And so the users would have to demonstrate that they have some unqualified First Amendment right to post on a platform that's controlled by a foreign adversary, which could use that access to then threaten our nation's security by gathering data on tens or hundreds of millions of Americans and also use it for covert influence operations of whatever form. And I don't think there's a First Amendment right to do that.

Judge: Can I give you a hypothetical? He does a fundraiser for his mosque. He has no idea that the mosque is under suspicion. You put him on the No Fly List. You now give him this declaration that says we're not going to put you on the No Fly List for anything that's happened in the past. Tomorrow he holds another fundraiser for the same mosque. Can you put him back on the list under this declaration?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So I -- again, I would want to know more about exactly --
Judge: There is nothing more. You put him on because he did a fundraiser for this mosque. Will this declaration stop you tomorrow from putting him on the list for doing a fundraiser for that mosque?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So I'll answer you directly, but I need to -- I feel compelled to, despite the premise --
Judge: All of the facts are the same.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: We -- we --
Judge: Just answer the question.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: No Fly List determinations are not made with respect to any particular event, conduct, anything. They are always based on a totality of the information.
Judge: So answer the question. Can you put him on the list for doing the fundraiser the next day for the same mosque?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: By hypothesis, if your suggestion is that somehow the fundraiser is to fund terrorism -- I mean, I -- I -- I -- I don't want to jump into a hypothetical where --
Judge: That's the -- that's the point, isn't it?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Huh?
Judge: That without a declaration that basically says, for this activity, whatever that might be, we're not going to put him on the No Fly List, then how is the case mooted?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I -- I don't think activity is the right unit of analysis. When you're trying to decide whether something is moot --
Judge: Well, but the charge is terrorism activity or the threat thereof. So it's all about activity.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yes. But it's based on a totality of information, and the standard is poses a threat of conducting in or engaging one of four enumerated acts of terrorism. I don't think it's reasonable to expect that someone will engage in those acts. And what Respondent has is a declaration saying, as of this date or when you were taken off the list, you were taken off not as a matter of grace, not because we think you belong on the list but we're just taking you off anyway, but was taken off because he did not satisfy the criteria.
Judge: Well, Mr. Lawyer -- I'm sorry. Finish your sentence. I -- I do find this a very perplexing case because you -- you have to show, in order to establish that the case is moot, that putting it in maybe simpler terms than we have used in our opinions, it's very unlikely or maybe very, very unlikely that he will again be put on the No Fly List. Do you agree with that?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I think the language is reasonable expectation of recurrence.
Judge: Well, it's a strange formulation where it says that you have to be absolutely certain that there's not a reasonable expectation. I don't really know how to put --
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Well --
Judge: -- those two things together, but let's just say it's some degree of unlikelihood, okay? It's hard to say how -- hard to tell how you can prove that without -- convince us of that without knowing why he was put on in the first place and why he was taken off. It's a -- you know, as you say, it's based on the totality of the circumstances. So there is the possibility that any additional relevant circumstance could be just the thing that tips the balance and he could be put back on.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So --
Judge: So that's -- that's why I'm perplexed by your mootness argument. But I'm equally perplexed by the fact because you have the burden of showing mootness. But the plaintiff has the burden of showing standing. So I'm not quite sure what good it would do the Petitioner -- I'm sorry, the Respondent if we say, well, the case isn't moot because the government hasn't proven that it's very unlikely that you'll be put back on, but once you get back in district court, you're not going to be able to prove that you have standing for purposes of injunctive relief because your -- your -- your claim that you're going to be -- you may be put back on is entirely speculative. So, anyway, that's why I find it perplexing.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I -- I -- I take the point. So let me try and address both pieces of that. First, as to, you know, the probability of recurrence or whatever, yes, it is our burden. We -- we agree with that. We acknowledge that. I guess what I would say is I would fall back on the fact that the standard is poses a threat of engaging in one of four enumerated acts of terrorism, that this is a U.S. person who's the plaintiff. And I would fall back on this Court's case law such as Lyons saying that for Article III purposes, it is unreasonable to expect a plaintiff to engage in illegal conduct again. And in the case of Lyons, even to simply be arrested by LAPD again, that was unreasonable to expect. If that's true for Article III purposes, I think you should be just as hesitant to expect a U.S. person to engage or to pose a threat of engaging in one of the four enumerated acts of terrorism.
Judge: How can someone tell you that they're not going to engage in a terrorist activity if they don't know what terrorist activity it is that you claim they did? How can I reasonably be expected to say I'm not going to do X when I don't know what X is?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Again, it's -- I don't think it's reasonable to expect anyone to pose a threat of engaging in international terrorism in that --
Judge: I mean, Mr. Lawyer, you're -- you're -- you're arguing the merits of the case. You obviously think that you have good reasons for putting people on the list. And, you know, on the other hand, the -- the suit, the whole gravamen of the complaint, is that you were not using good reasons. And we can't decide the merits of the case. I think that Justice's hypothetical is an extremely important one because it really asks, what does this declaration commit you to? The declaration clearly says that you can't use any facts that you know of now, so any facts that have happened in the past cannot be used to -- to relist the plaintiff. But the question that she's asking is, if he does the same kinds of things, if he meets with the same kinds of people, if he associates with the same kinds of organizations, can those same kinds of activities that put him on the list before put him on the list again? And I do think that you have to give a kind of yes-or-no answer to that question so that we can figure out what this declaration does and does not commit you to.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yeah. So, if you want a yes-or-no answer, my answer is yes because --
Judge: Yes what?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yes --
Judge: He can go back on?
Judge: Yes, you can put him back on --
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Correct.

Defendant: “You’re just trying to manipulate me for money, aren’t you? This whole thing with the kid, it’s just a way for you to trap me. I bet you’ve been planning this from the start, trying to get me to pay for a child that might not even be mine.”
Plaintiff: “I’m not trying to trap you. I don’t want your money. I just want you to be there for your son. I don’t want him growing up without a father like I did.”
Defendant: “Oh please, you’re just using that as an excuse. You’re probably just trying to get some child support out of me. I’m not even sure I’m the father, and you won’t even let me get a DNA test. I’m not falling for your tricks.”
Plaintiff: “I’ve been trying to get you to help out with the child, to be responsible. I’m not asking for anything but what’s right. This isn’t about money, it’s about our kid having a father.”
Defendant: “Yeah, sure. You say that now, but I know what you’re really after. You probably just sent me the pregnancy test results to force me into something I’m not ready for. You’re manipulating me into this whole thing. It’s all just a way for you to control me.”

Judge: All right, Mr. Willis, you're suing DJ Dre for $150 of a deposit because a party didn't happen. Tell me what happens here.
Plaintiff: Okay, so I was throwing his fraternity brother, which happens to be my actual best friend in real life.
Judge: In real life as opposed to fraternity life?
Plaintiff: Yes, I'm not in his fraternity. Okay, um, I was throwing a birthday party, a surprise birthday party for him, and I was referred to DJ Dre through one of their line sisters. And I, you know, I reached out to him and tried to book him for the service or actually booked him for the service. I booked him for the 27th.
Judge: 27th of?
Plaintiff: March.
Judge: And what day did you book him?
Plaintiff: For the 27th of March, I believe I sent the deposit March 14th.
Judge: Okay, go on.
Plaintiff: I went on to book him for the event. Due to finances, I had to cancel the event on the 22nd.
Judge: Why?
Plaintiff: Me and his fraternity brother's mom were the only ones paying for the event.
Judge: Cuz were other people—well, why are you throwing a party you can't afford? What was the plan originally?
Plaintiff: That everybody would contribute.
Judge: Other people?
Plaintiff: And then they didn't come through.
Judge: Oh, this is so sad. Does he know that we have all this litigation over what should have been his birthday party? Does he? It's like he must feel terrible.
Plaintiff: I tried. I tried to not tell him about it.
Judge: Well, he's going to know now, pal, because you're on national television. As it turns out, you changed your mind about the party, and then you tell—because of what you just said—and then you tell Dre that you want the money back. And what does Dre tell you?
Plaintiff: He tells me no, he doesn't do refunds.
Judge: Right. And then your answer to that is what?
Plaintiff: That's not what was said in the contract.
Judge: Okay. And what was said in the contract?
Plaintiff: So he sent me a note, and he sent me what I would be paying is for a deposit and the rest of the money and everything else that we cover, right? Um, under the deposit area, it doesn't say anything about non-refundable, refundable, none of that.
Judge: Okay, and let me hear from you, Dre. Why won’t you return the guy’s money?
Defendant: So, um, as I told him in the text messages, I don’t do refunds on deposits. Like, it’s kind of an industry standard for any industry that really does deposits.
Judge: Why? What’s the purpose of a deposit, Dre?
Defendant: So it's an insurance for us, honestly. Uh, we commit to whatever you want us to commit to, same with you committing to us. Then, um, yeah. So I’m turning down other gigs, any preparation work I got to do—
Judge: I mean, what do you think, Mr. Willis? Is the premise of a deposit to book you for the date?
Plaintiff: Right.
Judge: So what makes you think that you can put down a deposit and then just turn around and say, okay, you’re reserving that date for me, to the detriment of all other people and your own business, but you know what? I want it to be for free. So I changed my mind. In other words, the fact that it doesn’t say non-refundable—the default position is that it’s non-refundable. That’s the point of a deposit. You’re taking something off the market, whether it’s a car or a house or his services for a certain date. He’s taking it off the market on your behalf. And so, you know, then you change your mind. Why would you not lose the deposit?
Judge: Welcome back to the People's Court. The plaintiff says the defendant's simply an unscrupulous businessman who stole his money. The defendant certainly doesn't see it that way. Let's go back into the courtroom and see who's right.
Judge: On his claim against you, zero. And on your counterclaim against him, also zero. Good luck, gentlemen.
Judge: So there are no winners in this case in the People's Court. Both the plaintiff and the defendant have lost their suits.
Judge: That’s going to do it for us now, and we will see you in the next session of the People’s Court.

Judge: So you are a social media influencer?
Defendant: Yeah, it's crazy.
Judge: But what does being that have to do with you being a mom?
Defendant: I'm out there trying to make money and trying to support my child. I do send him money. I mean, I'm just trying to grow my business.
Judge: How much money do you send him?
Defendant: Um, it just depends on what's going on during the week, you know—what I have and what my income is each month.
Judge: On average, how much money are you getting from her, if any?
Plaintiff: She only sends about maybe like two, three hundred.
Judge: Two or three hundred dollars? You're a social media influencer.
Defendant: Well, I'm still growing my business. I mean, I'm—

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff let the defendant live with him, but she wanted a romantic relationship, and when he said no, she left without paying. The defendant says they dated for five years before and he’s the one who got jealous. It’s the case of "Want to Hear a Bad Idea?" Thank you, Douglas.
Bailiff: You're welcome.
Judge: All right, so Mr. Miller, the two of you know each other from high school?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: All right, how did you two know each other in high school? Did you date?
Plaintiff: We grew up down the street from each other. We dated a bit in high school, yes.
Judge: How long did you date?
Plaintiff: Four years.
Judge: Oh, that's a big "bit." That's all of high school. High school lasts four years.
Plaintiff: It was also about a decade ago.
Judge: I know, but that’s all of high school.
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: So, how did you guys reconnect, or had you stayed connected?
Plaintiff: We hadn’t stayed connected, but I reached out to her over Facebook to start being friends again, I guess. And after talking for a few months, she realized she wanted to move to Florida. I needed a roommate, and that’s when we decided to—
Judge: So you live where?
Plaintiff: Jacksonville.
Judge: Jacksonville, yes. All right, so you invited her to be your roommate?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Did you come up with any plan on what the rent would be?
Plaintiff: Yes, we had agreed on $450 a month in rent and splitting utilities. And I have proof of that being discussed.
Judge: I’d like to see that. What proof do you have? Is it Facebook?
Plaintiff: I have messages on Facebook as well as text messages. Unfortunately, and this is my own lack of foresight, I didn’t get a lease signed.
Judge: Okay, you can stay where you are, don’t approach me. That’s why my bailiff is there.
Bailiff: Thank you.
Judge: He gets all kinds of anxious when people approach. Oh my God, I can’t read that. That’s like 1.5 font.
Plaintiff: I know, I have a terrible printer. I can’t afford a new one.
Judge: Okay, let’s start with—it’s upside down, that’s how much I can’t read it. Okay, when does she move in with you? Listen, you’re young. How old are you?
Plaintiff: Twenty-nine.
Judge: Okay, let me explain to you what judge-friendly font is. It’s not one-point font.
Plaintiff: Understood.
Judge: All right, so she moves in with you when?
Plaintiff: August 1st.
Judge: So these are what exactly?
Plaintiff: These are messages within Facebook where we discuss the cost of rent being $450 a month, and she says $450 is okay with her and she can handle it.
Judge: Do any of them predate her moving down?
Plaintiff: No, I don’t have that.
Judge: Well, how did you two decide what rent would be before moving down?
Plaintiff: We decided it over the phone. We had many phone conversations and agreed on—
Judge: How does this happen in every single case where people tell me "Here’s the proof that we had the rent"?
Plaintiff: I know, it’s my fault. I trusted a friend.
Judge: Okay, no. You’re communicating through Facebook, and you guys are of a generation that takes pictures of the food you eat before you do, so why isn’t that here?
Plaintiff: Understood. But every time I bring up the $450, it’s not refuted by her.
Judge: That’s a different level of proof than what I’m saying. I would love to see a message saying, "No, Judge, I don’t owe him that." But okay. So now she moves in with you on what day?
Plaintiff: August 1st.
Judge: And then what happens?
Plaintiff: The defendant, Mr. Thompson, was seen exiting the jewelry store at approximately 3:15 PM. Surveillance footage confirms this timeline.
Defendant: Objection, Your Honor. The footage only shows a figure in a dark hoodie; it does not definitively identify my client.
Judge: Sustained. Plaintiff, please clarify the basis for identifying Mr. Thompson.
Plaintiff: Your Honor, aside from the footage, we have testimony from Officer Martinez, who apprehended the defendant less than two blocks away, with a bag containing items matching those reported stolen.
Judge: Officer Martinez, please take the stand.
(Officer Martinez is sworn in.)
Plaintiff: Officer, can you describe the events of the afternoon in question?
Officer Martinez: Yes, around 3:20 PM, I received a radio call regarding a robbery at the jewelry store. A suspect wearing a dark hoodie and jeans was reported fleeing westbound. When I turned onto Maple Avenue, I saw Mr. Thompson matching the description.
Defendant: Officer, is it possible that other individuals in the area also matched that description?
Officer Martinez: It is possible, but when I approached Mr. Thompson, he immediately ran. I pursued and detained him. In his possession was a black duffel bag containing various pieces of jewelry, which were later identified as stolen from the store.
Plaintiff: Was the defendant able to provide an explanation for possessing the jewelry?
Officer Martinez: No, he refused to answer my questions.
Defendant: Your Honor, I move to exclude the defendant’s silence as evidence of guilt.
Judge: Granted. The jury will disregard the defendant’s silence. Continue, Plaintiff.
Plaintiff: Thank you, Your Honor. Officer, did you conduct any forensic analysis on the items recovered?
Officer Martinez: Yes, we processed the bag and found Mr. Thompson’s fingerprints on both the bag and the jewelry inside.
Defendant: Officer, could these fingerprints have been placed on the items prior to the alleged robbery?
Officer Martinez: That is possible, but highly unlikely given the timeline and circumstances.
Judge: I’m finding in favor of the plaintiff in the amount of $1,800 in rent as well as the car damage. I don’t see any evidence anywhere that she’s supposed to pay utilities separately. That total is $2,467.12. Thank you.

Court Clerk: All parties, please raise your right hand. Be seated. Come to order, please.
Judge: Maryann Gambella?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: You are suing Yolette Cherubin Hy for $5,000 in personal injury that you say you have undergone as a result of her negligence in maintaining a sidewalk?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay. What happened?
Plaintiff: Well, um, it was actually last August, August 12th. It happened to be my birthday. I was out for dessert with my husband, my son, and his girlfriend in the next town from where I live. And after we had dessert, I decided that I wanted to walk home to burn off the calories from dessert.
Judge: So alone?
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: On your birthday? You couldn't get any of these guys to walk back with you?
Plaintiff: I can't believe I wanted to walk then.
Judge: I know. Well, they need to want to walk too.
Plaintiff: Not so much into their weight as I am.
Judge: Okay, so you decided to walk home.
Plaintiff: And, um, must have been about maybe four blocks from where the ice cream parlor was that I came upon the sidewalk.
Judge: Was it day or night?
Plaintiff: It was around 7:00 at night.
Judge: Was it light or dark?
Plaintiff: It was light.
Judge: And the walk was going to be how far?
Plaintiff: I would say approximately about a mile and a half.
Judge: Great.
Plaintiff: So I was walking along, and I came to this part of the sidewalk. I could see that it was rough. As I walked over it, my foot twisted, and then I started to fall, but my right foot caught into a piece of concrete, and I went down.
I was knocked unconscious, and the next thing I knew, there was a young man standing over me saying, "Lady, lady, you all right?" You know, it's so funny because when my foot caught, it was like everything went in slow motion. I knew I was going down. I knew it was going to happen. But I didn't realize that I had actually passed out.
So there was this young fellow—I guess he was coming home from work. He said, "Don't worry, I called the police. I called an ambulance."
Judge: Come on up, husband. How much time had passed when you got that phone call? Were you already expecting her?
Plaintiff: ’s Husband: Maybe only about 15 minutes.
Judge: All right, so you get the phone call from him, and he says your wife had a fall?
Plaintiff: ’s Husband: Right. He said, "Well, there's been an accident." I said to him, "Is she okay?" He said, "Oh yeah, yeah. She was unconscious, but she looks like she slipped on the sidewalk. There's police here now, and the ambulance will be here any minute." And I was there in like five minutes.
Judge: Okay. So what were your injuries? You ended up having to have dental surgery?
Plaintiff: So when I got to the hospital, I knew that I cut my forehead open. They told me they would have to put in stitches, internally and externally. It came to about 35 stitches.
Judge: 35 stitches? Wow.
Plaintiff: Yes. And then my husband said to me, "Do you know that you chipped three teeth?" The minute I heard that, I said, "It's one thing when I have 35 interior and exterior stitches on my head, but chipping my teeth? No, I can't go out looking like that."
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: Well, I paid a fortune for these teeth—they're all veneers. So I was like—
Judge: So you had to go in for dental surgery?
Plaintiff: Yes, I did. I went to the dentist a few days later, and it was $3,000 to repair those three teeth—$1,000 a tooth.
I also thought that I had broken my leg, so they x-rayed my leg. They said it wasn’t broken but recommended that I go to an orthopedic doctor, which I did later. Then he sent me to physical therapy. It just became a whole fiasco.
Judge: What do you do for a living?
Plaintiff: Oh, I’m a retired RN.
Judge: Then why are you suing for $1,800 for lost wages?
Plaintiff: After I retired, I had opened up a small delivery service company, and I'm paid as I work.
Judge: Do you have any proof that you would have made $200 per day and that you lost nine days?
Plaintiff: Well, I have my tax return—
Judge: What she does have is—hold on.
Plaintiff: ’s Husband: The company she works for as an independent contractor wrote a letter saying she didn't show up.
Judge: Let me have that. Let me talk to you. When did you first learn that all this had happened?
Defendant: The house is a rental. A two-family rental.
Judge: Do you own it?
Defendant: I own the house, but I don’t live there.
Judge: Right. So you rent it out?
Defendant: The tenant moved out. It was when people were cleaning the house for me after the renters moved out that they found letters. A lawyer had sent me letters, and in one of them, it actually stated that she broke her hip.
Judge: Did you break your hip?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: Why is your lawyer sending letters saying you broke your hip?
Plaintiff: I don’t know. I only hired the lawyer to find her.

Judge: Douglas, Allison Raa, you are suing your former hairdresser, Vincent Maresi, for $5,000, the cost of hair extensions, color treatment, a new haircut, and pain and suffering, correct?
Plaintiff: Yes, because according to you, he not only ruined your hair but deliberately ruined it.
Judge: And you have a counterclaim against her for $5,000 for saying such a thing?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Okay, let's hear it.
Plaintiff: Well, I've been going to Vinnie for, I want to say, two or three years, um, faithfully, and I was trying to get a hold of him at a certain point to do my hair, and I couldn’t, so I went somewhere else to get my hair done. And then the color didn’t match, it wasn’t damaged, it was just color that needed to be done. So I went and I scheduled an appointment with Vinnie to correct it. And he decided to do an ombre on my hair, which an ombre is, um, something that basically looks like it’s dark all on the top and then lightens up at the bottom, and um, he started to do the ombre, left the decolorizer on my hair.
Defendant: What’s a decolorizer?
Plaintiff: It’s like bleach, you know, to lighten like if you’re to get a highlight.
Plaintiff: And, um, left it on there for about two hours. By the time I got to the sink, it was just completely—just coming, coming out.
Judge: Your hair was falling out right there?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Plaintiff: Okay, my hair was coming out at the sink, and, um, then they rinsed me, and they asked me, did I want a blowout, and I said, and I know he could specifically remember, I said no, I held my hand up, was a no blowout for me because I knew once the heat went on my hair, it was going to just melt off. So I paid and ran out of there, and I went home and washed it out, dried it, and it was just breaking, coming off all over my floor.
Judge: A picture of it?
Plaintiff: Oh yeah, I have pictures of everything.
Let me see all the pictures here. The pictures. Okay, in what order is this?
Plaintiff: I wrote on the bottom, this is prior to going to the salon. That was right before, but if you look at the other pictures, you’ll see like day... you’ll see it after, blow-drying the first time, second blow-dry.
Judge: Why is your hair so dark here, and it was so light here?
Plaintiff: That's what he put on my hair was the blonde. That was what was breaking. So what happened was I ended up having to go and pay a fortune to get it completely corrected and get extensions, and get treatments, and get all my hair cut off, and I had to pay all this money to do this when he, I feel like he intentionally did it. Because on Facebook, I was having a conversation with one of my friends, and she asked me how my hair came out, and I said that, well, being that half of it is on my bathroom floor right now, I would say not so good.
Defendant: Then you pipe in and say, "Hey, you stray, you pay. At least we got all the crap hair off. I saved you a haircut."
Plaintiff: Why would you say that?
Defendant: Can I just, um, backtrack? Yeah. Can I just tell you a little bit about myself? A second-generation hairdresser for 20 years. I’ve been an educator for a major company for the first 10 years of my career. Um, I take my job very seriously, and I was looking through my phone and she made these comments at the end of the night, and it took me by surprise, and it got me, uh, mad, because I felt that’s why she’s saying, “Oh my God, he did this on purpose because I went to someone else.” So I said those two comments to her, “You stray, you pay.” I mean, that’s a pretty insensitive thing for you to say to a customer who’s been your customer for years when she’s upset because her hair’s falling out.
Defendant: I inboxed her right after that and apologized, and I said, "Let me see how you inbox her right after that and apologize. I need to come ASAP."
Plaintiff: Okay, this is before the appointment. I started filming next season. What do you film on?
Defendant: I was, at the time, filming for Jersey Licious.
Plaintiff: Do you have a role in Jersey Licious?
Defendant: Yes, a short role.
Plaintiff: A role, nonetheless.
Defendant: Okay, you say, "Hey, you stray, you pay. At least we got all the crap hair off. I saved you a haircut." And she responds, “You saved me a haircut? Really?”
Defendant: So you intentionally did this?
Plaintiff: And you answer, "Really?"
Defendant: That’s why I inboxed her right after that. And then you inbox her, “Would I do that to you?”
Plaintiff: I know, no one knows who you are talking about, but it bothers me when you bash me like that. I’m sorry that happened. Would I do that to your hair on purpose? No. That was the only way for that black hair to... you will have to stay dark, then have it straight up. That was the only way I couldn’t get the black hair lighter. If we couldn’t do it that way, then she’ll have to stay darker if she can’t be blonde.
Defendant: Oh, then you would have had to stay dark.
Plaintiff: You know, this is the second time I corrected her hair from going to someone. I always say you stray, you pay when you go to another hairdresser. You pay for the integrity of your hair sometimes when you don’t know what they’re doing to it.
Plaintiff: Yeah, but the one who did something to it was you in this one, right?
Defendant: If her hair fell out—have you seen the pictures?
Plaintiff: Uh, no. I asked her to come to, I—you know, she never came to the salon to show me, and my owner texted me and asked me if we can meet and talk, and I said, "Meet and talk about what?" You reimbursing me for all the money that I’ve spent having to correct my hair. And then he never responded.
Plaintiff: Okay, I have those texts if you want to see them.
Judge: Yeah, I’d like to see them. And then can you take a look at the pictures?
Defendant: Never saw any of these.
Judge: Okay, I know, but now you’re seeing them. So what do you have to say about them?
Defendant: I can’t say that’s my work because she never came back to the salon to show me. Nobody ever asked me to come.
Judge: Okay, I understand that she never came back to the salon. However, we have pictures of the hair falling out. Right? That’s what those are. It’s not like she decided to pin it on you, but it was somebody else who broke the hair. It was after your visit. So now what?
Defendant: Well, she is a hairdresser, so I do not know what she did at home to damage her hair. And when she says "intentionally," she’s almost accusing me of saying when she was in my chair, I intentionally damaged it.
Judge: Well, the reason why she thinks that you intentionally damaged it is because you are a jerk on the Facebook page. She never used the word "intentional" till you emailed her your nasty comment. Then she said, “What are you talking about? Was this intentional?” That’s when she uses it. Because it sounded like maybe it was when you talked that way. Do you understand?
Defendant: Totally.
Judge: Welcome back to The People’s Court. Harvey Levin here. So the question is, "What does 'You stray, you pay' mean?" Does that mean that he tried to screw her up for going to somebody else? Or does it mean you go to somebody else, and they’re going to do a bad job?
Defendant: You go to somebody else, and they’re going to do a bad job. You go to somebody else, you pay for it.
Judge: I mean, that’s what you get. You should stick with the regulars.
Plaintiff: Okay, but so you think you're saying what he said was innocent?
Judge: You think it was innocent?
Defendant: Uh, I don’t think it was innocent.
Plaintiff: You think "you stray, you pay" means, "I’m going to screw you up"?
Defendant: Exactly. You knew it.
Judge: You’re going to be the tiebreaker.
Defendant: Hey, I think you—you—you screwed up. You’re going to pay me because you went to the wrong guy. You should have stayed with me.
Judge: That’s what I think.
Defendant: You think you screwed her up?
Judge: Um, I don’t think he screwed her up. I think he, uh, made her—she should make her pay.
Judge: Okay, going inside the courtroom.
Defendant: He did respond: "I’m truly sorry that happened. I really am. I wish you would have come to the salon the day after so me and Carol could have talked to you. I know you a long time. You said on Facebook you intentionally did it, Vin."
Judge: See, that’s the problem. You know, there’s a lot of business is just human emotion. You know, if somebody talked to you like that and said if a doctor said something like that to you, would you go back to visit that doctor?
Defendant: You would never go back to—you’d have like a total bad vibe about it. I’m only human and sometimes I understand. Sometimes it takes that responsibility by, but I guarantee all my work and my shop backs up my clients and backs me up. You said on Facebook you intentionally did it, Vin.
Plaintiff: She says, "Let me know if you want to reimburse me for what I paid at the salon and what I’ve paid to fix it. If not, I’ll just see you 11:21."
Judge: Will let the judge decide. This is ridiculous. A friend wouldn’t do what you did, Vinnie. You have no clue how bad my hair is now. I had to get it all cut off and get 250 strands of mic crochet at $10 a blanking strand, plus dying it, plus treatments. I trusted you. This is so blank, Vin.
Defendant: Let me see the receipts from the next place.
Plaintiff: All the receipts for what you're suing for.
[Plaintiff presents receipts and bank statements.]
Judge: Okay, I think everything else is actually in those pictures too. She needs to see that you didn’t want to just go short.
Defendant: No, why not?
Plaintiff: No, I don’t look good with short hair at all.
Judge: What’s the pain and suffering?
Plaintiff: Pain and suffering to me is like, imagine you at a salon as a woman, and then...
Judge: Hold on, I’m going to imagine myself as a woman. Hold on.
Plaintiff: Oh my God, you know what I mean?
Judge: I do know what you mean. I think that that kind of silly talk is the reason why then it sounds disingenuous when we stand up and demand equal pay.
Plaintiff: Okay, bad hair.
Judge: Okay, bad hair day. Everybody gets it.
Plaintiff: Okay, Butch up.
Judge: Jeez.
Plaintiff: Can I say something?
Judge: No, you cannot, and if I were you, I wouldn’t.
Judge: Okay, because I’m about to rule and you’re about to get a lot of this back.
Defendant: Let us talk, let her talk.
Plaintiff: Stop!
Judge: So don’t make me angry. Okay, so I’m not ordering the almost $2,000 in pain and suffering. I’m not ordering the money back that you paid the defendant. I am going to order the defendant to pay that which you had to pay in order to get the benefit of your bargain, which was the good job. And I’m ordering him to pay you $2,855.
Judge: And let me give you a piece of advice. When my 14-year-old reflexively writes something on Facebook, I take her computer away. Okay? Somebody in your life needs to take your computer away. So you have to choose: Are you going to be a 14-year-old or are you going to be a businessman? Good luck. Oh, on your counterclaim for $5,000, in case it wasn’t obvious...

Judge: Rudolph Luk, you are suing Charles Ludi for $4,176.33 in damages that you say you have sustained because of an accident that you say was his fault. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: Um, I was out at the Riverhead Raceway on a Saturday night, and we left there about 10:00. I got on the Long Island Expressway, put the cruise control on. All of a sudden, I... oh crap, there's a deer, and I start slowing down, and it turned out to be a horse. And there were two horses in the middle of... what kind of... they were right on the Long Island Expressway! Oh my God! They were on the shoulder, so I start slowing down, and the horses start moving. I'm like, "He's not going to run in front of me," and then the horse ran in front of me, and I hit him. I slammed on the brakes. I skidded the horse. I must have knocked him off his legs. He landed on my hood, rolled over the windshield, over the roof of the car, and then got up and walked away. Not a scratch on him. Not... well, maybe a little scratch, but not...
Judge: How’s the horse?
Plaintiff: He got a big smile. So I see the horse was fine. He’s living... he's living large, apparently. 'Cause the horse’s curfew is apparently, uh, a little better than my children’s.
Defendant: Tell me what’s going on. We were at the movies, my wife and I. We were coming back home on the expressway, the L, which is like 55 miles an hour. And more, you know, nobody does the speed limit. But anyway, we noticed the, um, after our exit was blocked. The whole expressway was blocked by police. So I said, "Oh my God, it must be a bad accident." Now, we get off the expressway, and we live not far from there, and we went home. And if you have horses or any type of livestock, you usually go check it when you leave the house or be gone for a while. Sure enough, I looked in the backyard, and the gate is open. The gate is open, the gate was like J open, not destroyed, but open, and yeah, it was... yes, but your... does your horse have a posable digits? Like, how did your horse open the gate? What kind of gate?
Defendant: Pretty... he's pretty... yeah. Well, how did it open the gate? I don’t know. Why do you have a gate open? He did, he did push it open. He pushed it open. It was bent, kind of. One of the walls were broken of the gate. I had gave it to the insurance company.
Judge: Oh, how’d that work out?
Defendant: They, um... well, we were going to settle it privately, and we couldn’t, uh, come to a price agreement. And we did then decide to, uh, go to the insurance company.
Judge: Uh-huh.
Defendant: The insurance company looked at my fence, the barn, all the facility, and checked everything out, and they decided it wasn’t my fault, and they denied his claim.
Judge: Whose fault is it?
Defendant: That’s what I said. They said it was the horse’s fault.
Judge: What insurance company do you have?
Defendant: State... no. State Farm did not do that. I have a letter from them saying, "Get out of here." Swear to God, there’s no such animal. Pardon the pun.
Judge: Give me that letter from State Farm.
Defendant: Either you possess a gate that contains an animal, or you do not possess a gate that contains an animal. And the proof is in the fact that the animals are cha-chying down the Long Island Expressway. I’m sorry, that’s quite, that’s just ridiculous, utterly ridiculous. I mean seriously, didn’t you think it was ridiculous? ‘Cause you’ve got a Cheshire cat grin on. I mean, you have to know this is ridiculous. It’s not going to be his fault. He’s the only guy I know whose fault it isn’t.
Judge: What do the police do? What do they say? How did that go?
Plaintiff: I was actually a little upset with the police, ‘cause the deputy, the sheriff, they closed the expressway. There had to be 15 or 20 of them there, and not one of them came over to see if I was okay. They all went over to the horse. Yeah, the poor horse, the pretty horse. Finally, I yelled across the highway, all six lanes, “Hey!” And one of them came over. I said, "What about me? Covered with broken glass?" And, "Oh, you okay?" "Well, yeah, thanks for asking." Well, we know you... you don’t know who was in these cars. There were like six people standing there. So I was a little upset that nobody came to check on me.
Judge: Let me see pictures of your car, and let me see the estimates for repair. You’re driving a 1995... what?
Plaintiff: Toyota Avalon.
Judge: Okay, why are you asking for $4,000? That’s my first question.
Plaintiff: Well, the book value of the car at the time was around $3,000, and then it’s been a storage... I had to pay... and the estimate was crazy. They were like $6,000 to repair.
Judge: Let me see proof that you, uh, that you paid for storage.
Plaintiff: Where was it stored?
Plaintiff: Uh, at the muffler shop. That’s where I had it towed. I usually take my vehicles there to get repaired.
Judge: Yeah, but you know, usually it takes a few days to figure out this is a total loss, which is what you’re suing for. So why would you have 66 days of storage?
Plaintiff: He wouldn't give me a receipt unless I paid him.
Judge: Okay, do you have proof that you paid it?
Plaintiff: Yeah, right there.
Judge: Why would you have it there 66 days? Why so long?
Plaintiff: Well, because I expected his insurance company to come give me my money, take the car away. Right? And then they denied it. And now what do I do? You know, I’m like, I can’t get rid of the car. Where do you...
Judge: You got that the Blue Book value is $3,000?
Plaintiff: I got that on, uh, Kelly Blue Book.
Judge: What model did you drive?
Plaintiff: Avalon.
Judge: What? Could you be more specific?
Plaintiff: Um... do you have the title to it?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: May I see it?
Plaintiff: ‘Cause the difference is that your Blue Book has an XLS. I don’t know if that’s on the title.
Plaintiff: Should be.
Judge: Oh my goodness.
Judge: The horse hit the windshield. He rolled over the... I hit him on the hood and he just rolled over the windshield halfway through, over the roof, and off the side. And why is it a total loss?
Plaintiff: Uh, because it’s like $6,000 to fix a $3,000 car.
Judge: Right, you’re on. May I say something?
Defendant: I live for the moment. So if a horse gets out of a gate somehow and the, um, the horse lands on, on the driver’s windshield, is the horse owner responsible for the broken windshield?
Plaintiff: I think you're probably, he’s probably responsible for that horse, uh, forever. I’d say, and I’d say you have... so whatever the horse does, the owner’s responsible. I’d say so.
Judge: Yeah, even if he doesn’t know how the gate opened, I think he’s probably responsible for the gate and the horse.
Plaintiff: What do you think?
Defendant: Uh, I think the insurance company has... the insurance company wouldn’t pay, though. That’s the, that’s the thing here. You get the final words.
Judge: I think that he’s definitely responsible for that horse and always will be. Fair enough.
Judge: Going inside the courtroom. Can I see those photographs?
Plaintiff: Yeah. And you, she can look at the photographs of the horse if she wants to.
Judge: Would you like to see the photos of the horse, Shar?
Plaintiff: Yes. Yes, I would. I want to see if the... I want to see the horse that did this damage to this car and walked away with like, uh, minor scratches.
Defendant: Very little, very little damage on him. Very little damage on the horse.
Judge: But there's going to be some damage to your pocket in a second, which is a good thing.
Judge: But it is a good thing. The thing is, I have to say, is it is a good thing, and frankly, after I rule and damage your pocket, you should go back to State Farm and ask them, "What are they thinking?" Because I’ve been a State Farm customer all my life, and I’m very surprised to have seen that, that letter. I don’t know how else you do the math if the gate has to be sturdy enough that a horse can’t go through it. If the horse did, by definition, then you didn’t contain your animal. I mean, that’s, it’s... you know?
Judge: I also say, uh, your honor, is that, um, this car was placed at his friend’s shop. It’s a friend of his, and when I went to look at the car, which was probably a couple days right after the accident, um, I was thinking about buying it and giving it to my daughter, fixing it up, because it didn’t look that bad. It needed a windshield, it needed a hood. And actually, a friend of his, who does body work and used to work there, came and gave you an estimate of the car and we... from $750 to $1,000 to fix the car hood, windshield, head.
Defendant: Did you have that in writing?
Plaintiff: No, I do not.
Defendant: Did they tell you that it would be a $1,000 to fix?
Plaintiff: Anybody can, can make a car drivable again, but that’s not what I had. I mean, the car has dents in the roof now and the doors, all of that wouldn’t have been fixed for $700,000.
Defendant: But did they tell you they would fix it for $1,000?
Plaintiff: No. You’re... now we’re done. I mean, I don’t, I don’t want to drive a half-crashed-up car, you know, with a blue fender and a green fender and a, and a purple hood. You know, for $700.
Judge: No, I, I mean, I think the $6,000 estimate is bloated. I do think that, but I also think that we're... it’s probably a total loss because I’m valuing the car less than he is. So, I find that you are at fault. I find in favor of the plaintiff for the following: the $150 tofi. Did your friend charge you for storage? The $990 because he says it was out of friends. Why would the friend have charged you $990? What kind of friend is that?
Plaintiff: Well, was somebody who came out on a Sunday, you know.
Judge: Do you have a canceled check?
Plaintiff: No, I paid cash.
Judge: I feel funny about that, I’ve got to tell you.
Judge: So I’m going to do what we call a little rough justice here. I am going to find in favor of the plaintiff. I’m going to limit his damages to what I think is reasonable, and what I think is reasonable is $2,700. Verdict for the plaintiff.

Judge: Owner, the defendant has filed a countersuit for $1,500 for a wrongful arrest and harassment. All parties, please raise your right hand. Be seated. Come to order, please. Litigants have been sworn in. Thank you, Douglas. Welcome in. Okay, everyone's asked not to mention company names or last names. Ron, you're suing Sierra for $5,000 that you say is owed to you because she extended a car rental and damaged the car?
Plaintiff: Yes ma'am, and put me in a position where I couldn't make money off the car.
Judge: You are counterclaiming against him $1,500 for harassment and wrongful arrest. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: I rented to her previously, some months earlier, and she...
Judge: What did you do for a living?
Plaintiff: I rent cars. I rent cars and I have a party bus as well.
Judge: Are they cars that you own?
Plaintiff: Yes ma'am.
Judge: Do you have a business doing this?
Plaintiff: Yes ma'am.
Judge: Do you have an office where you do it out of? Do you advertise, or do you just go word of mouth?
Plaintiff: Word of mouth, um, business cards, and I have...
Judge: How many cars do you own?
Plaintiff: Right now it's just two.
Judge: What about when this happened?
Plaintiff: When that happened, it was three.
Judge: Okay. She is someone who had rented from you before?
Plaintiff: Yes ma'am.
Judge: And how long were you renting to her for?
Plaintiff: The original date was for one day and she told me she was going to come back and pay me some more for... um... I think she said she wanted to keep it a little longer. I think a week maybe.
Judge: And after the day passed, what happened?
Plaintiff: Okay, now hold on. What was it you rented to her?
Plaintiff: A 2013 BMW 535.
Judge: And how much were you charging her?
Plaintiff: $80 a day.
Judge: How much?
Plaintiff: $80 a day.
Judge: Is that competitive? Is that a good rate, or is it high or low?
Plaintiff: It was a desperate situation. I needed a vehicle at the moment.
Judge: And you couldn't go to like Enterprise and rent a cheap car?
Plaintiff: No, because my name is actually messed up with Enterprise due to a situation with my sister not returning a vehicle. So, until I paid them, I exhausted all the other rental car places.
Judge: When you went to the gentleman who does it in the alley, is that it? Like, how does this work?
Plaintiff: Kind of, sort of. You could say it like that because actual Enterprise... I'm on the do-not-rent list until I pay them.
Judge: Do not rent list?
Plaintiff: Okay.
Judge: What about Hertz or Avis or National or Thrifty?
Plaintiff: No, because the credit card that I use with Enterprise is affiliated with that, so I have to clear up the money on the credit card, and they don’t accept... so he doesn’t take credit cards.
Judge: No?
Plaintiff: It’s just cash.
Judge: So, you just give him cash?
Plaintiff: How much?
Plaintiff: I gave him $250 as a deposit.
Judge: Okay. So, she gave you $250 plus $80?
Plaintiff: Yes ma’am.
Judge: Okay, and then what happens?

Judge: I -- I think that the key -- the key difference for False Claims Act purposes is that in the first scenario, even -- even if the money is only in the public fisc for a limited amount of time, that is still endangering the public fisc.
It is going into the public fisc, and the government is providing the funds out of the public -- of the public fisc.
And -- and the political branches, Congress and the FCC, in choosing this rather unique setup, this design, did that quite intentionally --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Didn't they do it --
Judge: --to protect this --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: -- intentionally to avoid the cost, the administrative cost, of it -- the government being the one -- I mean, what's so peculiar about your argument to me is that I would think good government would actually be trying to hold down costs.
We have this program we've created, and it seems far more efficient to have the telecom companies just put the money in directly than it does to say: Give the money to us, and then we will in cost --you know, incur the costs of rerouting it out to the fund.
Judge: And --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I don't understand why that's a problematic thing from your perspective.
Judge: Oh, we don't think it's a -- it's problematic.
We just think those choices, which, as -- as you --as you lay out, may have been for very sound reasons, those choices have consequences, and the consequence of that choice to insulate the public fisc and perhaps to gain some efficiency in doing it this way --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Means that it can --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: But, Ms. Ho --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: -- be defrauded from your --your perspective under the FCA? The FCA doesn't apply in that situation?
