Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Ms. Ho, can I ask you a question? It seems like your argument rests on the premise that only one person can provide, and I'm not really sure why that's true.
I was thinking of an example of -- think back to the days when proctors used to give out test booklets and pencils.
So let's say that I'm working as a proctor.
It would be perfectly natural for me to say to the students: I'm going to provide each of you with a pencil and a booklet.
Now I didn't go out and purchase those with my own money.
The school presumably provided them to me and then I provided them to the students.
I mean, I think one person doesn't have to provide.
I think we could say that the United States provided funds, for example, that it -- this hundred million dollars that it got through debt collection, et cetera, and then also say that the carriers provided money to the funds through their fees or that the carriers provided funds to the United States, which was a conduit.
I just don't understand.
I mean, are you assuming that "provides" has to be exclusive, that there's one ultimate provider?

JUDGE: This is the plaintiff Ena Puit. She says she moved out of an apartment she'd been renting from the defendants for close to four years, did a walkthrough with them, and is now having a devil of a time collecting all of her security. When she questioned the co-defendant, he said he had to check with his wife about things and would get back to her. Well, he never did, so she's suing for the remaining $1,367. She most definitely deserves it.
These are the defendants, John and Christy Schaer. They say there were considerable problems when they did the walkthrough with the plaintiff, and they were being nice, refunding more than half the lady’s security. The fact that she’s suing them here today is laughable because they spent more money than they withheld to get the place even rentable again. They’re accused of doing a lady wrong.
All parties please raise your right hand.
Welcome back to the People’s Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff rented from the defendant for four years but isn’t getting her security back and says it’s because the defendant is afraid of his wife. But the defendants say they were generous; the plaintiff caused lots of problems in the unit. It’s the case of “your whip, I’m stiffed.”
JUDGE: All right, Ena Puit, you are suing John and Christy Schaer for $1,367, a balance in a security deposit plus interest that you say they are wrongfully withholding. Yes? Talk to me.
PLAINTIFF: On August 3rd, I received my item listing and my return check from my security deposit.
JUDGE: Okay, how long had you lived there?
PLAINTIFF: Four years.
JUDGE: Since 20--? You always paid rent on time?
PLAINTIFF: Well, not always on time. I made arrangements with them, and they agreed to them.
JUDGE: Did you generally have a good relationship with them?
PLAINTIFF: Yes.
JUDGE: Okay, and when you left, why did you leave?
PLAINTIFF: I left because I had to downsize.
JUDGE: Okay, so you leave, you do a walkthrough.
PLAINTIFF: We did a walkthrough.
JUDGE: Any comments made during the walkthrough?
PLAINTIFF: Yes.
JUDGE: What were the comments?
PLAINTIFF: The comments were on three holes in the walls that were on the main level floor of the smaller room. There was a hole in the kitchen screen, and then on the second level, there was the doorjamb. My granddaughter got locked in there by mistake. We opened it up. I had a friend try to fix it, and I guess it wasn’t fixed.
JUDGE: Okay, so you point that out.
PLAINTIFF: We all pointed it out together. It was written down, and those were the only damages spoken on.
JUDGE: Who did the walkthrough? He did or she?
PLAINTIFF: John and Christy.
JUDGE: Okay, did they say anything else to you at that time?
PLAINTIFF: No.
JUDGE: All right, so you leave, and you're expecting your security deposit check. Did they say anything to you about the security deposit check on that date?
PLAINTIFF: During the walkthrough, they said that they will get back to me with the amount of the repairs.
JUDGE: Okay, and so when they got back to you, how did they get back to you?
PLAINTIFF: In writing, it was 30 days later. I received the letter and the check on August 3rd.
JUDGE: Okay, I need to see the letter and the check. Okay, so go on.
PLAINTIFF: So when I received the letter and the check, immediately I called John on the phone. I shared with John on that day that I received the check and the letter, and I was in total disbelief and I was very upset and frustrated. I asked him to explain the contents of the letter. Why was it excessive? Because in that letter, it said they were going to charge from my security deposit: excessive cleaning, $300; damage to the upper bedroom doorframe, $150; damage to the walls, no figure; excessive wear carpet, $500; screen replacement, $50. That was a total of $1,000. So they sent me a check for $1,025.
JUDGE: Correct.
PLAINTIFF: Okay, and they were keeping $1,000 for damages.
JUDGE: Correct.
PLAINTIFF: Okay, you felt that was excessive. So what did you do?
PLAINTIFF: Well, when I called him, I asked him, "Could he explain to me what was the excessive cleaning and what was the excessive wear and tear on the carpet?" He told me, "Well, Ena, Christy sent that out to you. I need to talk to her." And at that time, he told me, "Ena, I'm putting together papers for you, and you'll get them." I haven't heard from John since August 27th.
JUDGE: Okay, what's going on, folks?
DEFENDANT (John): Well, I don't disagree with a lot that Ena said. She was a tenant of ours for four years. She made several late payments, but we worked it out. In general, she was a good tenant, but she left the place a mess. We spent in excess of $9,000 fixing and repairing the place after she left.
JUDGE: Okay, and uh, what was wrong with it?
DEFENDANT (John): The carpets were ruined. We had broken doorframes, broken doors, holes in the walls. Appliances were left filthy, bathtubs left filthy, walls with grease all over them, holes in the kitchen floor. She had a leak. I don’t know what she did with a refrigerator, but ice jammed up, and the ice just kept coming down and ruined a ceiling in the apartment below.
JUDGE: Wait, was it your refrigerator?
DEFENDANT (John): Yes, it was our refrigerator.
JUDGE: So what would she do?
DEFENDANT (John): There was still some obligation to let us know. Somehow, the ice jammed up, and ice cubes kept coming out of the refrigerator.
JUDGE: Right, but ice cubes don’t melt in a couple of seconds. What did the woman downstairs say?
DEFENDANT (John): She called us and said her ceiling was falling in.
JUDGE: Let me see the pictures of everything that you’re asking for.
[Judge examines pictures.]
JUDGE: All right, let’s see here. I see the holes in the wall, the damage to the doorframe. The carpet does look worn, but that could be regular wear and tear after four years. The cleaning charges—hmm. This kitchen doesn’t look spotless, but $300 is steep. Some of these damages make sense, but I believe the cleaning charge is excessive.
JUDGE: The only place I think they got a little greedy was on the cleaning. I'm going to order them to return $150 to you that I believe was wrongfully withheld, and based on the double damages clause in New Jersey, that’s $300. Verdict for the plaintiff. That’s my judgment. Good luck.

Then I think... 'cause you had to pay, if you didn’t pay, you don’t get it. So I went. So, he did the root canal. We went through that. He says, "Oh, you did good." I said, "Thank you, I did." My other root canal.
Plaintiff: Okay, so now this... I know how it happened, because he, the lab man told me, and this... he used the old crown. I don’t know what you're talking about.
Plaintiff: And the thing, the crown that came out of my mouth...

Plaintiff: Yeah, okay. Hold on.

It was April 25th, they called me and said they had a problem with the toilet flushing properly. I went over, inspected it, and the main line was clogged up. Then they called me again, very irate, in the middle of the night stating that the commode was backed up again. It was overflowing. The next morning, I cleared the line again with another snake.

Plaintiff: The kitchen drawer and the repairs underneath the sink.
Plaintiff: You know, in that lease. And we’re going to talk about that in a second because I think that might end up being a surprise.
Plaintiff: What you agreed to under $75, they—
Judge: They stomach?
Plaintiff: They agreed to that under $75.
Judge: How did they end up being $65 when all you did was put out a few mouse traps?
Plaintiff: Well, it was $50 for the service call from myself.
Judge: Says who?
Plaintiff: Well, in the lease, it states that if they contact me to provide services for them, they will pay a $50 service fee plus the cost of materials.
Judge: The strange part is that you didn’t charge that.
Plaintiff: How much was the rental of the snake?
Plaintiff: The rental of the snake was $14.91.
Judge: Oh, you did charge it on the other ones?
Plaintiff: Yes, $50 for the service call and then the cost of the snake.

Judge: Right?
Judge: The drawers are falling apart, there’s mice, plumbing problems. The guy comes and fixes it himself. Doesn’t hire a licensed contractor, does it himself, and looks at you and says, ‘By the way, you owe me $65 for today.’
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And you’re looking at him like, ‘What was this?’
Defendant: We didn’t know about these charges that he was suing us for. The itemized ones until this week. This is the first time we heard about the $50 service fee.

Defendant: I thought it was that he had submitted the bill to you.

Plaintiff: I did. I provided them with it on May 22nd.

Judge: Well, two points. So, first, even the federal regulations prohibit even sleeping. They don't even require any materials, including but --but not necessary under the federal regulation. So this is conduct that is understood by jurisdictions nationwide and even the federal government to be conduct that is prohibited, and so I want to make that point.
Defense Lawyer: See, I'll --
Judge: The second point --
Defense Lawyer: -- I'll tell you the truth, Ms. Capoor. I think that this is -- this is a super-hard policy problem for all municipalities. And if you were to come in here and you were to say, you know, we need certain protections to keep our streets safe and we can't have, you know, people sleeping anyplace that they want and we can't have, you know, tent cities cropping up, I mean, that would create one set of issues. But your ordinance goes way beyond that.
Your ordinance says as to a person -- and I understand that you think it's generally applicable, but we only come up with this problem for a person who is homeless, who has the status of homelessness, who has no other place to sleep, and your statute says that person cannot take himself and himself only and, you know, can't take a blanket and sleep someplace without it being a crime.
And -- and -- and that's, you know -- well, it just seems like Robinson. It seems like you're criminalizing a status.
Defense Lawyer: Well, it is not. And we agree with you that this is a very difficult policy question, and that's exactly --
Judge: But that -- it isn’t.
Defense Lawyer: -- why the Eighth Amendment --
Judge: Can you answer why?
Judge: Can I --
Judge: Why is it not? Just -- I mean, the issue has been laid out clearly. You're making a distinction between status and conduct. Okay. We see that. And you keep saying this is conduct. Can you explain why?
Defense Lawyer: The actus reus element, that's exactly what was missing in Robinson and that's what we have here. And that's why that law was so unique. It's a very peculiar --
Judge: So it seems to me that Robinson actually hurts you and not helps you in the following sense. You know, it seems both cruel and unusual to punish people for acts that constitute basic human needs.
So, here, unlike in Robinson, where, you know, you had at least the sort of disease state, drugs and -- and -- and the like, and potentially culpable acts that relate to that disease state, here, we're talking about sleeping that is universal, that is a basic function.
And so I guess what I don't understand is in this circumstance why that particular state is being considered conduct for the purpose of punishment.
Defense Lawyer: Well, I think that just illustrates the line-drawing problems because, if you look at biological necessities and what a person needs to do, you know, the Ninth Circuit's decisions in this area would allow --
Judge: Can I give you a hypothetical?
Defense Lawyer: Yes. Thank you.
Judge: Okay. So suppose the relevant ordinance prohibited eating on public property rather than sleeping or camping. We're talking about eating.
And the city, for very, you know, rational reasons, has determined that when people eat outdoors, it creates problems with trash and rodents and the like, and so it bans eating in public places and it punishes violators.
Now, just as here, that seems generally fine because most people have restaurants that they can go to, most people have houses that they can eat in.
But some people don't have that option. They have to eat in public because they're unhoused and they can't afford to go to a restaurant.
So is -- is your argument the same result, no Eighth Amendment problem, no problem with the city banning eating in public, even though that's a human necessity that everyone engages in, and, really, what's happening is you're only punishing certain people who can't afford to do it privately?
Defense Lawyer: Well, it sounds like -- I take for a moment that you're not saying the law -- that the law draws lines on any sort of irrational basis or any equal protection issue --
Judge: No. The city has a rational basis.
Defense Lawyer: -- and --
Judge: When people eat in public --
Defense Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: -- there is trash, there are rodents, there are problems. So the city says what we're going to do is we're going to say no eating in public.
What I'm concerned about from your argument is the suggestion -- you know, you call it conduct, I appreciate that, but what we have happening in operation is that people who are able to afford doing this thing that's a basic human need privately are okay. They're not punished for it.
But people who don't have any other option or opportunity except for to do it in public are the ones who are being targeted by this statute.
Defense Lawyer: So two responses. First, I think the Eighth Amendment is the wrong way to look at it. Someone might have a due process challenge to a law like that if there is a deeply entrenched liberty interest.
Judge: But punishment is happening. In my hypothetical, people are going to jail because they're eating in public.
Defense Lawyer: So, in that case --
Judge: Why is the Eighth Amendment not implicated?
Defense Lawyer: -- in that case, you would have a defense under Oregon law, for example, a necessity defense.
Judge: Counsel, on -- on -- on --
Defense Lawyer: And I want to get to that on the camping.
Judge: Counsel, I'm sorry to interrupt.
Defense Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: But, on that point, I think we're having some debate about where to lodge the defense, whether it's under the Eighth Amendment or under the Fourteenth Amendment.
But do you concede that there are instances in which a necessity defense, long recognized at common law, would apply to eating in public, sleeping in public, or other things like that?
Defense Lawyer: Yes, I agree. And, actually, here, in the case of camping, Oregon law recognizes a necessity defense, so as a matter of state law and policy -- and, again, that goes to the difficult policy questions -- that's why states are able to address the needs of what this issue raises.
And so, for something under Oregon State law, a person could raise that defense under the necessity defense, and then, if that's not enough, if they believe that that's not broad enough somehow --
Judge: And you're saying --
Defense Lawyer: -- they can argue due process.
Judge: -- Oregon law has that defense --
Defense Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: -- already built into it?
Defense Lawyer: That's correct.
Judge: All right. Thank you.
Judge: Let me ask you about Oregon law, because one sort of threshold concern that I have about this case is I understand that Oregon has enacted a statute, a new statute, that seems to address this very issue, so I'm trying to understand why this is -- is still a live case.
As I read the new law, it essentially codifies Martin's rule, that it says something about all regulations of this nature have to be objectively reasonable as to time, place, and manner with regard to -- with regards to people experiencing homelessness.
So it seems like the state has already precluded Grants Pass from doing the sort of thing it's doing here, so why do we need to weigh in on that?
Defense Lawyer: Well, no, it hasn't. So, first, both sides agree that this case is not moot. There is no state law challenge in this case.

Judge: All parties please raise your ready you see the come please. Okay Michael rthor, yes you are suing Derek ogrady for $2,400 four days of work that you say he owes you for Bobcat services at a construction site. What happened?

I uh put an end on Craigslist later in the summer to uh dig up some extra money for myself and my family and I have the ad right here it says Bobcat operator um skid year skilled operator for Bobcat pire. Okay so I got many calls Derek was one of them and he called me on a Thursday I believe he's he asked me if I could be out there on a Monday to do some work which is about 50 miles from my home and I said no problem so I loaded everything up I get to the first job site this is a bobcat if you don't know what it is.

Plaintiff: Um so I get that a first job site in Sound Beach and he shows me uh walks me around his property and shows me what to do there's about 100 yards of soil. We call it Phill in the industry that was ripped out and piled on this guy's property that had to be moved and put in dumpsters.

Judge: Okay. Have you ever had body damage on your car that you’ve had to pay for?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Okay. So, but the damage was a lot more?
Defendant: How much damage was it?
Judge: Yeah.
Defendant: It was the whole engine. The whole front end was just—
Judge: So it was a big accident?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Was it yours? Was it your accident?
Defendant: It was not considered my fault.
Judge: No? How?
Defendant: Not considered my fault.
Judge: What do you mean?
Defendant: The insurance company didn’t consider it my fault because the lady was not considered believable.
Judge: My point originally—I started off asking that to see if you understood how expensive bodywork is. But then I found out about this whole other accident.
Defendant: Mm-hmm.
Judge: But in any event, do you have pictures of the damage?
Plaintiff: I don’t.
Judge: You already got it fixed and you don’t have pictures to show me?
Plaintiff: The body shop has the pictures.
Judge: Well, okay, let’s think this through a moment. Who are the pictures for? I dare say they’re for me. This is exactly what you took the pictures for. This lady in the black dress. What are they doing—warming someone’s manila folder at a body shop? Are you crazy? Come on! Can a Toyota Corolla bumper cost $1,400?
Defendant: Yeah. That could definitely happen.
Judge: Yeah.
Defendant: That honestly can.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: I did bring the invoices, which detail all the work.
Judge: Show me the invoice.
Plaintiff: Have you taken your car there for work before?
Defendant: My daughter has.
Judge: Replace the left fender… Here’s a credit card receipt. And it costs $1,405, which is what you’re suing for. Now, at some point, you guys had negotiated where you were going to pay how much?
Defendant: He threw me the estimate of $1,800, so I was trying to figure out if I could maybe do something cheaper because I’m only 19 and—
Judge: Because you’re only what?
Defendant: I’m only 19 years old and I can only make so much money, Your Honor.
Judge: Okay, so he takes it there, and it’s $1,405 instead of—where did the figure $1,800 come from?
Defendant: It was the original estimate.
Judge: Which was from who?
Defendant: The same body shop.
Judge: And then they decided they were able to do it for $1,405?
Defendant: Right. He was able to mix the paint in a cheap way.
Judge: So then you tell him, "Good news, it’s $1,405," and then he says what?
Plaintiff: He said "okay," basically, in a text.
Judge: Okay. You tell him, "I’ll tell you what, if you pay upfront, I’ll give you a discount, you just pay me $1,100, I’ll foot the rest." And you say?
Defendant: I can’t do that. I have to go with our original plan.
Judge: Which was?
Defendant: $300 a month.
Judge: And then what happened?
Defendant: I was working overtime.
Judge: Well, now you’re paying the full $1,405. Verdict for the plaintiff.
Manipulation Techniques: evasion, minimization, emotional appeal, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Plaintiff: Yes, Your Honor, I’m suing my former friend, Canel Alberino, for $74 in loans, a parking ticket, and clothing items that she was supposed to repay for throughout the course of our friendship, but she hasn’t.
Judge: What’s going on?
Plaintiff: I’ve known the defendant since about third grade. We’ve been best friends, inseparable for a long time. About 2008-2009, we were both unemployed, going through financial issues. Hers were a bit more dire than mine because she had her apartment and her car. Me, I was taken care of by my daughter’s father, so I didn’t really want for anything. I had an account with Wachovia years ago when I was working downtown, and they closed my account due to an overdraw fee that they said I owed. But they waited a month and a half to cash a check that I had given them a while ago for rent. They waited until multiple things hit the account, so the account would go over. I disputed it, I paid it, but it took like a year and a half for the bank to recognize that I paid the red flag fee before they said I could open my own account.
Are you trying to explain to me why you take money and put it in her account?
Plaintiff: Yes, okay, you didn’t have creditors chasing you or a judgment against you or the tax man looking for you?
Defendant: No, it was just the bank issue. I asked her, since I had gotten my school loan, because she put the money in her account since I couldn’t open my own. I gave her $790 to put in the account, and it was just left in the account. She ran into some complications where she wasn’t able to pay all of her rent and, I think, a car note. She had a notice on her door letting her know that she had to pay the rent by a certain date. She called me, and we usually spoke on the phone every day. She let me know the issue, and I told her, “Well, there’s money in your account. I’m not using it right now. It’s not a dire need for me to have it right now. It’s not anything for me to loan it to you right now. Do whatever you need to do with it. Let me know afterward what you spent, and when you get back on your feet, that’s when you’ll pay me back.” I’m not going to—.
Judge: So how much did she take, according to you?
Plaintiff: She said that she took $550. That was to pay off whatever she needed to pay— I don’t know if it was for just rent or for both rent and car. I’m not sure.
Judge: At some point, you guys get into a rocky patch and you end up parting ways. Why was that?
Plaintiff: We went to South Beach with a group of girls. We all went to South Beach. That summer, me and her had a falling out over an ordeal with her and her sister that I didn’t primarily agree with, and she thought that I should be on her side and be mad at her sister as well. But I’m not a person of that nature.
Judge: But you’d been friends since third grade, and a disagreement on vacation in South Beach one time—it was just other events leading up to that?
Plaintiff: Yes, it was other events leading up to that, and that was probably the breaking point, I should say. South Beach was the kicker in, I guess, both of our lives for us to part ways.
Judge: You’re also suing her for a jacket and some shoes. What’s that about?
Plaintiff: The jacket was trivial, but the shoes as well. There was a principle behind it. I had stuff at her house. Me and her go shopping at Mandy’s every now and again. We had stuff at her house. I had stuff that I wanted to take back, but I didn’t have the opportunity to take it back at that point in time. During that time, we had a really big flood. It rained really badly, and it flooded in front of her building.
Judge: Wait a second. When was this in relation to when you stopped talking to each other?
Plaintiff: This was prior to us stopping speaking. The jacket was probably about 6 months.
Judge: And what about the shoes?
Plaintiff: The shoes were $9 shoes.
Judge: Are they whole shoes?
Plaintiff: No, okay, it was a shoe that I got. The shoes were left at her house because they were too big for me. I was going to go to the store and get store credit. I got them on sale, and she wore them. She said they fit her size.
Judge: You didn’t need them?
Plaintiff: They were only $9, but it’s the principle behind it. If you leave something at my house…
Judge: But you stayed friends after that?
Plaintiff: No, we didn’t stay friends after that.
Judge: You didn’t stop being friends over the $9 pair of shoes?
Plaintiff: No, we didn’t stop being friends over the $9 shoes. It was well after the $9 shoes. It was in the midst of us—it's just now that you’re suing, you’re going to bring up every little thing that happened between you?
Plaintiff: No, it’s not every little thing. This is in the context of the lawsuit.
Judge: Tell me about the parking ticket.
Plaintiff: The parking ticket was for her school. We went to her school, and we were both upstairs while she was talking to her advisor. I went downstairs, and the guy had already issued the ticket. He said he couldn’t give it back.
Judge: When did that happen?
Plaintiff: That happened in the summer.
Judge: What summer?
Plaintiff: Summer of 2008.
Judge: You’ve known each other since third grade, and now you’re locked in the most trivial legal battle. The plaintiff is suing over a $9 pair of shoes in addition to other stuff. There’s got to be more to this. Let’s listen to what’s going on.
Judge: She says that you had money in your account, you knew it was her money, you used it, and then you never repaid it. What’s going on?
Defendant: The plaintiff has never told me that I had to pay her money back. I never used what she’s claiming. It went from $550 to $700 now, which I have a text message to prove it. At the time, it wasn’t about my apartment or my car note. It was strictly about school and a cable bill. I told her, “My cable is going to be cut off.” I didn’t ask her for the money. I was telling her the situation, and she offered.
Judge: She said, “Don’t worry about it, you’re my best friend, I’ll give you the money.” Was this a loan or a gift?
Defendant: She said, “Do not worry about it, I’m making $45,000 a year now, I don’t need the money back.” So I said, “Fine.”
Judge: How much was it?
Defendant: It was $238 that I used for my cable bill.
Judge: So you have a statement from your uncle?
Defendant: Yes. He gave me $310 for tuition.
Judge: Is there a text between you two from back then that sheds some light on whether it was $550 or $238?
Defendant: I have a text message from her in March of this year.
Judge: What does it say?
Defendant: It doesn’t say how much money, but she said we could work out a payment plan for the $250.
Judge: And you were asking for more money?
Defendant: Yes, she texted back saying, “It’s funny how now you’re saying $300 when you owe me $550, but she’s suing me for $700 now.”
Judge: Do you have any evidence of the parking ticket being paid?
Defendant: Everything was taken out of that account for her.
Judge: Do you have any emails or texts from back then?
Defendant: I have a text message.
Judge: Let me see it.
Defendant: [Shows text message.]
Judge: These are not helpful.
Defendant: I have one more message.
Judge: Let me see it.
Defendant: I said, “I’ll send you monthly payments for the money you loaned me.”
Judge: Alright, I see.
Judge: The question before me is: Is this a loan or a gift, and how much is it?
Judge: The defendant is a woman of integrity. I’ll rule against her.
Manipulation Techniques: framing the narrative, minimization, emotional appeal

Dialogue:
Judge: Okay, Miss Kelly, you are suing Mr. Sterling for $1,556.16 for several of your items that you say you left at his place when you two broke up, and he has destroyed because you two broke up. Tell me what's going on.
Plaintiff: Um, I met Sterling on a dating site, mate1.com.
Judge: Which one?
Plaintiff: Mate1.com.
Judge: Got it. Alright, we'll get through this. Alright, mate1.com.
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay, I called him. Does it have some kind of specialty that… you know, I’ve never heard of that one actually. I work a lot, so I don’t get time to socialize. I’ve heard of dating sites, girl, I think the whole thing’s very efficient. I mean, I’ve been married for 20 years, but if I wasn’t, that’s how I would… it’s like internet shopping, it’s fine. But, um, I have a question for you though. Does it… it’s just anybody can be on it? Do you have to meet certain criteria to be on it?
Plaintiff: You do.
Judge: Yes. What's the criteria?
Plaintiff: A life.
Judge: Yes, alright. So, you meet him on what day?
Plaintiff: Valentine's Day.
Judge: Yes, we did. We met that Friday, we went out, we discussed what we wanted out of our life. He was real straightforward and honest, and I loved that about him. So, it was like a flick at first sight. Then he was telling me about his plans, his dreams and his aspirations. I really liked that because he was like so honest doing that, and I supported him right off the bat.
Judge: So, at some point, what? You supported him right off the bat? What does that mean?
Plaintiff: You liked him?
Plaintiff: Yes, I liked him.
Judge: Or did you mean actually financially supported him?
Plaintiff: No, I didn’t know what you were saying.
Judge: Okay, that's alright. So, you liked him and then what?
Plaintiff: We talked and he said he wanted me to move in.
Judge: A few weeks after you met him?
Plaintiff: Yes, I moved in.
Judge: Okay, you moved in a few weeks after you met. You move in, now you date and you date, a few years and it’s really working well, so you want to take it to the next level?
Plaintiff: No, it’s just… you move into what?
Plaintiff: To your place.
Judge: Yes, ma’am. Okay, do you have children?
Plaintiff: No, I don’t.
Judge: Okay, we got that going, right. So, because half the time when that happens, I have people moving their children into somebody who could be a pedophile, not to suggest that about you, but you know…
Plaintiff: Alright, so you at least it’s only you who you were putting at risk. So, you move in. How many weeks had you known him when you moved in?
Plaintiff: I think about 3 weeks, two and a half, 3 weeks.
Judge: Oh, you know all about a man by then?
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: Right, in 3 weeks you’re going to know everything there is to know. Yeah, how’d that work out for you?
Plaintiff: It was actually good for the first month, everything was alright, we understood each other, we talked about what and then after a month, it wasn’t so good.
Judge: Right, because it didn’t work out too well for you?
Plaintiff: No, wait and get to know somebody before you moved in with them?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay, have you done that before?
Plaintiff: No, it was actually my first time. I realized I was getting old and I really needed to have a relationship.
Judge: And that’s fine and I understand that, alright. I understand the loneliness, but if you want… sometimes you got to think long term. And if what you want is a relationship long term, then you want to get to know someone before you turn around and uproot your life, because it usually doesn’t work when it’s like this. You know, you have to invest a little time in a relationship that’s long-lasting, and if you can’t wait 3 weeks before you move in with somebody you don’t know each other, you’re going to fight non-stop and it’s going to turn out that you two maybe aren’t compatible to live with each other, which is in fact what you found out, because when you moved in, you two started fighting. And what are you fighting about?
Plaintiff: My job, my hours, that hour.
Judge: What do you do?
Plaintiff: Actually, I’m a sales rep and I’m a nursing assistant.
Judge: And you were working how many hours a day?
Plaintiff: Depending how I feel, I work like 14.
Judge: And how long was he working a day?
Plaintiff: Oh, he’s not working.
Judge: You don’t?
Defendant: Okay, alright, so you were lonely?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: You were still lonely?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: You moved the woman in, ma’am, you think your loneliness is over and then you're still lonely?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: Right, exactly. Exactly right, of course, if you knew her a little bit beforehand you’d say, well, maybe this isn’t the woman for me because we’re not compatible in that respect. And why aren’t you working?
Defendant: I’m retired. I’m a military veteran, PTSD.
Judge: Okay, you're going through some psychological issues?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: You’ve handed me a report by a psychiatrist?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: According to the report, you're suffering from post-traumatic stress disorder?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: And dissociative disorder?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: How long has it been? Where did you serve?
Defendant: I was on the USS Saratoga in 1986 when we bombed Libya and we had the hostages off of the… I mean, the kidnappers that kidnapped their cu’s, Lao, the big and they pushed the man off the… we got that, that was our F14s that made that 747 night, president.
Judge: What was your role?
Defendant: I’m a fireman. I help the jets take off.
Judge: Right. Okay, thank you for your service.
Defendant: Thank you, ma’am.
Judge: And I’m sorry that you're going through difficulty. All the more reason why you might want to know somebody before they move in. That’s when you break up. Whose idea was it?
Defendant: I take it, it was yours.
Plaintiff: Yes, it was.
Judge: Then when you leave, why do you leave stuff behind?
Plaintiff: Actually, I found the apartment and I came back the next day to get my stuff, and he didn’t want to let me in the apartment, he changed the locks, because you know, I was going to leave because I told…
Plaintiff: But you had taken some stuff, you hadn't taken anything?
Plaintiff: Not really, only what I needed for the day. I didn’t take anything.
Plaintiff: So you needed your stuff?
Plaintiff: So, I went over there in a cab. I went upstairs and knocked on his door. He didn’t want to let me in, and I said, “Okay, then put them outside. Pack up as much stuff as you can and put them outside.”
Judge: You're just shouting through the door?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: How are you communicating with him on the phone or no?
Plaintiff: I was talking through the door because he closed the door, he didn’t want to let me in.
Judge: Alright, and what does he say?
Plaintiff: Yes, I’ll pack it and put it out.
Judge: Yes, and he opened the door and he has the video camera on me and says, “I should just take up the stuff and leave.”
Plaintiff: So, I just took everything and I said, “There’s a lot more stuff I need.” I leave, and he says, “Just go, go.”
Judge: Do you have that video?
Plaintiff: No, ma’am, I didn’t bring it because I don’t think we were…
Judge: Was there stuff that you wouldn’t let her get?
Defendant: No.
Judge: It’s not what happened.
Judge: So, they move in a couple of weeks after meeting on a dating site. Is that ridiculous?
Defendant: You know what, the way communication is nowadays, I believe it is possible. 20 years ago, I saw my wife in the hallway of our high school, and I fell in love with her, and I’m really touched.
Judge: How soon later did you move in with her?
Defendant: I would say after we found out she was pregnant.
Judge: Okay, is it too long or ridiculous, a couple of weeks moving in?
Defendant: Yeah, I think it’s too soon to move in. Let’s get a different perspective, what do you think?
Plaintiff: I think it’s ridiculous, because you can actually… well, it can happen because people can meet each other and fall in love at first sight. Fair enough.
Judge: When Harry Met Sally. Going inside the courtroom, I went out. I go to see the psychiatrist once a week. When I went to go see the psychiatrist, when I came back, she was gone. You can visibly tell that she took her stuff out of the closet, stuff out of the drawers, so that was the first inkling you got that she was leaving?
Defendant: I was abandoned.
Defendant: The first thing I felt was abandoned.
Judge: Abandoned?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: So, I imploded, you know, it hurt because why would you leave like that? And so she wanted to call me back and talk it over. I didn’t want to talk about it. I didn’t want to… if you left, you should have taken everything.
Judge: Then what happened? What happened to the rest of her stuff that she didn’t take?
Defendant: Oh, the garbage got it.
Judge: The garbage got it?
Defendant: Got it.
Judge: How did the garbage get it?
Defendant: Did it come in and put its hands inside of the stuff and make it suck it into the garbage?
Defendant: You threw it out. I quickly put some stuff together and I videotaped, because I didn’t want her to say open the door and hit her or anything like that.
Judge: So, whatever she got and didn’t want to call back and say she missed stuff?
Defendant: Yes.
Plaintiff: Probably.
Judge: Okay, so what she says that you still had and refused to give her is a digital camera, a computer, six winter coats, two boots, six nursing uniforms, and some computer software. Let's talk about those. The digital camera and computer, did that stay behind?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: Okay. And why?
Plaintiff: Ask her.
Defendant: Okay, where is it now?
Defendant: In my house.
Judge: Okay. So, you do and those things belong to her?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Who do they belong to?
Defendant: We bought those while we were together, and she left it.
Judge: Okay, you bought them while you were together and she left them. But who paid for them?
Defendant: You're not working. She's working 14 hours.
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: Okay. So, you agree that she paid for them, but you feel like since it was during your time together… That’s the logic?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: That sounds a little unfair to me. But let’s keep going.
Judge: How much are you asking for in total?
Plaintiff: $1,556.16.
Judge: Alright, what is that for?
Plaintiff: The computer, the camera, and the clothes.
Judge: Okay, and do you have the receipts for them?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: Well, based on the evidence in the case, I’m awarding you the amount that you have requested. You have won the case.
Judge: The decision is in your favor. Congratulations.

Judge: The defendant is counter suing for $500 for failure to give adequate notice. All parties please raise your right hand. Be seated. It comes to order, please. Let the record reflect the plaintiff has been sworn. Thank you. Welcome, all right, Debra Bueno, right? You are suing Helen, you've asked us not to mention your last name, and we'll respect that, for $500, that you say she still owes you on rental payments that you made when you moved in with her. But according to you, she changed the agreements after you moved in, and you are counter claiming against her for $500 because you feel like you gave her back too much money. Tell me what's going on here.
Plaintiff: Okay, I gave her $1,500 up front for three months' rent for the summer.
Judge: And your plan was to live there for three months, right?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: She wouldn't allow me to have an air conditioner. I was upstairs in a tiny small bedroom.
Judge: Was there any written lease between the two of you?
Plaintiff: No, just this receipt, which you know, you can see that I gave her $1,500.
Judge: Was there any written lease agreement?
Plaintiff: No, just this receipt.
Judge: Okay, so it was for the three months, and you did the right thing. You each kept the original copy of it. So go ahead.
Plaintiff: She wouldn’t allow me to have an air conditioner, and when did you find that out?
Plaintiff: I found that out when I moved in, and I said, you know, I'm going to get a small air conditioner, and she said, "No, you can't."
Judge: Did she say why you couldn’t?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: Were your utilities included?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Did she offer you, "Well, if you pay extra, you can do it"?
Plaintiff: No, she didn’t.
Plaintiff: The girl next door had an air conditioner, and her room was just a little bigger than mine.
Judge: Did you say to her, "But that's not fair, you let her have one." I mean, did you bring that up?
Plaintiff: Well, I didn't want to get into her business. I just feel that, you know, she was being unfair to me. That I didn't have one.
Judge: What did you say to her?
Plaintiff: I said, you know, I can’t live this way all summer without an air conditioner.
Judge: You said that, and then you moved out that day?
Plaintiff: No, no, I was there for nine days.
Judge: You were there for nine days total?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: So pretty much moved out, that’s alright. So go on.
Plaintiff: And also, I just want to show you some pictures. There was a long, like, 40-foot walkway before you get to the entrance, and she refused to keep a deck light on.
Judge: Did you say to her, “There’s not enough lighting back here, can you put a light,” and she said no?
Plaintiff: No, it just happened to be dark, which of the two scenarios is it?
Plaintiff: I saw her put the deck light on once, and I asked her, you know, can you keep the deck light on at night to walk? It's a long walkway.
Judge: Can I see the picture?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Plaintiff: She said no.
Judge: What else?
Plaintiff: I also wanted to show you a picture of the window that had a board, it held the window up, and maybe that was partly why she wouldn’t allow me to have an air conditioner. I don’t know if the window was broken. I don’t know.
Judge: Is this how when you rented it?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Wouldn’t it be wise to say to her before you rent the place, “Will I be allowed to have an AC?”
Plaintiff: Well, I wasn’t informed beforehand that I wouldn’t be allowed to. I didn’t think that there would be an issue.
Judge: Okay, you moved out because she wouldn’t let you have the AC. Also, she always told me to park in front of this gold car, and there’s no way I could have fit.
Judge: Right, right, in this picture?
Plaintiff: And I kept parking on the side, and she said, “You can’t because of the septic tanks.” I don’t understand why I can’t park, you know, on a septic tank. I don’t understand that.
Judge: Alright, what’s going on? What happened? Did she get mad about the AC?
Defendant: No, what happened is she got mad when she had a car accident in the driveway. She had already been in the apartment in the room rather for 10 days. She hit a car in the driveway. She told me she had a driver's license, and she told me the car was hers. I learned from the car accident she has no driver's license. It’s expired or non-existent; I don’t know.
Judge: Whose car did she hit?
Defendant: My friend Mega’s.
Judge: Is she the one with the AC unit?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Why did she—did she ever ask you about an AC unit? Yes or no?
Defendant: Never even asked you.
Judge: So according to you, the reason she actually moved out is because what?
Defendant: Because she hit a car in the driveway. She wanted the case to be settled away from the insurance company because she had no driver’s license for 10 years and because it was not even her car. So here she is with the car of a third party in my driveway in a car accident with no driver’s license.
Judge: I called, I called, I think Mega called the police to talk about the accident and the police report to get a police report. And the first thing that I said to her when the policeman is there: “Debbie, move your car out of the driveway.”
Judge: These litigants were living in the defendant's house. The plaintiff was a tenant, but in less than 2 weeks, the plaintiff moved out because she couldn't have an air conditioner, and there were car issues. The defendant says the plaintiff hit a car in the driveway. Let’s listen.
Judge: So what you're saying, then, is she got mad because you would no longer allow her to park a third party’s car on your property?
Defendant: Yes, because I didn’t want to incur liability to whoever the third party is. I felt if I did what she wanted me to do, I would actually be in a bailment kind of a thing with this third party.
Judge: Okay, with her driving this car with no driver's license. Okay, well, alright. Let me ask you a question. Whose car is it, first of all?
Defendant: It’s my car, but it's titled under my ex-husband. I won the car over radio.
Judge: You have a license?
Defendant: Okay, it's expired. I have a learner's permit.
Judge: How long has the license been expired?
Defendant: Since 2009. I was sick. I was hospitalized. I couldn’t renew it.
Judge: Why do you have a learner's permit?
Defendant: Well, now, because it expired for 3 years, and you have to, if it’s after two years, you have to do it over again.
Judge: Can I see your learner's permit?
Plaintiff: She's making up a story that has nothing to do with why I moved out. That’s irrelevant.
Defendant: No, if you moved out ‘cause you no longer had a parking space because you hit the car, do you have an off button?
Judge: I’m in the middle of a sentence. You think that's going to help your case?
Plaintiff: It does matter. It is incredibly relevant.
Judge: Because if all of a sudden, it’s the way she’s saying, which is, "You can’t park your car there, you don’t want to live there anymore,” that’s a real reason you don’t want to live there.
Plaintiff: So don’t tell me that it’s not relevant. I know you have years of experience in courts, which is probably where you learned to roll your eyes at a judge. I know that, but you are wrong when you say that it’s not relevant.
Plaintiff: I had already decided to move out. It doesn’t matter whether she says, "I could park here or not," I had decided to move out because...
Judge: Did you tell anybody about that decision?
Plaintiff: I told her. I wrote her a note. "I cannot live here without an air conditioner." Before I even bumped into that girl's bumper.
Judge: Do you have that note?
Defendant: Well, she has it.
Judge: Do you have that note?
Plaintiff: She didn’t save it. Of course she didn’t save it. Never wrote a note.
Defendant: Excuse me, Judge. I had left her a note in the kitchen saying, "There's no way I can live here without an air conditioner," and that was before I bumped into Ma's car.
Plaintiff: Okay, she’s disputing that you ever wrote that.
Defendant: She can dispute that, but I did write it, and I left it.
Judge: Do you have any proof of it?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: Okay. Now let's go to the next issue. You gave her back some money.
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: How much money?
Plaintiff: I gave her $1,000 back to settle the case and not go to court.
Judge: Tell me about that.
Plaintiff: That's in, as far as I’m concerned, I am in court and it's extortion that she took $1,000 from me.
Plaintiff: I have the settlement agreement. I crossed out the lines that said she would, you know, in front of three officers. The second day I went out, the day after the accident, with one policeman. I went out the next day early. I was gone all day. She moved her things out all day. She was waiting for me to get the money, the $1,500 back.
Plaintiff: I came back about 7:00 p.m. when we started to discuss the money issue. It got exciting, and I got on the phone and got a policeman to come over. Three policemen came.
Defendant: So they let us work it out for two and a half hours. This went on. I’m going to settle it, I’m not going to settle it. I'm going to settle it, I’m not going to settle it, and the police didn’t just leave.
Plaintiff: No, they were very patient.
Defendant: Excuse me. No, you cannot interrupt. They were very patient.
Judge: And who wrote it?
Defendant: I did. I wrote it. And I failed to notice when she signed it that she scratched out the part about we hold each other harmless in the future. But she failed to scratch out the part that says "full settlement."
Judge: Okay, confirming Helen and Debbie have both agreed to cancel the previous rental agreement and institute a full settlement of monies Debbie paid Helen. The rental settlement is based on Debbie's turning over the key immediately and upon vacating today receiving $1,000 from Helen. Helen is receiving $500. Well, the $500 is hers, but each holds the other harmless.
Defendant: These are the words that were crossed out by you.
Defendant: Now, let me give you this, judge.
Plaintiff: Yes, I know.
Judge: Thank you, okay, no settlement. So go ahead.
Manipulation Techniques: gaslighting, persuasion, deflection, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge: All right, Anthony Reel, you are suing Monty McCoy, Real versus McCoy, for $1,248.196. That you won't return to you. Okay, tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: Yes, how you doing, Yan?
Judge: Good, how are you?
Plaintiff: Okay. On May the 16th, I hired Mr. McCoy to come and lay some new sod down on my yard because my grass had weeds in it and it was looking a little bad, so I figured I’d get a whole new grass done, you know, have it dug up and had Mr. McCoy lay some new sod. The way I was referred to Mr. McCoy, one of my neighbors down the street had him do the same thing, and I liked the way it looked. So he gave me Mr. McCoy's phone number, and I called him. I gave him a down payment. Two days later, he came and dug up my old grass. The next day, he laid the new sod, and then I finished paying him.
Judge: Then I guess it looked good?
Plaintiff: Yes, it looked good.
Judge: All right. What did he tell you about how to care for it?
Plaintiff: He told me that he was going to come back a week later and lay some fertilizer down and he was going to cut the grass.
Judge: But what did he tell you about watering it or anything?
Plaintiff: Yes, I know that everybody knows you have to water grass, old grass and new grass. So that’s what I did. I watered it every day. The rain helped; it rained a couple of days within that week.
Judge: Okay. He came back. Mr. McCoy came back. He said he laid some fertilizer down, he cut the grass. A few days later, my grass started browning. I called him back and said, "Mr. McCoy, you got to come back over here, my grass is brown." He comes over and he sees what's going on, and he started coming up with a lot of different excuses. "My kids played in the grass, ran on the grass." Did you water?
Plaintiff: Yes, I did water it. I told him. Then he said he was going to take a sample of my grass. He thought I got a leak underground that's messing my grass up. He was going to take some of my grass and get a sample test done.
Judge: I said, okay. He comes back about a couple weeks later. It gets worse. This is where my pictures start. When it got worse, and by now it’s in June.
Plaintiff: Okay, so go on.
Plaintiff: I’m calling, calling, calling. He’s not answering my phone calls. He started ignoring my phone calls, right? So I had to trick him. I borrowed my daughter’s phone and I called him with another number. He answered that one, right?
Judge: Yes.
Plaintiff: Yes, and I said, “Mr. McCoy, what are you doing? I paid you good money to fix my grass and look at my grass, it’s dying. What are you going to do?” He said, “Mr. Reel, I’ll be over there in two, three more days.” A week passed by, another week passed by. We’re getting into July now. I’m calling, calling, calling. He’s ignoring my calls, he’s not returning my calls. So now I decided I’m going to have to take him to court.
Judge: Okay. I have a question. Is this your house too, like that's part of your house, or is that the neighbor’s house?
Plaintiff: Let me see. See what I mean? There, all your house or is this someone else’s?
Plaintiff: No, this is the neighbor. I didn’t get to this part. This neighbor down the street, grass died that he did.
Judge: Wait, next door to you?
Plaintiff: No, about four houses down.
Judge: Oh, whose... Wait a minute, wait a minute. Are those... Did you hand me everything you handed me were pictures of your house?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: Oh, for the love of God, when were you going to tell me that?
Plaintiff: I got it on the back.
Judge: Okay, I’m sorry. That’s the neighbor whose grass you had seen and said, "Ooh, that looks good. I want me some of that before his died."
Plaintiff: Okay, before his died. Okay.
Judge: Is the neighbor suing him too?
Plaintiff: No, the neighbor, after the neighbor's grass died, he figured he better fix it. He fixed it. I got a picture in there.
Judge: Wait, to fix the neighbor’s?
Plaintiff: But not mine. Not mine.
Judge: I think they’re friends. I’m not sure. I don’t know why, but I have a picture... What’s going on?
Defendant: Good morning, Judge. This is what’s actually happening. Mr. Reel contacted me. I work in an area. I’ve got 72 houses that I basically maintain their area alone. He approached me, telling me something about he had a contractor do some popcorn ceiling in his house. He said the yard was contaminated from the popcorn ceiling chemicals.
Defendant: I told you what I can do. I tore it up, treated the soil, fertilized the soil. How do you treat the soil?
Defendant: With lime and liquid fertilizer, and that should take care of the chemicals from the popcorn ceiling.
Defendant: Well, that’s what we were trying to do, you know, use lime. Lime usually kills or neutralizes any chemical contaminants, usually.
Defendant: I did exactly what Mr. Reel asked me. Now, the first 7 to 10 days, you must not water it, saturate sod, not just water it but saturate it. Did you tell him that?
Defendant: I explained that to him and every other client.
Defendant: What he did, if you look clearly at stuff, it’s drying out. It’s not exactly dying; it’s drying out. If you look very closely, it’s drying out.
Judge: How do you dry out when you water grass? If you were grass, how would you dry out?
Defendant: I can’t. It dries out if you don’t water it properly, it will dry out. New sod.
Judge: Are there other things besides not watering it properly that could cause this sod to turn this way? For example, when he says that you said to him, maybe the chemicals from the popcorn ceiling... I’m going to get your sod tested. Did you ever test the sod?
Defendant: I never told him that. I don’t know where he got that from. I didn’t test it.
Judge: Did you ever say to him that you would take it somewhere to be tested?
Defendant: No.
Judge: All right.
Defendant: Not at all.
Judge: Did the neighbor’s yard also go bad?
Defendant: His yard? What actually happened is it separated after it settled, and the grass died. He called me back, he said, “Listen, can you just come redo the hill?” I said, “No problem. I’ll redo the whole yard as long as you pay for the sod.” He paid me for the sod. I went back down, redid the whole yard at my expense. I didn’t charge him any further labor. I didn’t charge him anything else but the sod.
Defendant: Now, I have pictures. His yard has been growing for weeks now, no issues. Now, the soil on the sod has dried completely out. When I walked across it, I got issues.
Manipulation Techniques: gaslighting, deflection, minimization, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Plaintiff: He just texted me recently, saying he wanted to get back with me.
Judge: Is that true, sir?
Defendant: No, it is not.
Plaintiff: I have the proof right here.
Judge: [Reading] “I’m horny. Come over.”

Defendant: It’s because if I don’t, she’ll call my wife or report me. I’m in a situation where I either get locked up or lose my wife for definite.

Bailiff: All rise.
Judge: Thank you, Douglas. Cassandra Jackson, you are suing Quenton Slaughter for $477 that you say he owes you when he broke your heart, your lease, stole your stuff, and ran off with your sister.
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: Okay. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: Well, we’d been dating for about two years. Recently, last year, we just moved in together for the first time, and everything was all right. But then it started... like, I know you want to go out on Fridays and be with your boys after work or whatever, but then it started getting to the point where it seemed like he was starting arguments about just any little thing—just to get outside. And then I noticed that when we had an argument, he’d go to his mother’s house and stay there.
Judge: So you thought?
Plaintiff: Yeah. Yeah. Yes, yes, yes. I thought so. Come to find out, one day I’m at work, and he called himself leaving. But actually, when I came home, the stuff was gone this time. And I’m like, okay? And then that’s when I started calling and confiding in my sister. And she’s telling me, "Oh, don’t worry about it," and everything. And she knows really what’s going on.
Judge: What do you mean? You were confiding in your sister, telling her you were having problems with him?
Plaintiff: Yes, I was telling her I was having problems with him.
Judge: What a good sister.
Plaintiff: And then, meanwhile, she’s the one who’s running around with him behind my back.
Judge: Yeah. Okay. So he moved out. Where’s your sister? Is she here?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: What a shame.
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: So he moved out in November, which left you with December, January, and February’s rent. And you haven’t heard from him?
Plaintiff: No. This is the first time I’m seeing him since.
Judge: What about your sister?
Plaintiff: I talked with her once. But like I said, she’ll always be my sister. I can’t change that.
Judge: She doesn’t have to be.
Plaintiff: That’s what I’m saying. I don’t talk to her.
Judge: What did she say to you when you found out?
Plaintiff: She said it never started out to be like that.
Judge: Oh, no. It never does. It just ends up like that.
Plaintiff: Exactly. And no one told me. I was left to find out all on my own.
Judge: How did you find out?
Plaintiff: Well, like I said, I started calling around, and my neighbors... when I asked about the furniture and stuff being moved, they said, "Well, I saw your sister."
Judge: Your sister was there?
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: Helping him move?
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: Was she there telling him not to take it?
Plaintiff: No. They were there taking it together.
Judge: So what happened next?
Plaintiff: I called.
Judge: Who did you call? Your sister or him?
Plaintiff: My sister.
Judge: What did your sister say?
Plaintiff: I asked her, "Were you involved in moving the stuff?" She denied it. I said, "My neighbor saw you." Then she finally broke down and said, "Well, it’s like this… me and Quenton, we’re together, and we’re living together now."
Judge: Wow. Wasting no time, huh?
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: Is there another sister, or is it just the two of you?
Plaintiff: Just the two of us.
Judge: Wow. What did you do next?
Plaintiff: I dropped the phone.
Judge: Let me turn to the defendant. What’s going on, man?
Defendant: Well, me and Cassandra were staying together or whatever, but all along, I was messing around with her sister.
Judge: Wow.
Defendant: Yeah. I ain’t want her no more.
Judge: You what?
Defendant: I ain’t want her no more. I got attached to her sister.
Judge: So what is she, a cougar?
Defendant: Yeah, she’s a cougar.
Judge: How so?
Defendant: She started off being a sugar mama.
Judge: How much older is she than you?
Defendant: She’s 45. I’m 26.
Judge: How old is her sister?
Defendant: 24.
Judge: Do you work?
Defendant: No, ma’am. Not anymore.
Judge: When was the last time you worked?
Defendant: About three months ago.
Judge: What were you doing?
Defendant: A car wash.
Judge: Why did you stop working?
Defendant: They wasn’t paying good enough.
Judge: How much are you making now?
Defendant: Nothing.
Judge: Less than what you were making before, huh?
Defendant: Yeah.
Judge: So, let’s talk about the stuff in the apartment. Why did you take everything?
Defendant: I chipped in on that stuff. It’s good as hers as it is mine.
Judge: Then why did you take all of it?
Defendant: I needed it.
Judge: Could you be a bigger jerk? Is that even possible?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Seriously, you left her with rent, stole her furniture, and ran off with her sister?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: You don’t deserve to walk among the rest of us.
Plaintiff: And he took my kids' PlayStations.
Judge: You took the kids' PlayStations?
Defendant: I needed something to play.
Judge: You don’t deserve to walk among us.
Defendant: It was as good as mine as it was hers.
Judge: What kind of person does that?
Defendant: I don’t know.
Judge: Do you ever do anything that you don’t just want to do for yourself?
Defendant: No.
Judge: I hope your new girlfriend is listening. Show me a receipt for the items.
Plaintiff: My rent was $1,600. He was supposed to pay $800.
Judge: So you were left with the full amount. And the lease is over now?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Well, I am ordering this degenerate to pay you the $2,400 in rent that is his portion for the last three months. As well as the depreciated value of all the items he stole. My judgment in your favor is for $3,810.
Defendant: (Muttering)
Judge: Get out of my courtroom. I can’t do this another second.
Bailiff: All rise.

Judge: Mr. Heartfield, you say you made a mistake by having an affair, but you know you are not the father of Ms. Streeter's child. You claim that Ms. Streeter was openly sleeping with other men besides you. Today's results will determine whether or not you'll be able to save your marriage, is that correct?
Plaintiff: Mr. Heartfield asked me to have a baby with him. We were dating for about eight months. A long time we were in a relationship, a committed relationship with just me and him. Throughout the whole relationship, he wanted to have a baby. He said he didn’t get to raise any of his.
Defendant: She’s telling you guys a bunch of lies. That’s convincing. I mean, if I didn’t know any better, I’d be convinced.

Judge: This is the plaintiff, Mark Brown. He says he rented a condo for a week on the island of St. Martin, and as it turned out, that was the week Hurricane Irma hit. The resort where the condo was located was shut down due to the storm, and when he asked for his money back, the defendant told him he would only refund half. Half? The condo was uninhabitable. There was no way anyone could stay there, and he's owed a full refund in the amount of $800. That's what he's suing for.
This is the defendant, Earl Cap. He says he always tells renters to get insurance for things like hurricanes, which the plaintiff has done in the past because he's rented from them before. This time, for whatever reason, the plaintiff opted not to get the insurance. A hurricane hit, and now he wants a full refund from him? No way. He’s not entitled to a refund.
He’s accused of creating a financial disaster. The defendant has filed a countersuit for $432 for monies he had to pay the resort.
All parties, please raise your right hands.
Judge: Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket, the plaintiff rented a condo in St. Martin, put down a deposit, but then Hurricane Irma hit. The resort was a wreck, and he couldn't go, so he wants his deposit back. The defendant says he should have gotten renter’s insurance. It’s the case of Rock Me Like a Hurricane.
Thank you, sir.
Mark Brown, you are suing Earl and Carol Cap for $800, a deposit that you put on a timeshare rental, and you want it returned to you. You are counterclaiming against him for some extra money you put towards the resort in order for him to be able to rent a bigger unit, correct?
Defendant: Correct.
Judge: Okay. Have you ever stayed in that timeshare before?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay, his timeshare, right?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And when you did that—you own a timeshare and you rent out—do you rent it out every year?
Defendant: Uh, it depends on the timeshare and who the guest is.
Judge: How often do you end up renting it out?
Defendant: Um, it depends. Usually once, maybe twice per year.
Judge: Okay, do you own several timeshares?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: How many?
Defendant: We’re timeshare junkies. We own about six of them.
Judge: All right, so sometimes you use them, sometimes you rent them out?
Defendant: We always use them, and our contracts allow us to use excess time that we can then rent out.
Judge: Right. All right, so you were going—where is this particular one located?
Defendant: This one is at Divi Little Bay Resort on the island of St. Martin.
Judge: That’s the Caribbean?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: And you’ve rented that one before?
Plaintiff: I’ve rented that before.
Judge: And how much were you paying for it?
Plaintiff: Uh, $1,600 a week.
Judge: Something funny happened on the way to the forum—a hurricane hit, correct?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And that was the week you were going to be there?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: A few months later, the island was devastated, basically unusable, right?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: The resort was closed at that time?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Has the resort ever reopened?
Defendant: Yes, it reopened afterward.
Judge: So, he wants his deposit back because the place was closed and he couldn’t go. Why aren’t you returning his deposit?
Defendant: Mark rented in 2015 a week from us at a different resort on the same island, and it was under the same terms. That is to say, we are not a company—we dispose of personally available time, and we do not offer cancellation insurance or interruption insurance. We ask all guests to get the same to protect themselves because we don’t offer any protection.
Judge: Can I see any written paperwork between you two? Any emails, anything like that? Any contract?
Defendant: There’s no contract. Just an email with the deposit made, the dates, and the confirmation from the resort.
Judge: Do you have a written agreement with him—Mark Brown?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Do you have anything where he acknowledges that it’s his responsibility to get insurance?
Defendant: No.
Judge: So if a hurricane hits an island where you rent a condo, can you get your deposit back?
Judge: I’m not 100% sure, but I don’t think so—unless you’ve got some kind of insurance specifically for that.
Judge: Anybody have a different opinion? Going once? Going twice?
(There is silence from the audience.)
Judge: I can’t believe nobody’s saying this. Nobody has any idea? Higher force? Whatever?
Judge: I’ve looked through the only paperwork anybody’s given me, and nowhere does it say the following: You assume the risk of an act of God.
Judge: I would never rent a place that had me assume the risk of an act of God. If you had a contract with him that said, You assume the risk of an act of God, I could understand you saying, “Hey buddy, I’m out the money. I’m not a company. I spent your money and don’t want to pay it back.”
Judge: But the deal is, he’s renting a place he can go to, and now he can’t go to it anymore. And you’re assuming that’s a him problem? I’m looking at it as a you problem.
Judge: You really think you need to raise your hand? Because I don’t see how that means he has to take a vacation at a different time.
Judge: You have the ability to find a solution. You can rent it out at another time because the timeshare allows you to rent that time. You understand what having a right to a place means.
Judge: And you are having them provide you with a subsequent time to rent out or use. So you’re not really out. The guy who’s out is him.
Judge: And he’s out how much? What was the deposit?
Plaintiff: $800.
Judge: Right. So tell me where I’m not getting this.
Defendant: The money we had to pay to the resort for the upgrade for the Brown family was a one-time, specific-date reservation.
Judge: If the resort couldn’t give it to you, then they would let you rent it at a different time.
Judge: You are now able to rent out a one-bedroom instead of a studio, right?
Defendant: Actually, no. Only the studio.
Judge: Well, then, I suggest you go through the resort.
Judge: My verdict is for the plaintiff in this case for $800. On the counterclaim, zero. That’s my judgment.
Manipulation Techniques: persuasion, evasion, deflection, minimization, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge: Okay.
Lawyer: One final question just for clarity. Could you walk through the text and show why your interpretation is consistent with the text?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So—
Judge: The relevant text?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yeah. So the 30 words of text about Barnett Bank—
Judge: Right.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: —which we've talked about. If we want to talk about "significantly interfere," I think the word "significant" does some work because it does a significant amount of work. Not any law that could be said to interfere with the banking power—we've talked about the fair lending laws, talked about the age requirements, the writing requirements—it has to be significant, and it has to go to the authorized federal power.
Judge: Okay. Is what’s codified the words taken from Barnett Bank, "significantly interferes," et cetera, or is it the holding of Barnett Bank? Is it how Barnett Bank itself understood those words?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: The latter. I think you could say it's both, but it's clearly the latter. I think, in their view, you didn’t have to enact any reference to Barnett Bank because they just start with significant interference.
Judge: And "case by case"?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: "Case by case" refers to the OCC in terms of their saying if you're going to proceed by order or regulation, you'd have to just look at escrow laws because it has to be a substantial— I mean, you might have a debate about what's substantially equivalent in escrow laws. But "case by case" is not referring to facts. It's referring to you can't just say we want to preempt everything on mortgage loans. You have to look at, like, escrow, down payment, maximum—you have to just go kind of law by law. But it's talking about the OCC.
Judge: Thank you.
Judge: Justice, any questions?
Judge: No.
Judge: Any other questions?
Judge: I just— I just have one thing on your distinction because I'm still trying to follow it. You rely on Anderson, and I guess the other case that sort of implicates the same facts as Anderson is the California case—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Correct.
Judge: —which you've talked about. And the problem I'm having with your distinction between product or power and the transaction is that in California, the Court describes the law at issue there, which it says is preempted, as a statute that attempts to qualify in an unusual way agreements between national banks and their customers and may cause them to hesitate to subject their funds to possible confiscation. So it seems as though the Court in this case says the reason why you're preempted is because you are trying—this law is trying to regulate the transaction between the bank, which you say is the reason why in Anderson they would say it's not preempted.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So—
Judge: So I don’t—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yeah, a hundred percent. And you're completely correct. What we're saying is you have the control on the power of the banking product, and there's a second fallback test, which is the undue burden, and that undue burden is the practical impact. So if you had a state law that said—the difference between California and the Kentucky law—that said the minimum age requirement is 61 to open up a mortgage, well, that is a law of majority. It clearly would impose an unusual relationship on the relationship between the bank and its customers. So we do think you could go and preempt these laws that do interact with the consumer and the state.
Another one would be a state—national banks or any bank can only be open for one hour during the week. That's going to be preempted. Or you have to pay tellers $1,000 an hour. It's going to be preempted even though, of course, Title VII applies to national banks.
But I do think the California case leaves open, and Anderson says, if the state law is so unusual with respect to the bank and its consumers to the point that it's interfering with their operations, it will be preempted.
Judge: Thank you.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Judge: Do you happen to know how many shelter beds there are in Grants Pass?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I believe the only shelter beds, at least at the time the record in this case was compiled, were at the Gospel Mission. There has been a detox place at times and a warming center that has been maintained. But in terms of shelter beds --
Judge: Well, we're talking about --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes, and I think --
Judge: -- a disproportionate --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I think it's approximately a hundred. There are men's and women's shelters.
Judge: Yeah. I thought it was much less than that.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: All right. So you want the district court to make individualized findings. You've asked us to vacate and remand. Can we go back to that so I understand it? I didn't quite understand it in your brief because I thought individualized findings had to do with the class action, but that question hasn't been certified here.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Right, but I think our basic point is that a person does not have an Eighth Amendment defense or claim unless they truly do not have another place to reside. By speaking of individualized findings, what we were --
Judge: So --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: -- saying is that it depends on whether that person has some other place, such as a relative's home.
Judge: I accept all of that.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yeah.
Judge: I guess what I'm asking you is -- and this is what I didn't understand from your brief -- are you saying that there can't ever be a class certification of homeless people?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: No.
Judge: That you have to have individuals? Or are you saying that the injunction is too broad if it doesn't provide for remedies that require the person to prove a certain --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: The --
Judge: -- number of things before --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: -- they're entitled to the injunction?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yeah. Yes.
Judge: I wasn't sure.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: No, the Eighth Amendment claim is a personal one and, in this context, depends on whether the person has another place to sleep. So the person cannot benefit from the Eighth Amendment claim without showing that they have no other place to stay.
Judge: Thank you, counsel. If the town next to Grants Pass, 10 minutes away, has just completed building a homeless shelter with many vacant beds, does that change the analysis? Would there still be a right to sleep contrary to the ordinances in Grants Pass, if someone doesn't want to be taken 10 minutes away where there's a shelter?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: That goes to the question of whether the beds are available. If they are just across the town line, it would be appropriate to take into account that there's a homeless shelter there, even though it's not in the City of Grants Pass. Often, two towns might cooperate to have one homeless shelter.
Judge: They might, but often the next towns don’t always cooperate. So what if it's 30 miles away? Is the shelter available in that case for your purposes, or are you going to tell me it just depends on all the circumstances so municipalities won’t have much guidance?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I think it depends on the accessibility. One of the fundamental points here --
Judge: The accessibility is that when an officer in Grants Pass finds a homeless person and says, "You violate our ordinance, but I will give you a ride down the road, 30 miles, to a new homeless shelter," and the person refuses, can that person be given a citation?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I think probably not, but let me explain why.
Judge: Sure.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Obviously, there are questions at the margin. But one principal issue is that the city is seeking to banish or expel its own residents—people whose children may go to school there, who may pay taxes there. If the 30-mile-away shelter requires the person to leave their community, that implicates --
Judge: How far does that go? Let’s say there are five cities around Grants Pass, all with homeless shelters. And yet, a person says, "I've been a Grants Pass resident for a long time; I don’t want to go to one of those shelters." Can that person be given a citation?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I think that would be a serious problem because the city’s ordinance would be requiring its own residents to leave.
Judge: You would say it would be a problem to give them a citation?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes, I think so, because the city would be implementing a policy of banishing people from Grants Pass.
Judge: Banishment is a strange word when you're talking about something 10 minutes away.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, again, the question is whether you can still realistically be part of the community where you grew up. The records show that most homeless people in Grants Pass are from Grants Pass.
Judge: Counsel, many people have mentioned this is a serious policy problem. The solution, of course, is to build shelter for those who need it. But municipalities have competing priorities. What if there are lead pipes in the water? Do you build a homeless shelter or take care of the lead pipes? What if there isn't enough fire protection? Which one do you prioritize? Why would you think these nine justices are the best people to judge and weigh those policy judgments?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: We're not suggesting that building homeless shelters is the only solution. Time, place, and manner restrictions are a sensible approach. Oregon state law requires that. For example, a city could have a provision that prohibits sleeping on sidewalks because it obstructs movement, camping near a school, or sleeping downtown, but might allow sleeping in a designated park that is patrolled for safety.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I think that's also right.
I mean, Congress didn't speak to this issue, and you could hold, consistent with your opinion in Jones versus Bock, that Congress's silence means that the ordinary procedures should apply.
And in this case, the ordinary situation is that disputed facts about affirmative defenses that are intertwined with the merits go to a jury.
Judge: If we get to the functional considerations that Justice Barrett was raising, it does seem to me like you would look at the policy of the PLRA.
The Court has said to look to statutory policies.
And that policy seems quite inconsistent with a jury trial right on the exhaustion question because the whole idea of exhaustion was to be speedy, to have the prison be able to resolve things quickly. And it seems just -- you know -- you know the argument on the other side, it just seems generally inconsistent with the policy in the PLRA.
So you want to respond to that?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Sure, Justice Kavanaugh. Two points.
I mean, I -- the State relies, I think, principally upon a 1966 case called Katchen versus Landy that has some language that sort of suggests that congressional policy can be relevant to functional considerations.
I think the Court has moved significantly away from that, and after cases like Granfinanciera, I'm not sure about the continuing viability of Katchen versus Landy.

Judge: What about Markman? Markman --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: I mean, Del Monte Dunes was an application of Markman itself.
