Defendant: 's Lawyer: And judges across the country are now going to superintend this under the Eighth Amendment.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: I -- I actually don't think that it -- it requires -- again, I don't think we should let the Ninth Circuit decisions characterize this.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: No, you want to -- okay. You -- you don't like the class certification, but that question is not before us, counsel.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: No, but all we're talking about is the core principle of Robinson, which is you cannot punish someone for a status. And -- and I think communities guided by that principle, and it's the only principle a court should be enforcing --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: How about --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: -- would retain a lot of flexibility.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: How about if there are no public bathroom facilities? Can -- do people have an Eighth Amendment right to defecate and urinate outside?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: No, we --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Is that conduct or is that status? --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: I -- it's obviously, there -- there is -- conduct there and we are not suggesting that cities can't enforce their --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Why not, if there are no public facilities available to homeless persons?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: The -- the -- that situation, you know, candidly, has never arisen. And whether or not there --I mean, in the litigation as I've seen. But no one is suggesting and we're not suggesting that public urination and defecation laws cannot be enforced because there are very substantial public health reasons for that.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, there are substantial public health reasons with drug use, with alcohol, and with all these other things too.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: And they can all be --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: But you're saying the Eighth Amendment overrides those. Why not in this circumstance right now?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: No, I'm not -- I'm not saying the Eighth Amendment overrides the laws against drug use.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Oh, I know that.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Oh, I'm sorry.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I know that.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: No, I misunderstood what you --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: That one -- that one the government wants to keep. I got that.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: No, I misunderstood your question. Sorry.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yeah. Last one. How about -- how about fires outdoors? I know you say time, place, and manner, but is there an Eighth Amendment right to cook outdoors?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: No. I -- I -- I --I think what -- what --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: That's -- that's an incident --a human necessity every person has to do.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: But this -- but this is one -- this is one of those things that, you know, is taken care of on the ground as a practical matter. There are restaurants where someone can go. There are --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, no, no, we're talking about homeless people.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: No.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: They're not going to go spend money at a restaurant necessarily. Let's --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, there may be inexpensive places. Some people get --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Let's say there isn't, okay?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: And --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Let's say that there is no reasonable --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: And -- and the local community --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Do they have a right to cook? They have a right to eat, don't they?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: They have --they have a right to eat, a right to cook if it entails having a fire, which I think it -- it --it probably --it probably would, but -- but, as I said, the -- the -- the eating, the feeding is taken care of in most communities by nonprofits and churches stepping forward --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: But if there isn't --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: -- as they have for 200 years.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: -- but, if there isn't, there's an Eighth Amendment right to have a fire?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: No, no, we are not saying there's an Eighth --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, I thought you just said there was.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, there -- there's food that you can eat without cooking it.

Judge: But I guess you keep -- your argument when you've -- in response to other people on the panel has been there's something about a threat of engaging in terrorism that is the thing that makes this situation one in which we can say with confidence that it won't reoccur.
So that sounds to me like it's pivotal to your argument that the Laidlaw standard is satisfied because of the nature of what is going on in this case.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Right.
The standard is the same.
It's the reasonable expectation of recurrence.
So we are not asking for a different legal test in the national security context. My point is that where the thing that has to recur is that an individual poses a threat of engaging in one of four enumerated acts of terrorism, that a court should hold is just not likely to recur --
Judge: But doesn't -- but aren't you skipping over the government's assessment of whether or not that's happening and the basis upon which the government is making that assessment? So this is what I mean, right? The government doesn't -- you keep saying it's a totality of circumstances analysis.
And I appreciate that.
But, according to the declaration and everything we understand, the government is making that assessment on the basis of certain criteria. Am I right about that?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I've told you the criteria.
Judge: No, no, no.
I mean even more than that, right? That there's a list -- like you say in the declaration -- I'm just trying to find it.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: It's on 118a.
Judge: 118.
Thank you.
In the declaration, that he was placed on the -- the No Fly List in accordance with applicable policies and procedures. So there is some policy that the government looks at in each case when it's assessing on the totality of the circumstances whether or not a person qualifies, right?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So the -- the policy as referred to there is the standard I just gave you.
Judge: It's that level of generality? A person can't make an argument that the government, in applying that standard, has used certain criteria that I'm challenging with respect to my application?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: No, I've -- I've given you the criteria for placement on the No Fly List.
You also have to satisfy placement on the broader Watchlist, which is reasonable suspicion that you are -- they call it a KST, a Known or Suspected Terrorist.
But --
Judge: All right.
So, if a person wants to argue in their claim that they're bringing in their lawsuit is that the government was mistaken about its assessment that my conduct qualified under the standard that you have articulated, and then the government said -- does not say we've changed the way we looked at what you did before, we've changed the criteria that we used to assess it or anything, the government just says, okay, you're now off the list and we think the case is moot as a result, why -- why does the court have some sort of reassurance that that -- that the government wouldn't look at the situation down the line and make the same assessment?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Because, in this case, if -- if it's as you just said, if I heard you right, then -- then I agree, that would pose a much tougher case. But, here, we have two additional things.
One, the Courtright declaration that we were just reading makes clear that he was removed from the list not just as a matter of grace or because, you know, we wanted to moot out the litigation, but he was removed from the list because he substantively didn't satisfy the criteria anymore.
And that, under the presumption of good faith and national security, is entitled to respect as, you know, and -- and absent some strong showing of bad faith. And then, second, the Courtright declaration says: And we're not going to reconsider this decision because you won't be put back on the No Fly List based on the currently available information.
So it adds a stickiness to that determination.
So it's not a matter of grace, and it's sticky and so isn't likely going to be revoked in the future.
Judge: All right.
Let me ask you about the standing question real quick. Does the government dispute that the plaintiff had standing at the time that he filed his suit in 2013?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: No.
Judge: All right.
So he did.
And don't we ordinarily assess standing at the time the person brings the lawsuit? If you have standing to bring -- to file the lawsuit, then, to the extent your circumstances change, we now move into the realm of evaluating it under mootness, right?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: That's correct, but I'll point out that Already, which was a case about mootness, did mention that, you know, in these circumstances where the -- the claim is no longer really live, that cases like Lyons and Article III apply with equal force, that a litigant cannot just rely on speculative injuries to keep the case alive. So mootness and standing should not be interpreted to be that different from each other because both are implementing Article III's case or controversy requirement.
And that's why, as I was mentioning to another judge earlier, I think, we -- we shouldn't interpret the two exceptions to -- to mootness that make it more flexible than standing unmoored from the purposes for which those exceptions were developed, which was really evasion of judicial review and, in particular, with voluntary cessation.
Judge: But isn't -- isn't -- I mean, Justice Scalia suggested that the voluntary cessation exception really is about whether or not the case is moot to begin with. It's not as though we're accepting that it's moot under a circumstance in which the government takes him off the No Fly List and then we're looking at is there an exception to mootness in this situation.
Instead, we're saying has the government actually mooted the case when it takes him off the No Fly List, right?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, I'm not sure about that.
I mean, Justice Scalia dissented in Friends of the Earth.

Judge: All parties, please rise. Welcome back to the People's Court. Next case in the docket—the plaintiff rented an apartment to the defendant, and the defendant sublet to a dude who had tattoos and nose rings. But the defendant says he had permission to do what he did. It’s the case of the nose knows.
Judge: Thank you, Douglas. Okay, William Peterman, you are suing your former tenant Richard Colville for $1,950—three months of rent that you say he hasn’t paid and owes you. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: Well, Richard was a good tenant. I had no problems there. What happened was he let me know that he was going to move. He said he had somebody interested in the apartment. I said fine. He moved, and right away must have given this other fella, this Ron, the key because before I knew it, he was in there with boxes.
Judge: Okay, I’m sorry. When did you realize that Ron was in there?
Plaintiff: Richard had told me that he cleaned up the apartment, and I went to go look at it.
Judge: When did you go in to look at it?
Plaintiff: Uh, I don’t know the exact date, but it was the end of March.
Judge: End of March or the end of February?
Plaintiff: The end of March.
Judge: And Richard told you that he would be leaving when originally?
Plaintiff: Originally, he said February, but it was March that he left.
Judge: He changed it to March because he stayed in March?
Plaintiff: He must have. Well, I didn’t get much rent.
Judge: From him? What did you think was happening in March when you didn’t get rent? What did you think? Like, don’t you get rent at the beginning of March?
Plaintiff: He was so good right along, I figured one month, you know, he was moving.
Judge: But if you think he’s leaving, then what do you think is happening with the apartment at the end of February? In other words, “I’m leaving in February.” Okay, you’ve been a great tenant. No worries. Alright. He’s leaving in February. Aren’t you on the last day of February? No? He’s going to tell me we’re there to check the place and re-rent it. What did you think was happening in March?
Plaintiff: I thought he was there in March.
Judge: Thank you. I’m going to stay an extra month. I know he didn’t leave exactly when he wanted to.
Judge: Did he tell you though, “I need an extra month, I can’t get out”?
Plaintiff: You know, I can’t recollect exactly, but I think so.
Judge: He calls at the end of March and tells you, “I’m cleaning up”?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And what happens?
Plaintiff: He told me that he had somebody interested in the apartment, and I said, “Okay, fine.”
Judge: That happens at the end of March?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And then what happens?
Plaintiff: This other fellow moved in.
Judge: When did the other fellow move in?
Plaintiff: It must have been just the end of March at the beginning of April.
Judge: Did you go by and take a look at the premises in March?
Plaintiff: The later part of March, yes.
Judge: Alright. There’s no security deposit here, right? So there was nothing that you needed to—so you go in the later part of March, and you see that the guy’s already moved in?
Plaintiff: Yeah. I can’t say exactly what day it was. It was either March or just right at the point of March ending.
Judge: And according to you, he gave you permission to hand that fellow the keys and the gate opener?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: And how did he give you that permission?
Defendant: By telephone.
Judge: Because Ron told—Ron was a friend of yours, right?
Defendant: Ron was a friend.
Judge: Is Ron still a friend of yours?
Defendant: Well, not now. I haven’t spoken to him since.
Judge: Okay, so you call him and say, “I’ve got a friend who’s interested in the place. His name is Ron.” What does he say?
Defendant: Okay, I gave Ron Mr. Peterman’s number, had him call Bill, and that’s it.
Judge: Did you ever speak to Ron on the phone?
Plaintiff: Yes, I did.
Judge: Oh, I thought you said you never met the guy.
Plaintiff: We were on the phone. I told him I would have to see him, we would have to meet, and I would get the rent.
Judge: Okay. And then did you have a lease with him or no?
Plaintiff: No, he’s not a lease maker. No written lease.
Judge: So, according to you, he tells you on the phone, “Yeah, Ron’s fine.” And then does he specifically talk about the keys and you handing them to Ron?
Defendant: Yes, because Ron told me that—
Judge: Oh, did he tell you, though?
Defendant: Yes, because Ron told me initially that Bill told him to give him the keys. So I called Bill and said I wanted to make sure, and he said, “Yes, you can give him the keys.”
Judge: Did that happen?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: My verdict in this case is for the defendant.

Judge: This is the plaintiff, Gordon Renault. He says he rented an apartment from the defendant and was quite literally electrocuted. The place had faulty wiring. He got the shock of his life when he touched one of the appliances. The paramedic told him his heart rate shot up to 200 beats a minute, and he moved the heck out. Now the shady defendant won't return his money, and he's suing for $5,000 today.
This is the defendant, Rebecca Theer. She says the plaintiff had, like, 15 people living in the apartment, and the power went out. She got someone there to fix it immediately. When she told the plaintiff she wouldn't be renewing his lease, the problem started. Bottom line—he's making this all up in retaliation for kicking him out, and she owes nothing.
She's accused of shocking a tenant.
She has filed a counter-suit for $1,590 for rent and eviction costs.
(All parties stand.)
Judge: All parties, please raise your right hand.
(Participants swear in.)
Judge: Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff got electrocuted at the apartment he rented from the defendant—so bad, he almost died—but the defendant won’t return his security deposit.
The defendant says there were 15 people living there, so what does he expect?
It's the case of “I'm So Shocked.”
Judge: Thank you, Douglas.
Ruby Rena and Gordon Renault, represented here by Gordon, you are suing Luther—who doesn’t want his last name mentioned—and Rebecca Theer, represented here by Rebecca Theer, for $5,000 in rent that you want returned to you, punitive damages, and a security deposit.
You have a counterclaim against them for $1,590 in rent, cleaning, and eviction costs.
Wow, this didn’t go very well. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: We moved in on February the 8th. May the 16th, I was cleaning the kitchen stove. I noticed that the boards were burnt.
Judge: What exactly are you talking about?
Plaintiff: The stove is actually inserted into the counter.
Judge: And so you were cleaning the stove and what? What did you notice?
Plaintiff: Boards were burned. Exposed wiring.
Judge: Okay. And so what did you do?
Plaintiff: At that time, I confronted her about it and asked her about a prior fire, and she denied the fact that there had been a fire.
Judge: What made you think there had been a fire?
Plaintiff: Boards were burnt that were under the stove.
Judge: Okay. You saw evidence of a fire. And she said?
Plaintiff: There has not been a fire that I know of.
Judge: And then what?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: Right. And then what? Were you able to use the stove and the oven?
Plaintiff: Yeah, the stove was new. It had been replaced. The boards just hadn’t been replaced.
Judge: Okay, so what did you do?
Plaintiff: There were no more issues at that time because everything was fine.
Judge: Okay. Is this a picture of what you're talking about?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: Okay. Everything’s fine after that—until it’s not. When’s your next problem?
Plaintiff: Thanksgiving Day.
Judge: Your oven quit working on Thanksgiving?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: That’s a bad day for the oven to stop working. Were you done cooking or not?
Plaintiff: No, ma’am. We was right in the middle of dinner.
Judge: Oh, that’s awful. So what happened to your turkey?
Plaintiff: Well, it didn’t get done.
Judge: There's no neighbors you could have—?
Plaintiff: We actually had our daughter finish cooking it.
Judge: Okay. Good. Good. So that happens, and then you call her when?
Plaintiff: The next day and tell her, "Listen, the oven—"
Judge: What did she say?
Plaintiff: She had a maintenance man at this time that supposedly would start taking care of issues.
Judge: And?
Plaintiff: He never showed up.
Judge: Okay. So then what happens?
Plaintiff: December 13th, our refrigerator went out.
Judge: Okay, but why did you wait until—? I mean, I need an oven. You don’t need an oven sooner than December 13th?
Plaintiff: We kept going to our daughter’s and using our daughter’s because it was convenient.
Judge: Did you have any communication with her, tell her the guy you hired—?
Plaintiff: My wife confronted her several times, asking her when the oven was going to be replaced.
Judge: And what would she say?
Plaintiff: The maintenance man is supposed to be taking care of it.
Judge: Okay. What was happening? The maintenance man didn’t take care of it?
Defendant: Weeks passed. We had decided not to put a new oven in, and we gave them a toaster oven.
Judge: Who’s "we"?
Defendant: Oh, that’s just my father.
Judge: You decided that people who rented a place with an oven could just do without one? That’s not really up to you.
Defendant: No, ma’am, but—
Judge: So why did you—? Would you like me to decide that for you? You're going to live without an oven?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Right? That sounds like—"Who do I think I am?" Right? Well, that’s exactly what I'm asking you. Why would you be in a position to decide that they’re going to live there without an oven?
Defendant: Well, we knew that they were going to be evicted the next month, so we didn’t—
Judge: What do you mean by "evicted"?
Defendant: We were just going to evict them when the contract ended.
Judge: So meaning you weren’t going to renew it?
Defendant: No.
Judge: But the contract went to February 8th, right?
Defendant: Right.
Judge: So there were two more months of them living there. More than two months. And you decided that you’d give them a toaster oven, and they would make do?
Defendant: Well, that was my sister’s—what she decided.
Judge: Then your sister's a jerk. Kind of. Because you can’t really do that.
Defendant: Okay.   and saying, "This is good enough for the likes of you," I am ordering the defendants to pay the plaintiffs the sum of $200 as a rent rebate.
Judge: That happened on Thanksgiving, so it's about a month and a half that you were without an oven. What's that worth? Kind of depends if you cook or not. I'm going to order the defendants to rebate rent to the tune of $200.
Judge: So there's a net judgment in favor of the plaintiff in this case, not of $5,000 but of $200.
Judge: On your counterclaim, for the reasons I've explained, zero.
Defendant: That’s kind of a cheap shot, don’t you think?
Judge: You handed them a toaster oven instead of fixing the problem.
Defendant: We didn’t mean to make it sound like that.
Judge: Well, it certainly did.
Defendant: We pulled them out of the homeless shelter to rent to them, and obviously, we just tried to help them.
Judge: It didn’t work.
Defendant: The tenant next to me was homeless. I gave her a home eight years ago, and she’s paid rent every single month. Never been a day of trouble.
Judge: Well, okay. Thank you very much.
(Defendant exits.)
Judge: Sorry about that. That’s the way it goes.
Plaintiff: Lord willing, it happened like it happened.
Judge: Sounds like this was a whole mess. Are you okay now? Where are you living now?
Plaintiff: I’m blessed. I’m in the homeless shelter.
Judge: Well, good luck to you.

"Judge:
This is the plaintiff Andrew Wilds. He says he rented the second floor of his two-family house to the defendant, and when he moved his wife and son in, there was suddenly a mouse problem. He thinks he brought the mice from Jamaica where he lived prior to moving in. The man caused all sorts of crazy trouble. Bottom line, he stopped paying the rent and he’s here suing for the $1,675 he’s owed.
This is the defendant, Silbert Rose. He says he lived in that apartment for 44 years without any problems. Then all of a sudden, the plaintiff turned evil. It was like a switch went off in his head and he was forced to leave. He owes him money for all the trouble he caused. He’s accused of running out on rent. No parties, please. Alright, your welcome back to the People's Court, next case in the dock.
Judge: The plaintiff says he rented the second floor of his home to the defendant and when the defendant brought his wife from Jamaica, there was suddenly a mice problem. But the defendant says the plaintiff is pure evil. It's the case of I hate those Mice to Pieces.
Andrew W, you are suing your former tenant Silbert Rose for $1,675 that you say you are owed in rent and late fees from when he departed. Tell me what’s going on.
Plaintiff: Okay, it came to a point where he paid his rent for March late.
Judge: How long had he been living there?
Plaintiff: He had been living there over 40 years. He was a tenant of my aunt. My mother passed away, I inherited the house. So I didn’t charge him a security deposit when I took over the house.
Judge: Did he pay a security deposit when your aunt was renting to him?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: How much in security deposit did he pay your aunt?
Plaintiff: $350 back in 1972.
Judge: And you know that because he say so?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: What, do you have any evidence that he paid a security deposit?
Plaintiff: No. What I had informed him was that if he was able to produce the receipt, I had no problem with giving him back his security deposit.
Judge: Was he able to?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: When did you start having problems?
Plaintiff: The problem actually started when he moved his family in from Jamaica.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: Okay, not Queens, but the island.
Judge: Oh, you say that like it’s a bad thing?
Plaintiff: Right.
Judge: Okay. Why, you’re saying... my child’s godmother is from Jamaica, starting to take a little offense, but go ahead.
Plaintiff: Okay, the first problem was that he had a problem with the wife. They stated that he had committed domestic violence against her and had him put out on the street. The man was crying. He was put out. She had an order of protection against him, and then eventually, she led him back into the house. As time went on, other issues started to arise. The noise. 5:00 in the morning, she’s using a carpet sweeper. They walked like there was no floor in the house. This bouncing... I mean stomping, it was just out of control. I had to tell her in the text one day that this is America now. I don’t know what you all did in Jamaica, but in America, Black people usually wake up on a Saturday and Sunday a little bit later in the day.
Judge: Okay. The sun...
Plaintiff: We started receiving mail at my residence with other people’s names on it.
Judge: Is it a communal mailbox?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Didn’t you just look at it, think it was an error, and send it back to the post office?
Plaintiff: So, one of the letters had a gentleman’s name, Mr. Lily. I spoke to Mr. Rose and asked him if he had any relatives named Charles Lily, and he said no. Later on, another envelope came in for Charles Lily from UPS. So we wrote on it, return to sender. UPS turned around and returned the same envelope back to us.
Plaintiff: So previously, maybe the day or so before, the son, Mr. Jackson, came to my door and asked me if I received any mail, which I didn’t. The person that received the mail was my daughter and wife. My wife got into an argument with Mr. Jackson over this envelope that came from you. Come on up.
Judge: Wife?
Plaintiff: ’s Wife (Anna):Good morning.
Judge: Good morning. So tell me about the argument.
Plaintiff: ’s Wife (Anna):So I go out there, and I saw that it says Charles Lily. I told him at the time, no, there is no Charles Lily that lives here, and you should not be giving this mail to him. I asked him what was his name, and he refused to give up his name.
Judge: Did the UPS guy end up giving him the mail?
Plaintiff: ’s Wife (Anna): Yes. He ended up... yes, he ended up giving the mail.
Plaintiff: ’s Wife (Anna):I got into it with his stepson saying, ""I am Charles Lily. It’s from my uncle."" He kept on saying, ""From my... said, so your uncle is named Charles.""
Judge: I just want you to know that whatever mail comes to this house and it’s not with your name, your mother’s name, or Mr. Rose, it will be going back where it belongs, at the post office.
Plaintiff: ’s Wife (Anna): Right.
Judge: Did you ever get to the bottom of all this or just alert the authorities?
Plaintiff: We went to the local post office, we put in a claim, they did it also because her federal income tax was missing. We put in a claim for that, and you ended up seeing the check was forged by someone.
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Well, whose account did it go into, or did it just get cashed?
Plaintiff: It got cashed, yeah.
Judge: That’s the end of that, unless it got cashed at a check-cashing place. It must have been something like that. There’d be pictures. Doesn’t it say where it got cashed?
Plaintiff: Yeah, they sent me the MICR form paperwork for me to do so. So I had to prove my signatures, and this person just printed my name, and they started misspelling.
Judge: Come closer to the microphone, Mom. Step back a second. Don’t push it.
Plaintiff: ’s Wife (Anna):
Right, but okay, so, but were they able to see what entity cashed the check?
Plaintiff: They never stated such a thing, but they showed me a picture, a photocopy of what the signature looked like.
Judge: I’m just curious, what... who’s cashing these checks without like a, you know, without a... a... cameras and that kind of thing.
Plaintiff: Alright, but... but you ended up getting your money back from the IRS.
Plaintiff: ’s Wife (Anna):
I think so.
Judge: Mr. Rose, how are you? 44 years living in a place is a long time. I’d hate to have some stepson come in and ruin it like that. What happened? What was your stepson doing, getting all kinds of mail that you didn’t... did you ever confront him and say, ""I’ve been living here 44 years; you have ruined everything for me""?
Defendant: I did.
Judge: And what did he say?
Defendant: Like he didn’t know about it.
Judge: Oh, he didn’t know about it, but you don’t believe he didn’t know about it.
Defendant: Well, it’s true. I don’t believe him.
Judge: I didn’t believe him either.
Judge: Are you still with the mom?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: You are? And did she falsely accuse you of domestic violence?
Defendant: Yes, she did.
Judge: Why are you with that lady? What magic does she have?
Defendant: I don’t know.
Judge: You are no longer living there, correct?
Defendant: Right.
Judge: Alright. So he’s suing you because he says that you didn’t pay rent for April.
Defendant: Yes, I did not, because I thought I was living out my security. That’s the reason why I didn’t pay that much.
Judge: Well, let’s talk about that. Your security... do you have any proof of having paid security or you don’t have that after 44 years?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am. I think I have the first check... see?
Judge: Oh, great. Can I see it?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: 1972, am I right?
Defendant: Yeah, that’s the one.
Judge: Do you have a check that goes with this?
Defendant: No, I just paid in cash and you gave me a receipt for it.
Judge: Take a look at that and tell me what you say. Does that look like your aunt’s writing and all that? I mean, I don’t know if you’d be familiar with it.
Defendant: Does.
Judge: Alright. And does it say security deposit on that receipt?
Defendant: Boy, I was ready to... I just keep getting surprised around here. It doesn’t say that.
Judge: Okay. So what was the amount of your security deposit?
Defendant: $190, not 350.
Judge: No? Okay.
Judge: So your security deposit was $190 in 1972. Got it. So if your rent is $1,000 in April, how is it you were calculating that the 190, 44 years earlier, was sufficient to pay rent in April?
Defendant: No, but the rent keeps going up, you know?
Judge: Yeah, I know. That’s my point, right? Rent keeps going up, so you can’t pay 1972 rents.
Defendant: Are you happy where you’re living now?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Do you wish you were still living there?
Defendant: Yeah, sure. I didn’t have a problem. I didn’t have a problem with him.
Judge: No, I know. He didn’t have a problem with you either. It’s the people you surround yourself with.
Defendant: He had a problem.
Judge: So can landlords and tenants get along indefinitely? Or is there inevitably a falling out?
Plaintiff: I really would... no, I rent from my grandma and she’s been the greatest landlord ever, so...

Judge: So, if the parties came to court and made an argument that was specifically directed at the delegation clause somehow being undermined, this would require showing that your argument addresses the same level of concern as who decides.
Again, if we think about it, you've got who decides, you've got arbitrability, and you have the merits. They pointed to a provision that talks about a court deciding controversies regarding the promotion.
This threshold question is not a controversy regarding the promotion. And I think that's why, in the district court, in the court of appeals, in this Court, the way respondents seek to get to a delegation invalidation is to argue that the arbitration agreement has been narrowed and there's been some corresponding narrowing of the delegation clause.
Judge: Can we set aside the way they framed it? Because I agree it's a little confusing if you start from the world where we have a delegation agreement, then we have a subsequent agreement, and the question is to what extent the subsequent agreement is a carveout or narrows or whatever.
Let's say we don't frame it that way. Let's say we frame it in the way that I want to think about it, which is: We have contract A that absolutely answers the who decides question, and then we have contract B that implicitly decides the who decides question.
Because I understood by operation of law, based on what was mentioned earlier, that if you don't pick arbitration, if you're silent about it, then the background rule operating is that arbitration is not what happens, and any question about whether or not arbitration is what happens—the who decides—goes to the court.
So we have contract number 1 that picks the arbitrator as the one who decides, and we have contract number 2 that implicitly picks the court as the one who decides.
I thought you agreed with me at the beginning that in that situation, it's a question for the court.
And that's what we're asking—Which of these contracts is actually operable today?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I think the difference between your earlier hypothetical, as I understood it, and this one is that the second contract, in your earlier hypothetical, specifically said that the delegation clause— it identified that there wouldn't be a delegation; it would be on some sort of different terms.
In this case, the second contract says nothing—
Judge: Right, but—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: —about delegation.
Judge: Do you disagree with me that, by operation of law, when a contract says nothing, the court is the one that decides what it means and what happens?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: In the abstract.
If the only—
Judge: No, not in the abstract. In the way we look at the law.
If it doesn't say anything about arbitration—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So if the contract didn't say anything about arbitration—
Judge: Yeah.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: —we wouldn't be able to invoke it as part of a motion to compel arbitration.
Judge: No.
What you're doing is you're invoking contract 1.
So—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: That's right.
Judge: What I'm saying is, he's invoking contract 2.
We have two people presenting contracts, one of which suggests that this is supposed to be decided by the court, and the other suggests that this is supposed to be decided by the arbitrator.
And so, in that situation, isn't the question which contract controls?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I think it's important to figure out what the correct starting point is.
And I think one of the remaining places of daylight between the respondents and the petitioner here is what the right starting point is.
We say the right starting point is the arbitration agreement that we seek to enforce.
Judge: Yeah.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: And here, that is the delegation clause in the first contract.
That delegation clause, under the straightforward operation of Section 2 of the FAA, remains valid, enforceable, and irrevocable unless they can make some state law challenge that would displace it.
And so the question becomes—and this is the analysis the lower court didn’t do—have they, by pointing to the forum selection clause that talks about resolving controversies about the promotion, done anything under state law to displace the who decides delegation clause in the first contract?
Judge: Counsel, can I ask you just a practical question?
I fully appreciate your argument that parties might send to the arbitrator the question of who decides and, at the same time, have the merits decided in one place or the other, even within a single arbitration agreement.
The arbitrator decides whether agreements go to court or arbitration. Some will; some won’t.
I get that.
I guess I'm a little curious why your client is fighting this so hard if, at the end of the day, you're going to wind up saying, yeah, sweepstakes disputes go to court.
So, if the arbitrator is going to have to send it to court anyway, why are we here?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: That’s the practical point that I tried to make earlier in response.
I think, if we thought this was going to end up back in court, we wouldn’t have invoked—
Judge: No, I—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: —the delegation clause.
Judge: I fully appreciate that, and I also appreciate that under Schein, an arbitrator could sanction you for a frivolous effort to keep this in arbitration.
My question is more practical than that and more detailed.
Why, in this case, are you fighting it when you have this second agreement that would seem to route the dispute to court?
I accept that maybe the arbitrator should have decided this.
I'm granting you that.

Judge: Just looking to history, I know South Carolina you have in your corner. Do you have any other antebellum cases from the states?

Defendant: 's Lawyer: I think that's right, but I think what the Alabama cases—
Judge: So South Carolina is your best one, I think.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: That's right. I think—
Judge: And they've admitted that they're an outlier. What do we do about that?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So I guess a couple of points. First, I think it is true that the four states that I think were on the other side of this, whether judges or juries had to make these recidivism findings, they were an—they were an outlier as to the default common law rule in this context. I concede that the majority of states had a default common law rule that these recidivism findings or these recidivism facts had to be in an indictment and proved to a jury beyond a reasonable doubt. I think—so I think South Carolina, Alabama, Louisiana, Kansas, they were outliers with respect to that tradition. But what I don't think they were—
Judge: But some of them weren't even members of the original states that formed the compact that led to the Sixth Amendment.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: That's true, Your Honor, but I think the—and as to your second point, the later cases, Alabama, Kansas, they—although they come later, they recognize that there had been a settled tradition in those states. And I think Plaintiff and the government have shown nothing to contradict that. So I think that is a fair assumption. And I do think that—so the fact that there is this different common law tradition in the majority of states, it doesn't—I don't think that's enough to establish that this was a fundamental principle that was incorporated into the Constitution, because we have these states within that majority, Virginia, Massachusetts, Maine, these are significant states, that were a part of the initial compact and that allowed legislatures to deviate from the common law rule. And then when those supplemental information statutes were challenged in court on constitutional grounds, courts rejected those challenges all the way through to this Court's decision in 1910 endorsing the Massachusetts Ross decision from 1824. So I think there's just a uniform string of precedents saying this majority common law rule that recidivism has to be in the indictment and proved to a jury, it's something the legislature can alter.
Judge: Can I ask you about bifurcation? Do you think bifurcation is completely in the discretion of the trial judge?

And then on bifurcation—I think bifurcation, the other problem which I mentioned earlier is that states have varying procedures on bifurcation, and so some of them make it discretionary, and I think—so it's going to have—it's hard to say exactly how this is going to play out in the states.

Judge: So I guess that drawing a line between testimony about a class or a group on the one hand and testimony about an individual happens at trial all the time. Judges are familiar with drawing that line.
I'm interested in the law professors' evidence professors' brief because they point to one context in which that happens with fair frequency, which is with respect to eyewitness testimony.
And they say courts often allow expert testimonial regarding factors that, on average, interfere with accurate eyewitness identifications.
However, courts do not allow experts to draw an individual inference and testify that a particular witness is likely inaccurate.
They go on to say, because social science research isn't reliably—even though it is itself replicated and reliable, it can't support a reliable statement about an individual case, especially when that social scientist hasn't really examined that individual case. And so judges, they at least say, are pretty familiar with the kind of line.
So is this the line you're drawing?
Prosecution: Yeah, well, I think that brief—the point the professors are making in that brief is that concerns that the expert has testified in some way that overstates the expert's ability to draw an inference about the particular case are not unfamiliar in federal court.
Courts handle those kinds of concerns every day under Rule 702.
And you could have a similar dynamic for testimony like this.
Judge: Right.
And I guess what I'm suggesting is that there is a difference between an expert talking about a group, an "on average," and here are the statistics that relate to how people operate or think or whatever as a group, and that that's actually a different kind of testimony and ultimate, you know, goal and helpful thing for jurors when they are trying to draw the inference as to whether or not this individual, right, is a member of that group, meaning acted in the same way or thought in the same way.
That's still left to the jury so long as the expert doesn't himself say—go on to say, and this individual is in that group, right?
Prosecution: I think that's exactly right.
We think that's how Rule 704(b) should work.
And here, the expert certainly left a great deal for the jury—a great number of the links in the chain of inferences for the jury itself to draw.
Judge: Thank you.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
Rebuttal?
Defense: Thank you. I'd like to start by just making absolutely clear for the Court that the government is not defending the Ninth Circuit's case law on Rule 704(b).
So, on the mirroring situation the Court has talked about, there's a Ninth Circuit case from 2005 called Younger, where the Ninth Circuit says that evidence is admissible because it does not particularly speak to the defendant, with "the defendant" in italics in that opinion.
So the government there is moving away from the Ninth Circuit's view.
I don't fully understand exactly what the government is saying about what we've called 100 percent testimony or "always" testimony, but I do want to absolutely stress those are real-world examples.
They're at page 23 of our brief.
That's the exact testimony that agents like the expert in this case gave in cases like this until 2013, when the government discovered there actually were some blind mules out there.
So it's merely a situation of this particular scenario where the government has backed off it for empirical reasons.
But you could have other cases where an expert would testify in the real world that people with schizophrenia can never tell right from wrong, that executives in corporations, when they lie to government investigators, always know they're telling a lie.
That would be perfectly real-world examples. And I think, regardless of what the government's precise position is, it's very difficult to argue that kind of testimony would be okay under Rule 704(b).
Let me give you one last scenario about the government's line-drawing problems.
There's a case called Watson from the Third Circuit that's in our brief.
The government itself seems to agree with the outcome in Watson.
In that case, the prosecutor asked the expert: Do you have an opinion as to whether or not the defendant here had the requisite intent?
The expert says: Yes, I have an opinion. People like this generally have the requisite intent.
The government seems to agree that is out under Rule 704(b).
Now, maybe that's because, at pages 28 to 30, it draws a contextual rule of some kind.
But there are these very difficult line-drawing problems under the government's rule.
Our rule is simple.
If you talk about the defendant herself, it's covered by Rule 704(b), or a class of people including the defendant, you're covered by Rule 704(b).
Here, the class of people, the expert is quite specific—people carrying large quantities of drugs across the border.
That's the class.
The defendant here is unquestionably a member of that class.
And so his testimony is about the defendant.
I want to say one last thing.
Some of the Justices asked about some of the history and intent of Rule 704(b).
Let's just use the Hinckley case.
And the Senate report is only a page long.
I would urge you to read that if you think that's important here.
And what the Senate said was we're concerned about experts testifying about the subject of mens rea.
And so after the Hinckley trial, if an expert were to testify, we think people like this who are exhibiting these conditions generally couldn't tell right from wrong or probably couldn't tell right from wrong or in most circumstances couldn't tell right from wrong, it seems unreasonable to think Congress would have thought that was okay under the Hinckley trial.
The problem in the Hinckley trial was that the expert was testifying not just about facts from which a jury could infer mens rea, but the expert was expressing an opinion on the ultimate issue of mens rea from which the jury could go into the jury room and do nothing but say, oh, we agree with the expert.
And whether the expert expressed that opinion in terms of probably this defendant had the mens rea or didn't have the mens rea or certainly, the problem is that the jury can go back to the jury room and say: Look, sounds like the expert thinks people like this generally have the mens rea. He must be right. We'll go along with that.
The point of the right to a jury trial and the point of the ultimate issue doctrine is that when it comes to the special subject of mens rea—and I want to be really specific here—mens rea as the element of the crime to convict or as the defense, that particular subject has a wall around it.
The jury is required to make an independent moralistic, qualitative determination.
And that's what the expert did wrong here.
He said most people like this know they have drugs in their car.
Knowledge is the exact element of the crime.
So whatever else Rule 704(b) may cover in terms of statements that cover mens rea, this one explicitly did, and that's why it went over the line.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
The case is submitted.

Judge: Right. And you kind of lost control. So, why didn’t you go knock on her door and tell her, "Hey, um, I hate to tell you this, ma’am, but I ran into your gate."
Defendant: That wasn’t going to happen at 13. Uh-uh. Nope.
Judge: Why not, Christopher? Why not?
Defendant: I don’t know.
Judge: You were scared.
[The conversation continues in similar format, discussing liability, responsibility, and ultimately concluding with the judge’s verdict.]
Judge: I’m not going to issue a verdict against you because you have no parental liability in this circumstance. But I am going to issue that verdict against you, Christopher. Okay? Because you’ve admitted that that was something that you did. It’s fine. It’s an accident. But when we have accidents, we’ve got to make it right, and there’s the last $100 to make it right, right? And I’m sure that they can afford that $100, but it was important to them, I think, to make sure that you understand that you’ve got to make it right. Okay? You’re a nice young man, so go make it right. Work this summer and pay off that $100. Verdict for the plaintiff: $100. Good luck.
[Final thoughts from the participants and concluding statements by the judge and courtroom commentary.]
Judge: That will do it for this case. Litigants for the next case on the way into the courtroom right now.
Manipulation Techniques: evasion, emotional appeal, playing the victim

Dialogue:
JUDGE: This is the plaintiff, Shareif Ahmed. He says he was stopped at a seatbelt check on the Parkway, and just as the officer gave him the all-clear, he was rear-ended by the defendant. The defendant told him he was sorry because he was talking to his mother and promised to pay for his damages. But it’s been almost six months, and well, here they are. He’s suing for $2,680.02, the cost to repair his damage.
JUDGE: This is the defendant, Jose Vega. He says the plaintiff was inching up and then just locked his brakes up, and he tapped his bumper. The plaintiff took all his information but never contacted him. He’s very surprised the plaintiff actually turned around and sued him. He’s suing him for way too much to boot and is trying to milk him for everything he’s worth. Owe him all that money? No way. He’s accused of not watching where he’s going.
BAILIFF: All parties, please raise your right hand.
JUDGE: Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff was rear-ended by the defendant just after a seatbelt check. The defendant says it was just a tap and the plaintiff is a scammer. It’s the case of ‘I Ta That.’ Litigants have been sworn. Your Honor.
JUDGE: Shareif Ahmed, you are suing Jose Vega for $2,680.02 that you say he owes you to repair your car as a result of an accident that you say was his fault. Tell me what happened.
PLAINTIFF: I was coming off the exit seatbelt check. I passed right through.
JUDGE: Seatbelt check?
PLAINTIFF: Yeah, there was a County Police checkpoint making sure everything was legal as you were passing through. If you were doing anything illegal, you’d be pulled over. Right? So I went right through, stopped at the red light, about to go, and I get rear-ended.
JUDGE: Were you stopped, and then they rolled into you? That’s not a reason to hit you, but I’m just asking—why do you think it happened?
PLAINTIFF: I have no idea what happened.
DEFENDANT: What actually happened was, he had pulled up to the light. The light changed, and he started making his way out.
JUDGE: Were you going straight or turning?
DEFENDANT: Right turn. He starts making his way out, and I actually hit him on the turn. He just stopped, so I ended up hitting him.
JUDGE: You didn’t hit him that hard?
DEFENDANT: No, which is why I really don’t feel like I did as much damage as he’s claiming.
JUDGE: Let’s see. Do you have pictures of the damage?
PLAINTIFF: Yes.
JUDGE: Okay, so let’s set it up. The plaintiff is driving, the defendant is behind him. The plaintiff stops in the middle of the turn, and that’s why the defendant hits him. Is that a defense?
PLAINTIFF: Absolutely not. He should have had enough distance behind him to stop.
JUDGE: But the argument is, when you make a turn, you’re supposed to complete it.
PLAINTIFF: Someone could be in the crosswalk, and they could stop just in case.
JUDGE: That’s a good point. Last word?
DEFENDANT: I believe the law says if you rear-end someone, you’re at fault.
JUDGE: What kind of car is this?
PLAINTIFF: A Honda CR-V. He shattered my rear window.
JUDGE: Shattered your window?
PLAINTIFF: Yes. The door frame was bent.
JUDGE: How do you figure you didn’t hit him hard when you shattered his windshield and bent the door frame?
DEFENDANT: I barely have any damage to the front of my vehicle.
JUDGE: Your car was better made. Why haven’t you paid for his damages?
DEFENDANT: I don’t feel like I did that much damage.
JUDGE: But you shattered his windshield.
PLAINTIFF: He admitted fault at the scene, saying, “I’m so sorry, I wasn’t paying attention, I was talking to my mother.”
JUDGE: Why didn’t you call the police?
PLAINTIFF: He begged me not to.
JUDGE: And then he never paid?
PLAINTIFF: Right.
JUDGE: And you found out he had no insurance?
PLAINTIFF: Yes, his insurance expired the day before the accident.
DEFENDANT: I was on my way to pay it.
JUDGE: You were driving on the way to pay for your insurance? Do I look stupid to you? That is not the truth. You had no insurance.
JUDGE: You have zero defense. I find in favor of the plaintiff in the amount of $2,680.02. I hope you learned your lesson.
DEFENDANT: I have.
JUDGE: Take pictures, don’t move the car, call the police. Case closed.
Manipulation Techniques: gaslighting, deflection, emotional appeal, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge: All parties, please be seated.
Judge: Welcome to the People’s Court. This case is about whether the defendant illegally towed the plaintiff’s car and whether the plaintiff is entitled to compensation for the tow fee and radiator damage. Let's begin. Mr. Uden, according to you, you were the unfortunate victim of a wrongful tow that damaged your car. Is that correct?
Plaintiff: Yes, your Honor.
Judge: Where was your car parked?
Plaintiff: I park my car in the housing parking lot. My assigned number is 12.
Judge: And were you legitimately parked there?
Plaintiff: Yes, I pay the rent every year.
Judge: It’s not about paying the rent. Was the spot you parked in assigned to you?
Plaintiff: Yes, this is my parking space.
Judge: Mr. O’Grady, you were asked to tow the car because it was what?
Defendant: We were told it was illegally parked.
Judge: Why?
Defendant: The reason was not given to us. The contractor called us to tow the car.
Judge: Do you have proof that you were authorized to tow the car?
Defendant: Yes, your Honor. Here is the receipt from the housing authority. They stamp it when the tow is requested.
Judge: Is this a government housing project?
Plaintiff: Yes, I have been living there for 40 years.
Judge: So, the housing people asked you to tow the car, and you feel that you were towed inappropriately because you were in your assigned space?
Plaintiff: Yes, I was parked in my spot.
Judge: Did you ever talk to them about it?
Plaintiff: Yes, but they denied responsibility. They said it was my responsibility to check the number. I had the sticker with my plate number and parking number 12.
Judge: So, they are saying you weren’t in number 12?
Plaintiff: No, I was in the right spot. They did not check the windshield sticker.
Judge: Mr. O’Grady, do you know what space you towed him from?
Defendant: That’s between him and the housing authority.
Judge: Mr. Uden, what happened when you got your car back?
Plaintiff: The radiator was leaking. I took it to a garage, and they replaced it.
Judge: Do you have the receipt for the radiator repair?
Plaintiff: Yes, here it is.
Judge: That’s what you’re suing for, correct? The $415 for the radiator and the tow fee?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Mr. O’Grady, if you damaged the radiator, you would be responsible for replacing it. How do you respond?
Defendant: Towing does not normally damage a radiator. I provided a video showing how we tow vehicles properly.
Judge: Mr. Uden, do you have evidence that the towing caused the damage?
Plaintiff: The car was not leaking before. After the tow, it started leaking.
Judge: But do you have documentation from the mechanic stating that the tow caused the damage?
Plaintiff: No, they did not give me anything specific.
Judge: Mr. O’Grady, how do you tow cars?
Defendant: We use a standard wrecker with a front lift and install dollies on the back wheels.
Judge: Where is the radiator in the car?
Defendant: In the front.
Judge: Do you have any additional evidence linking the tow to the radiator damage?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: What year is your car?
Plaintiff: 1997 Toyota 4Runner.
Judge: That’s an old car. I have to consider whether the radiator failure was due to age rather than towing. Also, you are suing the wrong person. Mr. O’Grady was following orders from the housing authority. If they ordered the tow incorrectly, your issue is with them.
Judge: My verdict in this case is for the defendant.

Plaintiff: She told me that she was pregnant and I was like, 'Okay.' And, 'Whose is it?' And she said, 'Yours.' And then I was like, 'So... What happened to the other guy I've seen? What happened to the other guy I heard about?'
Defendant: It wasn’t no other guy.
Plaintiff: So you still maintain that that was a guy that lived in the backhouse behind your house and that wasn’t another guy he saw leaving your house?
Defendant: Yes.
Plaintiff: And you're saying you weren't involved with anybody else during that time except for me?
Defendant: Yes.
Plaintiff: I saw the guy across the street... I found out there was a guy texting you saying 'I'm outside.' So when I went outside to get the water, I looked across the street and saw the same guy.
Plaintiff: I didn’t say anything, and I probably spent the rest of that day on the couch till the bus came. Bus came, I hopped on the bus, soon as I got on the bus, I sent the message... 'I’ve seen the messages in your phone, but I’ve seen the guy across the street.'
Judge: So what happened to this other guy, Ms. Hall?
Defendant: It wasn't no other guy.

Judge: -- identified the dealers.
Judge: Justice Alito was asking you about specific red flag dealers.
But that paragraph doesn't identify dealers, and it seems to me that that's one of the distinctions between this case and Direct Sales and, for that matter, this case and Twitter.
Let's talk about Twitter.
There was a specific rogue actor, ISIS, and there was a specific attack in France.
And so the attempt was to draw the line between them, and we said it wasn't enough.
In Direct Sales, there was a specific manufacturer, a pharmaceutical company, selling to a specific doctor, causing specific harm.
And Justice Alito asked you what specific red flag dealers there are.
You haven't sued any of the retailers that were the most proximate cause of the harm, and you haven't identified them that I can tell in the complaint.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Justice Barrett, there are many, many paragraphs that specifically identify rogue dealers in the complaint.
If you look at approximately paragraphs 147 to 203, they identify specific dealers that have been found to have sold guns in bulk to traffickers that go across the border.
If the question is you haven't named in paragraph 247, which says that these manufacturers know that they're selling to dealers who sell to cartels, I think that is pushing a little bit past what is necessary for drawing reasonable inferences from a motion to dismiss.
But I want to pause too on Twitter and Direct Sales.
Direct Sales involved an entity that was selling large quantities of morphine to a doctor.
The entity was licensed.
The doctor was licensed.
And if you look at Direct Sales, what Direct Sales says is that the quantities that were at issue were in line with that defendant's marketing practices.
There was nothing unusual about the quantities at issue there.
What was unusual in that case and what is different in that case is that that man -- the Direct Sales manufacturer did not know that that specific doctor was a problem.
It had been put on notice that there were other doctors who were selling lots of their products to people who shouldn't be getting them, but unlike this case, where these manufacturers and the distributor are alleged to know who the dealers are and what problems they are causing, the -- the manufacturer that was held liable for criminal conspiracy in Direct Sales didn't know anything about that doctor.
All it knew was that the doctor kept sending them legal order forms and they kept fulfilling the orders.
That was Direct Sales.
This case is much like Direct Sales, if not stronger, for that reason.
Judge: What do you do with the suggestion on the other side and in the amicus briefs that your theory of aiding -and -abetting liability would have destructive effects on the American economy in the sense that, as you've read in the briefs, lots of sellers and manufacturers of ordinary products know that they're going to be misused by some subset of people? They know that to a certainty, that it's going to be -Subject to Final Review pharmaceuticals, cars, what -- you can name lots of products.
So that's a real concern, I think, for me about accepting your theory of aiding -and -abetting liability. And, relatedly, you've referred often to the motion to dismiss.
Of course, as you're well aware, getting past that is often the whole thing.
So I don't think we can just rely on the motion to dismiss.
But the broader point, I'd be interested in your reactions, how we rule for you but don't cause that problem that is identified with great force in the briefs.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Sure.
So let's take Budweiser as an example.
As you heard Mr. Francisco say today, if Budweiser had a practice, was alleged to have a practice, of selling bulk quantities of Bud Light to liquor stores that were arranged next to high schools and it was selling more and more into those high schools, knowing that those liquor stores were regularly serving underage students, and, in fact, Bud Light designed it to put out a new can that says Best Prom Ever and sold it right into that high school, that is the allegations in this case.
If you have a product manufacturer of a dangerous product that is alleged to have done all of the things knowing who they're selling to and what is being done with that product, then and only then, I think, that product manufacturer doesn't --has a problem. If you look at the examples that are given in PLCAA that aren't at issue in this case, the examples that the congressmen and senators were concerned about in PLCAA were when a car dealer sells a car to someone who later drives drunk, when Campbell's is sued because someone is killed with a soup can.
Those are the things that PLCAA was concerned about.
This case is -- marches through in detail allegations taken as true at this stage that these manufacturers know that they are selling a dangerous product to specific rogue dealers who are -- who are selling to straw purchasers for the cartels across the country.
Judge: What if a --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: That's --
Judge: -- beer -- what if a beer --I'm sorry.
I cut off your sentence.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: That's okay.
Judge: What if a beer manufacturer knows that the per capita beer sales in a small college town are, you know, 50 times more than another town without a college there? Is that enough?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: I don't think that alone would be enough.
I mean, you -- you do have allegations in this complaint that the -- the number of dealers that have arranged themselves along the border of Texas and Arizona, of Mexico, are vast.
I don't think that itself would be enough.
It would be you know the dealers are there, you know what they're selling, you know who the bad apple dealers are, because we're not talking about every dealer in the country.
We are talking about a small percentage of retailers responsible for about 90 percent of the crime guns that are found.

Judge: I'd like to give you an opportunity to respond to the arguments raised, I think primarily in the brief for the Solicitor General, that requiring retention hearings at the early period that you propose will prejudice procedures under the civil forfeiture regime.
Judge: Yeah, but the Solicitor General elaborates that there are all sorts of procedures necessary to support forfeiture that will be compromised by a somewhat repetitive hearing—or whatever the precursor to make the other one repetitive is—that will require either ignoring those interests or compromising them, including such basic things as preservation of the property itself.
Judge: Thank you.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
Judge: The— in Neumann, there was a petition for remission. How similar is your retention to that?
Judge: Well, I understand that, but it seems as though a proceeding, short of the forfeiture proceeding’s determination in Neumann, the Court said it was unnecessary to sustain constitutional stature of the forfeiture. It wasn’t—you did not need that intervening process of remission.
I don’t—and I don’t see how that’s different from your intervening retention proceeding.
Judge: Thank you.
Judge: Well, you just said that it’s not discretionary. What if it were discretionary?
Judge: Yes.
Judge: Could the state create an innocent owner defense but say that it can only be adjudicated at the final forfeiture hearing?
Judge: Well, some of my colleagues may not be interested in this question, but I am interested in this question. You have asked us to say that the Constitution requires this thing called a retention hearing, so I would just like to know what is this thing that you are asking us to recognize?
So how soon? What happens at it? How is it practicable for the police? And why is it necessary for the owner?
Judge: Well, let me just interrupt you, because the last argument in your brief says that Alabama violated the Petitioners’ rights by failing to provide a retention hearing. Anyway, assume that that is part of the question. Go ahead.
Judge: Well, let’s just take what might be sort of a typical case. So a car, similar to the facts in one of these cases, that a car is stopped by the police, they find a large quantity of meth in the car, the person driving the car is not the owner of the car, the person driving the car is the spouse or domestic partner of the owner.
And then, within a short period of time, there’s this innocent owner defense, and the owner of the car, I suppose, testifies, “I had no idea this was going on.” And then what do you think it is reasonable to require the state to do in that situation?
Judge: Does that happen in New York City, perjury prosecutions under those circumstances? Do you know of cases like that?
Judge: All right. Thank you.

Judge: Mr. LeBlanc, who do you really want to be with?
Defendant: I love my wife, Your Honor. I want to be with my wife.
Judge: Then why are we here today?
Defendant: Because it’s hard to try to be with my wife, knowing there’s a possibility these other kids are mine.
Judge: But whose actions created this situation, Mr. LeBlanc?
Defendant: I understand that, Your Honor, but it’s not easy. My wife is struggling to accept me bringing children into our marriage from outside. And I’m just trying to hold everything together.
Judge: So you’re saying it’s everyone else’s responsibility to adjust, but not yours?
Defendant: It’s not like that. I’m just trying to do the right thing for my wife, my family, and the kids.

Judge: How will this work, living together with these rules?
Defendant: Me and Ms. Bogardus just have to figure something out. Things are complicated, but we’ll sort it out eventually. It’s not that big of a deal, Your Honor.
Judge: Not a big deal? You’re in court over this, and you’re acting like it’s nothing.
Defendant: Well, I just want things to go back to being normal. That’s all I’m asking for.

Judge: I'll mention just as an aside some of the amicus briefs go into the fact that it -- it may well be more difficult to travel on the weekends.
But I guess putting that aside, the second practical reason, the procedural one, is that we're talking about deadlines with severe consequences.
The question of how and when your deadline runs should be easily understood and easily calculated, especially when there are severe consequences.
And on the government's view, you have to undertake a case-by-case determination for each deadline in the regulations and each deadline in the INA.
Maybe I could give an example.
So there's a 180-day deadline in the regulations, Section 1003.23, a 180-day for seeking relief from in absentia removal.
That statutory deadline -- that regulatory deadline doesn't cross-reference the statute.
So you might think, if you pick up the regulation and pick up the definition, you know how it applies.
But the government --
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, but that -- that --in seeking relief, that -- that's requiring you to file some sort of a document.
Judge: That's true, although, as my colloquy with Justice Barrett explained, there are other deadlines in the regulations, like things like getting married and getting fingerprinted.
And the point is that on the government's view, even though that regulatory deadline is facially clear, you need to go to the INA and see if there's a statutory analogue for that 180-day deadline, it turns out there is in Section 1229a.
But even then you're not done.
On the government's view, now you have to construct a family tree for the statutory deadline and the regulatory deadline and see which one came first.
Only then can you figure out if your deadline is on a Saturday or Monday.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Right.
I may understand it.
This is the argument for a uniform rule.
It makes things -- it makes things simpler.
So I --I -- I get that.
I get that.
Judge: Yeah, and I think --
Lawyer of Defendant: Counsel, it's not only that it's simpler, but the INA is filled with provisions like this where the individual's activities are tied to the government's activities, correct?
Judge: That's right.
Lawyer of Defendant: And, here, Section 1302, going back to Justice Barrett's question in part, says that when someone's visiting the United States, if they're going to stay longer than 30 days, it says -- the provision states that noncitizens who remain in the U.S. for 30 days or longer must apply for registration and be fingerprinted before the expiration of such 30 days.
So that's very clear like our 60 days here.
Under the government's theory, I don't know whether they can be fingerprinted by the government on Saturday or Sunday.
We'd have to figure that out, correct?
Judge: Right.
Lawyer of Defendant: We'd have to figure out whether the agency is open for them to register on Saturday and Sunday, correct?
Judge: Right.
Lawyer of Defendant: So these are the practical difficulties. With respect to the issue here, yes, there's an obligation on the alien to depart, but there's also a responsibility tied to the motion to reopen, correct --
Judge: That's exact --
Lawyer of Defendant: -- which is a court activity?
Judge: That's exactly right.
Lawyer of Defendant: So it's not simply an obligation that's tied only to the individual.
If the individual does something, then the government has an obligation to -- to dismiss their order of removal and give them additional time, correct?
Judge: That's right.
And I think it's telling that the regulations tie this departure deadline to a filing deadline.
It shows that in the government's view at least before, these things maybe aren't that different at all.
But the confusion, as your questioning highlights, there are hundreds of instances of the word "day" in Title 8 of the Code of Federal Regulations and dozens, if not a couple hundred, of instances in the INA. And in the government's view that for each of those, not only -- that the statute and regulations require somebody for each of those to undertake this intricate analysis of statutory and legislative history just doesn't make sense, especially -- I mean, it does -- it's hard enough for lawyers to figure out in an individual case an individual deadline under the government's view, is it Saturday or Monday.
The idea that pro se noncitizens --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Do you know --
Lawyer of Defendant: Are you seriously -- oh, no, go ahead.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: No, go ahead.
Lawyer of Defendant: Do you seriously think that there are people in the position of your client who rely on this and say, well, this is really important for me to get out of the country or withdraw my -- my acquiescence in voluntary departure within 60 days, but, wow, I read this, so I've got another two days? I mean, seriously?
Judge: Seriously.
And let me make three points.

Judge: So, first, there are plenty of immigration organizations that provide guidance to noncitizens about how deadlines apply.
Secondly, that accords with --
Lawyer of Defendant: I mean, if you were providing advice, would you say, okay, you know, you've got the extra two days? Would you?
Judge: I mean, if the government's position is adopted in this case, certainly not, but I think, in the absence of that, everything points --
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, with how, you know, the way things are --were at the time when --when this came into play, would you say, well, you've got the extra two days?

Judge: We will hear argument first this morning in Case 23-334, Department of State versus Munoz.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Last fiscal year, the Department of State issued 11 million immigrant and nonimmigrant visas.
It also refused 62,000 visa applications on the basis of one or more of the inadmissibility grounds in 8 U.S.C. 1182(a), including applications from approximately 5400 noncitizens seeking to live with their U.S. citizen spouses or fiancés.
Under the doctrine of consular nonreviewability, a noncitizen outside the United States cannot obtain judicial review of a consular officer's denial of a visa.
Congress has not provided for that form of review in the INA, and when it added new visa-related provisions in 2002, it reaffirmed that it was not providing an end run around that.
Nor is an end run available when a U.S. citizen family member—here, the noncitizen spouse—wants the noncitizen to be admitted into the United States.
As the Kerry v. Din plurality concluded in 2015, that is not a liberty interest protected by the Due Process Clause, and the U.S. citizen is affected only indirectly by the government's action against the noncitizen.
Thus, the plaintiff cannot challenge the denial of her husband's visa application any more than she could challenge a decision at the end of a removal proceeding that he will be removed from the United States or at the end of a criminal trial that he would be sent to a prison far across the country.
With respect to the second question presented, even assuming that the plaintiff has a sufficient constitutional interest to trigger any judicial review, the Court should at the very least hold that the State Department satisfied the Mandel standard.
The consular officer provided a facially legitimate and bona fide reason by citing a valid statutory ground of inadmissibility, the unlawful activity bar, just as Justice Kennedy's concurrence had found had been the case in Din, where the government cited the neighboring terrorist activities bar.
I welcome the Court's questions.
Judge: Just for clarification, assuming that the plaintiff does, in fact, have a liberty interest, would the consular nonreviewability preclude her winning here?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: We think that it would.
Judge: And how would that work then?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: The only potential exception there would be if there were a constitutional right that this Court has recognized, that that would be the only potential exception.
We otherwise think that the point here is—of consular nonreviewability is that the person whose interest is directly at stake doesn’t have a right to review.
And, a fortiori, it seems that somebody who is only indirectly affected by the government’s decision in that proceeding also cannot challenge that.
And so the second question presented, assuming that she has a constitutional interest, would be that we at least satisfy what—
Judge: Well, I understand that.
I’m just trying to figure, without even getting to Din, whether or not nonreviewability would preclude any—her having any recovery at all, whatsoever, that you wouldn’t even have to get to Din.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Well, the case has been litigated on the presumption that there would be an exception to consular nonreviewability if—if we were to lose on the first question, and that’s what Justice Kennedy’s concurrence said in Din.
Judge: Yeah.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I don’t know what the cause of action is—
Judge: Yeah.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: —that she has in order to bring that.
The Ninth Circuit has been assuming that there is one, I guess, under the Constitution.
We think there is not one under the APA.
And none of the courts of appeals has found that there is one there.
And so we think that there still would be reasons to say that there isn’t going to be any review even in those circumstances, but at the least, we would satisfy Mandel.
Judge: Counsel, the husband has no right of review, you’re correct. And it’s a judicially created exception of nonreviewability.
So, if we make an exception, it’s us accepting something that’s not statutory or constitutionally required.
Her point is, whether it’s her or her husband, they each should have a right to dispute whatever basis it was that formed your denial.
And let’s assume it was something as simple as misidentification.
You thought he was John Doe, and you had a whole criminal record on John Doe.
And he’s not John Doe; he’s John Smith, John Smith Doe, which is another person.
So the reason she’s asking for an explanation and one that’s required is that she says: I have a right equal to the Mandel professor’s.
They had a First Amendment right to invite a professor to come speak to them.
I have an equal constitutional right to live with my husband if I can.
The second question is, is that a liberty interest? And you say it’s not.
So let’s take each of your points, okay? As far back as 1888, this Court said in Maynard that getting married is something more than a mere contract, which is your position in your brief, that it’s only statutory rights or benefits.
In 1923, this Court described in one breath the right “to marry, establish a home, and bring up children.”
And that right is one long recognized at common law as essential to the orderly pursuit of happiness by free men. That’s our Meyer case.
In Loving v. Virginia, we said marriage is fundamental.
In United States v. Windsor, we said marriage is more than a routine classification for purposes of certain statutory benefits.
So—and Obergefell, I’m not going to cite it, but it said something to that effect.
So, if I assume that there’s a liberty interest that has to be protected by some sort of process, then the question is, what kind of process is enough?
And in Mandel and in Nken, we talked about the statutory—citing at least a statutory basis for exclusion.
Here, you’re saying she’s entitled to nothing.
Why do we have to go that far? Why don’t we just address, given all of our centuries of statements about marriage being something more?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: We don’t disagree that marriage is an important right and that she has liberty interests that are implicated there.
To step back to the beginning of your question, we think that the doctrine of consular nonreviewability is rooted in the political branch’s plenary power to determine which noncitizens should be admitted to the country and what procedures are going to be used in order to make that determination.
And so that’s not just a judicial exception to general principles of reviewability.
That’s the background against which Congress enacted the INA.
It provided for certain remedies in review and did not provide for this.

JUDGE: This is the plaintiff, Jean Claude Felder. He says he brought his truck into the defendant's shop for a tune-up. A few days later, it overheated, and he brought the truck back and was charged another $1,500 for some additional repairs. It needed a few days later, it overheated again, and when he brought it back, the defendant told him he now needed a new engine. A new engine? Why did this guy charge him thousands of dollars and string him along when he knew he was going to need a new engine? He is suing for $5,000, the amount he's owed.
JUDGE: This is the defendant, John Sa. He says the truck had almost 200,000 miles on it and was in bad shape when the plaintiff brought it in. The plaintiff drove it another 400 miles after he made the repairs, then he blew the head gasket on the engine. Well, he's sorry, but he has no way of knowing when an engine is going to blow. The truck is 16 years old, and he's not responsible for paying the plaintiff a penny. He's accused of making unnecessary repairs.
JUDGE: All parties, please rise. Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket—the plaintiff brought his truck to the defendant’s shop, says he was totally scammed, overcharged, and the car got screwed up to boot. The defendant says it had 200,000 miles—give me a break. It's the case of blowing a gasket. Thank you, Douglas.

JUDGE: Jean Claude Felder?
PLAINTIFF: Yes.

DEFENDANT: Yes.

PLAINTIFF: Yes.
JUDGE: Okay, how many miles does it have?
