JUDGE: What do you do that you drive so much?
PLAINTIFF: I’m a plumber.
JUDGE: All right, so what happened?
PLAINTIFF: So first, I got the tune-up. Then, over the weekend, it broke down, it overheated.
JUDGE: That was not a problem you were having before?
PLAINTIFF: No, I had no problems before. No overheating, nothing at all.
JUDGE: So the car overheats, and then what?
PLAINTIFF: So it overheats. I called John. I said, "John, my car’s overheating." He said, "Okay, bring it back." So I brought it back to him Monday. He kept my truck for five days. I kept calling. I said, "John, everything okay?" "Yeah, yeah, I’m doing more work. I’m doing more work to it." On the fifth day, I got it back. He charged me $1,500.
JUDGE: But during that time, he’s telling you what he’s going to change or do, right?
PLAINTIFF: Yeah.
JUDGE: And what was it that was wrong with the car?
PLAINTIFF: He changed some parts in the truck, like the head gasket and some other parts.
JUDGE: What did you do the first time?
DEFENDANT: It was a tune-up.

DEFENDANT: The intake manifold gasket was leaking antifreeze, filling up in the crankcase.
JUDGE: Does that have anything to do with your original repair?
DEFENDANT: No.
JUDGE: Were you anywhere near those parts in your original repair?
DEFENDANT: No.
JUDGE: Do you feel that this truck, with 190,000 miles, at some point isn’t worth fixing?
DEFENDANT: When the truck came back the third time, the head gasket was blown. That is when I stopped and told Mr. Felder—
PLAINTIFF: Hold on one second.
DEFENDANT: I told Mr. Felder that it’s beyond repair, it’s going to keep breaking. Get an engine if you want to keep the truck.
JUDGE: Okay. The first time you brought it was what date?
PLAINTIFF: January 29.
JUDGE: And then you had to bring it back when?
PLAINTIFF: I brought it back that Monday. I don’t know the exact date.
JUDGE: Do you have paperwork for every trip?
PLAINTIFF: Yeah, I have every—yeah.
JUDGE: Can you pull the paperwork?
PLAINTIFF: Yes.
(The conversation continues with back-and-forth discussion about repairs, mechanical issues, and evidence presented in court.)
JUDGE: So here’s what’s going to happen. I’m going to order him to return a portion of that bill—$800. Verdict for the plaintiff.
(Verdict is delivered, and reactions from both parties are given.)
JUDGE: Good luck, folks. Both sides.
REPORTER: So the plaintiff gets $800 back, not the $5,000 he was seeking. Mr. Sa, how do you feel about this?
DEFENDANT: I respect the judge’s decision. However, the problem with the vehicle was the intake manifold gasket at the time I examined it. Eventually, the car came back with a head gasket issue, which is a different issue. Ultimately, it’s an old car and is due for a new engine.

Judge: Justice Gorsuch? Justice Kavanaugh?
Judge: I think you said it's a judgment call and a matter of degree. A 10 percent state law, would that be significant interference?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: So, if it's non-discriminatory -- I'm assuming for purposes of the hypothetical it would be non-discriminatory, although I think requiring that it be non-discriminatory makes it particularly unlikely that a state would ever do something like that.
Judge: I understand.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: But indulging the hypothetical, then it would -- we'd be exactly where we are now. It's a question of significant interference, and it would be a question of degree.
Judge: Judgment call for whom? I guess for us, for the nine of us to just decide?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, the question as to what "significant interference" means is ultimately a legal question, and it turns on what the actual practical on-the-ground impact is. And if the bank in that scenario said, look—
Judge: If it's a judgment call, who's making the judgment call? Are we making the judgment call or the court of appeals?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: It ultimately would be a legal question. And, Justice Alito, you asked earlier about the standard of review. That would be de novo. I mean, to the extent that it rested on factual findings, you know, that would be a different standard. But the ultimate legal question of significant interference is for a court and ultimately, you know, subject to review by this Court.
Judge: And I guess I'm going to go back to Franklin then and say, well, we're not just doing this -- we're not totally at sea when we have to do this under your approach. Franklin says some limits on your advertising and how you describe your product. That is significant interference. And you agree that that's correct?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: I think that's a way to understand that case. And so, if you wanted to give guidance to lower courts, you could use Franklin National Bank as an example, just as the Barnett Bank Court did in its opinion.
Judge: And I guess, here -- I mean, this -- maybe this is for remand or for us, but telling a bank not how you describe your product in your advertising, but you actually have to pay money that you wouldn't otherwise pay, I mean, that's—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well—
Judge: -- much more direct interference with the operations of the bank, it seems to me. Maybe you have an explanation for that.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, then Bank of America, it has -- you know, would be able to, you know, try to carry its burden of establishing that standard on remand.
Judge: Isn't that just -- I mean, do you want -- you'd need a trial. That's just common sense, isn't it?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Yeah.
Judge: To tell -- tell someone you have to pay out large sums of money collectively, rather than how you describe your product in your advertising, isn't one more significant interference than the other?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: No. So I'll take the Franklin side of that question first if I may. So just to be clear about the law in Franklin, it went well beyond advertising and it disabled banks from even being able to use the word "savings" in their deposit slips, anywhere in their bank offices. You know, it just eradicated the word or any of its equivalents from the premises of the bank. And I think, you know, what made -- you might think about that as posing a First Amendment problem today. It was also a discriminatory law that gave certain state institutions the ability to use that word. And so it posed a number of distinct problems, but I think ultimately, too, it posed a conflict with the text of the federal statute because the state in that scenario sought to significantly interfere with the exercise and express statutory power that Congress granted.
Judge: The advertising was not an express power. The advertising the Court made clear was an incidental power.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Right, but the power that I think ultimately the Court focused on was the express power to accept savings deposits, and in particular, the use of the word "savings," I think, was critical to the Court's analysis.
Judge: Right. And, here, the express power is the lending and the incidental power is the escrow accounts, correct?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: The way the Bank of America articulates the power, we're not disputing their articulation of the power for purposes of this Court's decision. It is the power to offer mortgage escrow accounts to consumers.
Judge: Mm-hmm.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: So the question is the extent to which the law significantly interferes with that power.
Judge: Do you still think McCulloch v. Maryland was correctly decided?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Yes. We have no issue with McCulloch, and it goes a long way to answer that question.
Judge: And why is that correctly decided and this different?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: So we point to this in our brief, but there are a couple of key distinctions. That case involved a discriminatory tax on the Second Bank of the United States. And at that time, the Second Bank of the United States functioned more like the Federal Reserve Bank, and it had a really public-facing component. Modern national banks don't really resemble the Second Bank of the United States. And the laws that we have as in this case are not discriminatory laws. And, in any event, it's a question of preemption and it's ultimately Congress that lays down the standard, and the standard is "prevents or significantly interferes with."
Judge: Thank you.
Judge: Counsel, you're drawing a distinction which I also saw in your brief between express powers and incidental powers. Can you just explain to me why that matters? And I'll tell you why I'm thinking about it. It almost sounds to me -- and correct me if I'm wrong -- that you're saying that if a power is express, something more like a control test might apply just as a matter of economic logic, but that if it's incidental, we get into this more fact-specific inquiry. Am I understanding your position?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: I think you're right to point out that we do underscore the fact that this is an incidental power so that Congress hasn't said anything specific on this subject. And, indeed, it's a kind of second-order incidental power that is at issue, which is not just the ability to have the accounts but then, you know, to set the interest rate. And so I think the reason why we're focusing on that is preemption questions typically turn on what Congress says in the text of the statute, and so you want to look at the text of the statute.
Judge: Well, I mean, I do agree with that, but you've characterized Barnett Bank as an express conflict, but Barnett Bank goes out of its way to say we don't have an irreconcilable conflict there. It wasn't that the federal statute said, do X, and the state statute said not X. And so it was about significant interference.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, so in Barnett Bank, you're right that there was an impossibility preemption. It wasn't impossible for the bank to comply with both the federal and state statute. But the Court did say there was an express conflict based on the text of the statute. The Court left no indication that if the law were less than a complete prohibition, it would automatically be preempted under the control test.

Judge: So you taped money all over it?
Plaintiff: Yes, as a joke, because I told my wife the day before that money didn’t grow on trees.
Judge: So you say, "Pick a branch, any branch?"
Plaintiff: Pretty much. I told my wife to get a few twenties off the tree.
Judge: Had he seen the tree before?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: What did your wife say when this was happening?
Plaintiff: When Sean said he needed to borrow money, he promised to pay it back the next morning when his mother got off the boat from The Vineyard.
Judge: Did you get paid back the next morning?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: Has it been paid back at all?
Plaintiff: No. It’s been a year and a half now.
Judge: Are you kidding me?
Plaintiff: No. And every time I see him, he says "tomorrow, tomorrow." It’s like being in the musical Annie.
Judge: So, Sean, why haven’t you paid him back?
Defendant: I was unemployed.
Judge: For a year and a half?
Defendant: I did get a job, but I didn’t pay him.
Judge: Why not?
Defendant: I don’t know. I see my mother sitting on the other side, so I pretty much know I lost this case.
Judge: Why is your mother sitting on his side?
Defendant: Judge, your guess is as good as mine.
Judge: Has your mother told you to pay him back?
Defendant: Yes. She’s been telling me that for a year and a half.
Judge: (to the mother) Why are you sitting on his side?
Defendant: ’s Mother: Because he should have paid him.
Judge: And you never told your son he had to pay him back?
Defendant: ’s Mother: I told him multiple times. And Sean told Brian that I would pay him, but I didn’t even know about it.
Judge: So Sean, you lied about your mother paying him?
Defendant: I never said that. I just told him I’d pay him when I could.
Judge: Did you work in the last year and a half?
Defendant: I started working three and a half months ago.
Judge: So, you could have paid him?
Defendant: I guess.
Judge: You guess?
Defendant: I just can’t believe he took me to court over $60.
Judge: I can’t believe you didn’t just pay him back and avoid coming here. Your mother even told you to pay him. Verdict for the plaintiff. Pay the $60.
Defendant: Fine.
Judge: Case dismissed.

Judge: So it is true you were texting Marissa.
Defendant: Well, I see my messages, but I have an assistant who runs my account, you know. I can't be doing those day-to-day things. I got other things that I like to tend to throughout the day.
Judge: All right. So this additional money that is on your messages on social media, is that you speaking or is that your assistant?
Defendant: That's my assistant speaking on my behalf.
Judge: So it is true.
Defendant: It's my assistant speaking on my behalf.

Defendant: Before I left. She did not, Your Honor.
Judge: So, wait, you go to the movies and have a good date night, and by the end, you're in the bed with your ex-husband?
Plaintiff: Yeah, he didn't want to be sexually active with me, so... That's not what a real wife... So, I was over it. He gives it to other women, but he won't give it to me.
Defendant: Your Honor, I…
Judge: You have sex with other women, but you won't have sex with your wife?
Defendant: She has no factual proof. I have never slept with any other woman outside of our relationship. When did she come back?

Plaintiff: He has only been dating this woman for three months, and now suddenly they're engaged. I think she's a terrible person, and I don’t want my children to be around her. This is about the well-being of my children.
Defendant: This is about the well-being of your children, but you won’t even let me meet them?
Judge: Do you even know her?
Plaintiff: I have met her.
Judge: Answer yes or no. Do you know her?
Plaintiff: Technically, yes, I have met her one time.
Judge: Okay, so you’ve met her one time.
Plaintiff: I know people who are friends with her. I’ve seen, you know...
Judge: So you’ve met her one time?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: And based on that one time of meeting her, you believe she’s a bad influence on your children?
Plaintiff: Yes, I do.
Judge: He should not have to come to court to place himself on child support in order to see his kids, especially when it’s for a reason that has nothing to do with them.

Judge: We'll hear argument first this morning in Case 22-674, Campos-Chaves versus Garland, and the consolidated case.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Thank you, Your Honor, and may it please the Court: Each of the non-citizens in these cases failed to attend a removal hearing after receiving written notice of the time and place of that hearing. Under the rule adopted by the Ninth Circuit, however, the non-citizens can rescind their in absentia removal orders based on a supposed lack of notice. That holding defies text, context, and common sense, and it threatens to unsettle hundreds of thousands of in absentia orders that have been entered over the course of nearly three decades.
In reaching that extraordinary result, the Ninth Circuit misread the statutory scheme and previous Court decisions. The Ninth Circuit is wrong that the omission of time and place information in a notice to appear renders all subsequent notices invalid.
Congress created two distinct forms of notice, and it made both of them potential grounds for in absentia removal. The removal orders in these cases were based on notices of hearing that specified a new time and place for the removal proceedings and that warned the non-citizens of the consequences of failing to attend those proceedings. That’s all the law requires for a notice of hearing to be valid.
As to previous Court decisions, we acknowledge that the Court is not writing on a blank slate when it comes to notices to appear, but those decisions do not decide these cases. The question presented today was not briefed, argued, or necessary to resolve in those cases. The Court's narrow decisions do not create the sweeping defense to removal that the non-citizens here seek. I welcome the Court’s questions.
Judge: Well, counsel, previous rulings seem to work against you, so I think it would be good for you to spend a bit of time on that.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Certainly, Your Honor. The important thing about those rulings is that they were narrow decisions addressing specific questions. One ruling, for example, was about the interaction between the stop-time rule and notice to appear requirements. The statutory text alone resolved that case. Additional analysis in that opinion was largely dicta.
The meaning of “change” was not briefed in that case, it was not argued, and the Court did not have before it the wealth of argument and evidence that we have brought to bear in this case, including dictionary definitions and contextual evidence about the meaning of "change."
Judge: I take the point, but it is a very direct statement. The ruling stated that by allowing for a change or postponement to a new time or place, the law presumes that the government has already served a notice to appear that specified a time and place. This wasn’t a ruling reaching beyond the case—it was just the obvious understanding of the statutory scheme. Even if it’s dicta, it reflected what the majority of justices thought was pretty clear.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Your Honor, if I may address that in two parts. The first statement acknowledges a presumption in the statute that the notice of hearing will follow the notice to appear. We agree with that presumption. But the real question in this case is what happens when that presumption is not met—when the notice to appear did not contain the time and place information.
Now, regarding the statement about the meaning of "change," I acknowledge that this is a difficult statement for my argument, but I do believe it was dicta. It is also incorrect dicta because the Court in that case did not have the evidence that we present now, including dictionary definitions and contextual evidence regarding the meaning of "change."
Judge: Counsel, that seems to beg the question. The finding in that case was that Congress intended notices to be full and complete. Congress knew that the government was issuing notices without specific times and places, and it ruled against that practice. That was the holding. So if you’re arguing that a notice of hearing can stand alone without a proper notice to appear, that runs against the statutory framework.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Your Honor, in this case, we believe we satisfied that requirement by providing a new time and place. The non-citizens had the information they needed to attend their hearings. Even if one insists that a notice of hearing must change a previously set time or place, that requirement is satisfied here because "change" is broadly defined. Moving from an indeterminate time to a determined one is a kind of change.
Judge: So you’re saying no time and place changes to… a new time and place?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Your Honor, what we have done in cases where the original notice lacks a specific time and place is inform the non-citizen that a hearing will occur, and then later provide the specific details. Moving from an unknown to a known time and place constitutes a change within the meaning of the statutory scheme.
Judge: But didn’t you lose that argument in a previous ruling? I thought the Court already rejected that interpretation of the word "change."
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Respectfully, Your Honor, I don’t agree. That ruling only decided whether multiple documents could be combined to create a valid notice to appear. The Court said a notice to appear must be a single document. We do not dispute that point in this case. What’s different here is that Congress created two forms of notice—both of which serve as bases for in absentia removal.
Judge: Can you clarify what you mean by two forms of notice?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Congress established both the notice to appear and the notice of hearing. Both are valid bases for in absentia removal.
Judge: So you believe they operate independently of one another?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: They are independent in the sense that each can serve as a valid basis for removal. The notice to appear initiates the proceeding, but a later notice of hearing with a specified time and place is sufficient under the law. The statute is clear on the requirements for a valid notice of hearing—it must set the time and place, and we did that here.

Judge: So, Ms. Lema, after everything that’s happened, who do you want to be the father of your child? Mr. Scott or Mr. Anderson?
Plaintiff: I want Mr. Anderson to be the father. He is more stable, Your Honor. He’s got his life together. I feel like he’s the one who can provide a better future for my child.
Defendant: Wait, what?
Plaintiff: You know, Mr. Anderson, you have a family. You have children. You’ve got everything together. And I... I just want my child to have a stable life. You’re the one that can give that to her.
Judge: So you’re saying you want Mr. Anderson to be the father because you think he’s more financially and emotionally stable?
Plaintiff: Yes, Your Honor. I know it’s a lot to ask, but he’s the one who can offer a better life for my baby. And I need that stability for her.
Judge: So, are you making this decision for the well-being of your child, or is this about your own desires and circumstances?
Plaintiff: It’s for my baby, Your Honor. I just want what’s best for her, and I know that’s you.
Defendant: I didn’t ask for this! Why are you bringing me into this now?
Plaintiff: I just know you’re the right one... You’re the one who can give her everything.

Judge: This is the defendant, Ronnie Johnson. He says there was no formal agreement about rent, but now that the plaintiff knows he collected a good chunk of money from his car accident, he's got his hand out. The plaintiff has a gambling problem, he needs to cover his losses, and he’s sorry, but he isn’t the plaintiff's personal piggy bank. He’s accused of failing a friend.
All parties, please raise your right hands.
Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: these former friends are in court because the plaintiff says that the defendant was a freeloader who didn't pay rent, came into money, and then didn’t give him anything. But the defendant says there was no agreement to pay rent. It’s the case of "It Ain’t Free."
Daryl Buck, you are suing Ronnie Johnson, your former best friend, for $4,160 that you feel he owes you and refuses to pay. Correct?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: Go on.
Plaintiff: Ronnie and I were best friends for a good 12, 13 years. We were always together. Then he had a car accident, and I picked him up and took him to surgery twice.
Judge: As a result of the car accident?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: And he had to get out of his apartment for—I don’t know why—and he calls me and says, "Daryl, I need a place to stay."
Judge: Were you getting evicted?
Defendant: I wasn’t working, I had no income.
Judge: So that’s a yes?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: So, he needs a place to stay. And then what?
Plaintiff: Well, I have a second bedroom. I have a two-bedroom, two-bath condo in Pompano Beach, and the second bedroom was open. It has a sofa bed in there, and I was expecting him to stay in there. But he came in and just crashed on my couch. Five minutes later, our mutual friend, English Rick, came over with boxes and stuff—furniture and knickknacks—that the moving company never picked up from Ronnie’s place. And they put all that in my second bedroom, which I thought he would stay in.
Judge: Well, why didn’t you set some ground rules? Why didn’t you tell him, "You stay in the second bedroom and get a storage unit?"
Plaintiff: I didn’t know it was happening.
Judge: Well, I know, but when you saw it happening, why didn’t you just say to him, "Listen, I don’t live this way. I don’t want to live among all this stuff. You get a storage room, and you can stay in my second bedroom?" Why didn’t you just do that?
Plaintiff: I, uh, I didn’t think of it.
Judge: Okay. So now you’re living with him for a year and a half almost?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: During that time, was there any agreement between the two of you that he was going to pay?
Plaintiff: The first six months, he was kind of rehabbing from his back, and he said, "Daryl, don’t worry about it. You’re my best friend. Without you, I’d be on the street. Thanks for taking up all your space for me. I owe you. When I get my settlement, I’m going to take care of you."
Judge: "Very well"?
Plaintiff: Very well.
Judge: Which means what?
Plaintiff: He figured it was around $10,000–$12,000.
Judge: How do you figure that?
Plaintiff: That’s what Ronnie was telling me.
Judge: Okay. So, in January, you tell him he has to start paying, and he does pay to live there, right?
Plaintiff: Yes, he did.
Judge: You asked him for $400 a month, and that’s what he started paying?
Plaintiff: We agreed.
Judge: And according to him, you made him promises that you would pay him part of your settlement. Was there ever an amount specified?
Defendant: No, there was no amount specified. I didn’t even know how much my settlement was going to be. I originally thought it would be around $670,000, but it ended up only being $200,000.
Judge: But all those other months before January, did you tell him, "I’m going to take care of you. You’re my best friend?"
Defendant: I may have said something like that, but there was no contract.
Judge: So what happened? How did this friendship end?
Defendant: He locked me out. I had to go to the doctor, get new prescriptions, buy new clothes, and stay in hotels for 20 days until my condo opened up.
Judge: Why did you lock him out?
Plaintiff: He was falling into bushes, and people in my condo had to pick him up and carry him inside. I told him, "Ron, you gotta stop this drinking."
Judge: So you changed the locks?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: You can’t do that. If someone is paying rent, they’re a tenant, and you have to go through the proper eviction process.
Plaintiff: I didn’t know where he was, Your Honor.
Judge: That doesn’t change the law. You should have taken him to court for eviction.
Plaintiff: I have affidavits from people in my condo who saw him passed out in the bushes.
Judge: I’ll read them, but this isn’t a contract. I find in favor of the defendant.
[Music]
Judge: I’m kind of sad that a 12-year friendship is over because of what he feels is a lack of gratitude on your part. But that’s for you two to decide. Verdict for the defendant.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I certainly understand that intuition, and if the Court thought that it were prudent to simply try to rule narrowly here and not dictate broader First Amendment principles, we have no problem with the Court assuming that heightened scrutiny applies.
We think the law easily satisfies it. We do think that intermediate scrutiny is a more appropriate framework for this kind of law that's not directly targeting protected speech. But, in any event, there's a compelling national security interest here, and the law isn't just narrowly tailored; it's precisely tailored.
It's trying to fix the thing that's creating the problem, which is the PRC's involvement and the Chinese government's ability to exercise this control over the corporate entities.
Judge: How are we supposed to think about the two different rationales here and how they interact, the data collection rationale, which seems to me at least very strong; the covert content manipulation rationale, as the hypotheticals have illustrated, raise much more challenging questions for you about how far that goes.
And if that alone -- if you didn't have the data collection piece, you only had the covert content manipulation piece, and then the point that Congress would not have enacted this just based on the data collection rationale alone, just your understanding of how the two arguments fit together.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Sure.
And -- and let me walk through our defense of the data protection rationale and why we think it's a full justification for this law and the Court could stop there and then be responsive to their arguments that somehow the interest in preventing covert manipulation somehow taints it.
So just on data protection, I think that it should be beyond dispute that, of course, our nation has an enormous interest in keeping the sensitive data out of the hands of our foreign adversary.
And it should also be beyond dispute that our foreign adversary has an existing capability through its laws and through the way that these companies are integrated to get its hands on that data.
There is no question that Congress was sincerely motivated by that concern.
There's a whole lead-up to the statute here where the executive branch across two different presidential administrations was expressing concerns about the data problems.
Congress was extensively briefed on those problems.
It passed a companion data protection statute at the same time that was intended to prevent selling data to foreign adversary nations.
The statute is shot-through with protections that I think are key to this concern about closing off the vulnerability of access to the data.
So that's a sincere justification for Congress's desire here to act.
We think it's a compelling interest and it's narrowly tailored.
Then you get to the question of what to do about the fact that there's also this interest in covert content manipulation.
And in the First Amendment context, this Court in cases like Heffron has made clear that once you have a justification that satisfies the First Amendment, you don't need to go further and look at other justifications to decide whether they would independently satisfy First Amendment scrutiny.
So I think it's not necessary for the Court to go on and probe whether it thinks that covert content manipulation itself independently justifies the law.
Now my friends say that's all fine and good, but they think covert content manipulation is just per se illegitimate.
And I honestly don't understand how that argument could carry the day because just imagine if Congress passed a law that said the PRC can't covertly manipulate TikTok.
Obviously, that law's not going to violate any constitutional principle.
It's a laudable goal, I think, for our legislature to protect us from foreign adversary interference like that.
And so there's nothing something -- there's nothing that's inherently impermissible about wanting to guard against that risk.
Maybe you could say that it sweeps in too much protected speech in the way it's operationalized in the Act here, but there's certainly no fundamental taint -- taint or anything akin to racial discrimination to call into question whether Congress could seek to vindicate that as one of many interests.
So I guess, to just kind of bring it all together, what I would say to the Court is they have basically acknowledged that data protection is a compelling interest.
That was Congress's real interest.
It provides a sufficient basis on its own to uphold this law. The Court could say just that and -- and affirm.
Judge: I don't know how we do that unless we accept your argument that the post-divestiture provision that stops them from conferring on the algorithm is not a speech impediment, meaning it -- it's very hard for me to say that it's not motivate -- to decide that question, that it is a speech impediment and one that on its face itself has to be analyzed separately from the data.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: So, Justice, let me begin by saying again that we do think that an interest in preventing any operational agreement between the U.S. subsidiary and ByteDance, which is the relevant provision you're talking about, is justified by data protection alone.
And that includes with respect to cooperation on a content recommendation algorithm specifically because of the concern that it necessitates data flows between the companies.
So I think that as a factual matter, that could justify Congress enacting --
Judge: So, if it's --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: But, to the extent that you think that actually the prohibition on coordinating with respect to an algorithm reflects some kind of impermissible content-based problem with the statute, the statute has a severability clause.
And I certainly don't think that it would give the Court a basis to invalidate this law or to -- or to stop it from operating with respect to all of the provisions that operate to protect data security.
At most, it would suggest that that little piece of the law has to be on its own severed from the rest of how the statute operates.
Judge: How does that affect whether we would apply -- because, assuming it's data protection, then I would think that strict scrutiny wouldn't necessarily apply.
I could understand applying intermediate scrutiny.
But how do we do that with respect to this part, the algorithm issue? How do we get to intermediate scrutiny with respect to that?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: The way you get to intermediate scrutiny there is to recognize that prohibiting foreign adversary control over the operations of the platform, including with respect to the fundamental backbone of the system, is not based on any protected speech or -- or content-based in the relevant sense...

Judge: So you are asking her for child support?
Defendant: Yes, Your Honor. I need to get paid.
Judge: Okay, so how much money does she make a year?
Defendant: 45. 47. thousand.
Judge: Is it 45 or 47?
Defendant: Probably 90. 45, 47, 90.
Judge: You obviously came here to waste my time.
Defendant: No, I came to get paid.
Judge: Okay, so which one is it? Is it 45, 47, or 90?
Defendant: All of them. Sometimes you get 45. Sometimes you get 47.

[Courtroom Transcript]
[Judge enters the courtroom]
Bailiff: All parties please raise your right hand. People's Court is now in session. The Honorable Judge Marilyn Milian presiding. Litigants have been sworn, Your Honor.
Judge: Thank you, Doug.
Bailiff: You're welcome.
---
[Plaintiff’s Case]
Judge: Okay, Miss Savage, you are suing Miss Garo for $1,000, half of your $22,000 deductible as a result of some tree limbs that fell on your car during a storm. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: So on February 25th—
Judge: Of what year?
Plaintiff: Of this year.
Judge: Of 2022?
Plaintiff: Yes, 2022. Around 9:00 a.m., I was asleep and got woken up. It sounded like gunshots, but when I looked out the window, it was branches falling on top of my car. It was a rainy, freezing morning. The trees were frozen, and the branches fell on my car.
I immediately ran outside, and the cops were already there. But I couldn’t file a report because they said it wasn’t a car accident.
Judge: So there was no police report?
Plaintiff: No, I couldn't do it. They said they don't do that.
Judge: Did you take pictures that day?
Plaintiff: Yes, I did.
Judge: Do you have pictures of the damaged car?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma'am.
Judge: Were these pictures taken when the storm happened?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma'am.
Judge: Let’s see them.
[Plaintiff presents pictures of the damaged car]
Plaintiff: These show the damages to my car. And I don't have an American car; I have a German car.
Judge: Your point being?
Plaintiff: That the repair is expensive.
Judge: What year is your car?
Plaintiff: It’s a 2017.
Judge: And the repair cost was $6,000?
Plaintiff: Yes, that’s what my insurance company informed me.
Judge: But you didn’t do the repair, right?
Plaintiff: No, my insurance company did, and they paid $6,000.
Judge: What needed to be repaired?
Plaintiff: I have everything that shows the repair details.
Judge: Let’s look at the repair bill. Now, this tree we’re looking at—there’s frozen ice on it, and that’s what caused the branches to fall, correct?
Plaintiff: Yes, but I initially thought it was a dead tree.
Judge: Cutting into a tree doesn’t kill it. Were there leaves on the tree in the prior November?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: So how long had that tree not had leaves?
Plaintiff: Probably a year before.
Judge: So the tree had been dying since you moved there?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And the tree is right on the property line, correct?
Plaintiff: Yes, that’s correct.
Judge: And you confirmed that with a land survey?
Plaintiff: Yes, I did a land survey after she mentioned it.
Judge: So once you found out, you asked her to split the cost because the tree was dead?
Plaintiff: Yes.
---
[Defendant’s Response]
Judge: Miss Garo, what is your response?
Defendant: The plaintiff came to me five months after the storm, trying to extort three grand for some so-called damage to her car. She’s just trying to get money. The tree branches didn’t even touch her car. She never mentioned anything until five months later, and I refuse to be ripped off.
Judge: The plaintiff says the tree branch came down on her car during a storm, and you own half the tree. So she believes you owe her half the cost of the repair.
Defendant: The branches fell, but there was no damage to her car.
Judge: But there is damage to the car. Do you have security camera footage?
Defendant: Yes, but my camera is far away.
Judge: This picture shows damage. Don't you think?
Defendant: Yes, Your Honor, but the damage could be from before.
Judge: The police were there, but it’s not a criminal matter. You see there’s damage, right?
Defendant: Yes, but how do I know the accident wasn’t from before?
Judge: Do you have evidence that the damage was pre-existing?
Defendant: No, but I don’t believe I should have to pay.
---
[Judge’s Verdict]
Judge: Trees falling in a storm are considered an act of God unless it’s a dead tree. If it’s dead, it becomes a homeowner’s responsibility. Miss Garo, did you ever contact the city about the tree?
Defendant: Yes, multiple times. But the city only takes care of branches touching power lines.
Judge: Do you have proof of these calls?
Defendant: Only text messages.
Judge: And your boyfriend didn’t know it was his tree?
Plaintiff: No, not until later.
Judge: The tree is clearly shared property. The issue is whether you, Miss Garo, were responsible. You live in a town where you could hire someone to cut the tree. I understand you thought the city should handle it, but the fact remains—it was a dangerous tree.
Because it was a known risk and a shared tree, I find you half responsible for the deductible. I rule in favor of the plaintiff for the amount of $1,000.

JUDGE: All parties, please stand. Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket, the plaintiff hired the defendant, a process server, but the papers were never served, and he wants his money back. The defendant says he served the papers, so pound sand. It’s the case of revenge is best served in court. Thank you, Douglas.
JUDGE: Okay, William Railly.
PLAINTIFF: Yes, ma’am.
JUDGE: You are suing Mark. You’ve asked me to name you only by your first name and his process-serving business for $645. You want the amount you paid him returned to you. You want one day’s pay, and you want the cost that you paid a new process server. You want both those… What happened?
PLAINTIFF: On or about January 25th, I contacted the defendant and his company and discussed the possibility of hiring him in regard to doing a service.
JUDGE: Had you ever used him before?
PLAINTIFF: I have not.
JUDGE: Did he come recommended?
PLAINTIFF: Yes, someone recommended me to him. We discussed it.
JUDGE: What kind of lawsuit was this?
PLAINTIFF: It’s a civil court case in Queens County, vehicle accident, leaving the scene. It took a while to find out who the individual was who was responsible for the accident. We finally got that information, started the process, and hence I needed a server to proceed with it.
JUDGE: And so you hire him when?
PLAINTIFF: On or about January 25th, 2012. We discussed. I think the amount was $180. We agreed on that. Same date, I sent an email to him, basically sending him the copies of the documents needed to be served. I have a copy of the email if you’d like to see that.
JUDGE: Yes, please.
PLAINTIFF: And here’s the copy of the—
JUDGE: So what goes wrong?
PLAINTIFF: On or about—let me see now—
JUDGE: Why does everybody say that, ‘on or about’? Y’all watch too much TV. When did you do it?
PLAINTIFF: I apologize for that.
JUDGE: That’s all right, everybody does it. It’s kind of funny. Go ahead.
PLAINTIFF: I contacted Mr.—excuse me, the defendant—on April 6th as well as April 23rd to find out where we were in the service of the case.
JUDGE: You waited that long? That was kind of patient.
PLAINTIFF: I figured 120 days, give or take.
JUDGE: Which is about either when it would expire or it had expired at that point, right?
PLAINTIFF: Right. So I had again, as well, sent him the email asking an update or request. I have a copy of that email as well.
JUDGE: Yes. Is he answering your emails?
PLAINTIFF: No.
JUDGE: What about phone calls?
PLAINTIFF: Phone calls, I think we spoke twice on the phone. Once initially, to set up the—
JUDGE: Yeah, but now are you calling him and telling him, ‘What up? I gave you this January 25th, have you served?’ Are you calling him?
PLAINTIFF: Mhm.
JUDGE: Okay, and no answer?
PLAINTIFF: No answer.
JUDGE: All right, so what do you do? You hire a different process server?
PLAINTIFF: I wanted to wait and give the defendant the benefit of the doubt to see where we were.
JUDGE: Doubt? The guy’s not even calling you back.
PLAINTIFF: Well, that was true, but, you know, I don’t know what’s going on, so I figured let me proceed a little bit more.
JUDGE: You don’t know what’s going on because he won’t let you know what’s going on.
PLAINTIFF: Yeah, that is true. On June 23rd, I receive a phone call—on June 23rd, I receive a phone call from the defendant as well as an email.
PLAINTIFF: I am still awaiting payment for the service of process on [case name]. Please advise of payment as service was completed a while ago, and I have a copy of this.
JUDGE: Okay, so did you say to him, ‘Dude, here are all the emails I’ve been contacting you with, and you didn’t tell me anything?’
DEFENDANT: That was it.
JUDGE: And I don’t understand—if service, if they’ve achieved service, don’t you have a court date?
DEFENDANT: I believe the court sets the date, but I don’t know the process in which the court acts.
JUDGE: How does it work?
DEFENDANT: It depends on which court it is, Your Honor. Some courts will docket it very quickly and send you notification. Some courts will wait almost a year or more to give you a court date, and some will even not even give you a trial date or court date; they’ll just refer you to immediate arbitration.
JUDGE: So did you serve this pro—did you?
DEFENDANT: I did. I served it on April 23rd.
JUDGE: That’s the date of his second reminder email to you, three months later.
DEFENDANT: Well, that is correct.
JUDGE: How did you serve it?
DEFENDANT: I served it by personal service pursuant to New York law, by delivering it to the residence, and a person of suitable age and discretion took it on the person’s behalf.
JUDGE: Who was that?
DEFENDANT: That was the doorman of the building.
JUDGE: The doorman? You left it with the doorman?
DEFENDANT: Yes, Your Honor. That is an accepted method of service under New York law.
JUDGE: Did you try serving it directly to the defendant first?
DEFENDANT: I attempted to, yes. I went to the address multiple times, and she was not available.
JUDGE: And you have an affidavit of service?
DEFENDANT: Yes, I do.
JUDGE: Let me see it.
[DEFENDANT hands over the document]
JUDGE: [Reading] This affidavit states that you left the papers with the doorman. Did you follow up with a mailing?
DEFENDANT: Yes, I mailed a copy to the same address the next day, as required.
JUDGE: Did you notify the plaintiff that you had served the papers?
DEFENDANT: I did not immediately notify him.
JUDGE: Why not?
DEFENDANT: I was waiting for confirmation that everything was properly processed before reaching out.
JUDGE: But he was reaching out to you, multiple times, asking about the service.
DEFENDANT: I understand that, Your Honor, and I regret not responding sooner.
JUDGE: Mr. Railly, when you finally got this email from the defendant, what did you do?
PLAINTIFF: By that point, I had already hired another process server because I had no way of knowing if the service was actually done.
JUDGE: Did the second process server successfully serve the papers?
PLAINTIFF: Yes, and they provided immediate confirmation.
JUDGE: Okay. So, Mr. Mark, why should he pay for a service that he had to redo because you failed to communicate?
DEFENDANT: Your Honor, I did perform the service. It was completed, and I have the affidavit to prove it.
JUDGE: But you didn't communicate that to your client. He was left in the dark. Do you understand why that’s a problem?
DEFENDANT: I do, but the service was legally completed.
JUDGE: That’s not the issue. The issue is whether he got what he paid for—a timely, professional service that kept him informed. And from what I’m seeing here, he didn’t.
PLAINTIFF: Exactly.
JUDGE: I’ve heard enough. Here’s my ruling…
JUDGE: Mr. Mark, you were paid to perform a service. The service includes not only the act of delivering the papers but also keeping the client informed. You failed in that regard.
DEFENDANT: But the papers were served, Your Honor.
JUDGE: That’s not enough. Your client had no confirmation of that service. He repeatedly contacted you, and you ignored him. He had no choice but to hire someone else. That means he paid twice for something he only needed once.
PLAINTIFF: That’s exactly my issue.
JUDGE: And it’s a valid one. Mr. Railly hired you in good faith. You did the job but failed to inform him, which left him in an impossible position. That’s negligence on your part.
DEFENDANT: Your Honor, I understand, but I didn’t intentionally ignore him.
JUDGE: Intent isn’t the issue. The outcome is. The question before me is whether he is entitled to a refund. Since he had to pay someone else for the same service due to your lack of communication, the answer is yes.
DEFENDANT: But—
JUDGE: I am ordering you to refund the plaintiff $645. That covers the original service fee, the cost of hiring a second process server, and his lost wages for the wasted time.
PLAINTIFF: Thank you, Your Honor.
JUDGE: Judgment for the plaintiff. Case dismissed.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.
Justice Thomas, anything further? Justice Sotomayor?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer:
Your earlier answer suggests to me that you think this case won't end this matter unless we rule on the government's first ground, that this is a government program in total, correct? Because what you're -- I think what you're saying is we -- if our ruling relies simply on the hundred million, that your next case is going to be: Okay, I'll accept that, but I'm going to fight about whatever the recovery is under the False Claims Act.
That's what you just said to me.
Defendant: 's Lawyer:
I -- I -- I think -- I think what I was -- what I was -- what I was trying to say is the text of the False Claims Act reads that so long as any portion of the money in government -- is government funds.
Our -- our position is that the government doesn't provide a cent of the money at -- at all.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer:
If we say they provide at least a hundred million, is your argument going to be that recovery is limited to that hundred million or that recovery, assuming you go under the False Claims Act, that recovery is limited to a hundred million, or are you going to take this position you're now taking, that the government hasn't suffered any harm so that nothing would come to the government?
Defendant: 's Lawyer:
Well, our -- our -- our position is that the government hasn't suffered any harm.
If you -- if you disagree and you hold that the government has provided any portion of that money, then, Justice Sotomayor, you're right, that then does -- that becomes a damages issue because the --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer:
So this case doesn't end.
Then you're going to argue that their damages have to be limited to a hundred million? What are you going to argue?
Defendant: 's Lawyer:
Well, I think we would -- you know, our position is that there is no fraud here at all.
We would go -- we would go back and -- and have -- have a trial.
Our position is that there's -- there's no fraud here, period.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer:
Assume there's a finding of fraud, that you should have paid more -- you should have given them a greater discount than you did.
So assume you -- you lose the fraud parts.
Are we back to a case where you're going to claim the damages are limited because?
Defendant: 's Lawyer:
Justice Sotomayor, I think, if we're in a situation where we have a trial on the claim, that would depend on what --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer:
Counsel, just answer my --
Defendant: 's Lawyer:
-- like, what the -- what the jury --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer:
-- answer my question.
Defendant: 's Lawyer:
It would be -- it would be what -- whatever the jury finds in terms of damages, the limit on that would be the amount of damages the government sustains, and that -- that would depend on what the jury were to find in that case.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer:
And you're saying there would be no damages because the -- the -- the fund got the hundred million from the government.
Defendant: 's Lawyer:
Our -- our position is no fraud, no damages.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer:
Okay.
Defendant: 's Lawyer:
Thank you, Your Honor.
Judge: Justice Kagan? Justice Gorsuch?
Judge: Ms. Ho, I just want to follow up on that just so I understand.
So assume you lose on the hundred million.
It would be sent back.
Defendant: 's Lawyer:
Yes.
Judge: And there would be a trial.
Defendant: 's Lawyer:
Yes.

Judge: Justice Kagan? Justice Kavanaugh?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer
I want to pick up on what you were saying to the Chief Justice because -- and in some of the colloquy you were having earlier because I think there's an important distinction to be made between what an agency can do or maybe should do as a matter of good government and what the role of the courts is in reviewing what the agencies do. And we start with the deference on the arbitrary and capricious standard, but given the uncertain lines that were reflected in your answers to Justice Barrett and Justice Kagan and Justice Jackson about how far to go, it seems to me the deference of the courts has to be huge with respect to how the agencies think about the scope of what they're going to consider. And it seems to me the problem that has crept in is conflating what the agency can do and should do from what the role of the courts is here.
And by the courts taking an overly aggressive role, it's in turn created an incentive for the agencies to -- to do 3,000-page EI -- you know, environmental impact statements.
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
I think that's absolutely correct, and it's -- it's no coincidence that all the -- almost all of these -- this Court's cases about NEPA have been where a court has required the agency to do more than the agency concluded in its own judgment was necessary. And so I -- I think the -- the Court could accomplish a lot in terms of NEPA litigation by emphasizing the points that you just did against -- again, it's not manufactured -- this Court has said reasonableness is the -- you know, is the standard in measuring how far an agency should go.
And who better than the agency in the first instance, say this is the decision that's before us, this is what I need or what I think I need to -- to consider that, to have considered environmental issues enough to go forward with a decision. One other point about the arbitrary and capricious.
Sometimes agencies treat the EIS as if it's agency action that they should independently review.
The ultimate question is whether the agency action should be set aside because of some defect in the EIS in the end. Analytically, the EIS is not its own agency action.
It's part of the record on which the agency is acting. So, if -- if the court finds some defect in some detail of the -- of the environmental impact statement, not only does that not render the whole EIS invalid, but it -- but you have to ask a further question, should we be setting aside the agency's decision on the basis of -- of something in a document? It's a very important document, but, again, it -- it shows in judicial review some attenuation between what the EIS does and what the agency's substantive decision is.
Judge: And just to underscore something that I think you're going to agree with, the new Act makes it impossible for the agency to -- to do the kind of detail that some courts have demanded.
So --
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
Yeah, it does --
Judge: -- the deference, it's going to be like deference squared with the new Act, it seems to me, but --
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
Well, it does -- it does exempt appendices from that.
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
I know.
I -- you know, I --
Judge: Yeah.
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
Yes.
But -- but I -- but I -- but I think it's like --
Judge: Don't try that here.
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
I think it's like saying we'll get around -- get around the word limit by putting it in the -- but an agency has to be able to -- an agency has to be able to document technically some of its judgments.
So the E -- but I -- but I -- I do think that that would ease the task of judicial review because it necessarily will focus the -- the decision -- the decision in -- in readable, understandable forms and force the agency to give the -- to give its most important reasons.
And a further detail for, you know, some hydrological studies and whatnot, that can be dealt with in the appendix. So I think it -- it can be a useful separation of the agency's explication of its reasons and what should -- what should be the primary focus of the court.
Judge: A last -- last quick thing.
What do you think we should say about Sabal Trail?
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
I think it's a close question, frankly.
And we -- we -- the government argued that -- that FERC was not required to evaluate the emissions at the -- at the other end.
There is a difference between this case and -- and FERC pipelines because, in this case, once -- you know, the STB doesn't control where trains are going to go.
That's all left up to private decision-making. When you have a pipeline, FERC is authorizing the pipeline to take gas from here to here.
And so, if you had -- if you had the power plant right, you know, at the end of the pipeline, it would be sort of hard to say that -- that that's not a -- a indirect effect at least of -- of the pipeline. But we are very concerned, as Mr. Clement is, once you start expanding that to -- in -- in Sabal Trail, there were five refineries it could go to.
And so the courts tend to look at that as a factual question.
If you could figure out where it's actually going to go, you should investigate it.
I don't think that's the right way to look at it or not -- not the full way to look at it --
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
-- because legal responsibility, they're not responsible for which particular refinery it's --
Judge: Thank you.
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
-- it's going to go to.
Judge: Justice Barrett?
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
Just one quick follow-up on that because that was my question too about Sabal Trail.
You know, Mr. Clement said, well, at least what Sabal Trail has come to stand for. And would the government agree that even if Sabal Trail itself was correct for some of the reasons that you say, that what courts have interpreted Sabal Trail to mean is too aggressive?
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
Yes.
No, that -- and that's -- that's what I was -- was trying to convey because that gets you into thing -- that gets you into speculation.
It gets you into other agency responsible.
It gets you into individual decision-makers and -- and all that.
Judge: Justice Jackson?
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
Yes.
So I'm reflecting on your conversation with Justices Kagan and Barrett, and maybe I don't understand how Mr. Clement's test works, but I thought that each element operated independently to narrow the circumstances in which further study would be required. So right next door but within the regulatory jurisdiction of another agency, I thought, would not pass Mr. Clement's test because he had a separate element about it has to be in your own jurisdiction. Is that how you understand?
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
Yep.
I -- I think it could be understood that way.
I'm not -- I'm not sure he means -- he means the hardest form of that.
Judge: But maybe -- maybe he can respond on rebuttal --
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
Yes.
Judge: -- but that's how I thought -- I thought --
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer
That sort of -- that sort of highlights a -- a -- a --
Judge: Highlights the concern, right?

Judge: Christina, what do you have to say about what your sister is saying?
Defendant: Basically, I just feel like we shouldn't even be here. We're sisters. This is a family matter. I don't even know why she even brought me here.

Judge: Ms. Budker, you are suing Dennis Dugan, your former architect, for $800, a return of a deposit that you gave him for some drawings. Mr. Dugan, you’re counterclaiming for the remaining balance that you say is owed for those drawings, which is $1,612. Let me hear from you. You have a home in what town?
Plaintiff: We have a home in Forest Hills Gardens, which is—I brought as an exhibit to show you—a preserved community historic district.
Judge: This is Forest Hills Gardens in New York?
Plaintiff: Yes, in New York.
Judge: All right, so you had a house, and you had a balcony, and…?
Plaintiff: I have a picture of my home with the extension. I filed an application to put a roof over the balcony.
Judge: And when you filed your application to put a roof over the balcony, they told you to get an architect?
Plaintiff: I was initially allowed to file first. I would like to give you a short presentation about how Forest Hills Gardens Corporation works very strictly on architecture and construction procedures.
Judge: I understand, but that’s not in dispute. So, they told you that you need to get an architect and submit proper drawings?
Plaintiff: Not at first. Initially, I submitted a sketch done by Joseph.
Judge: And what did they say?
Plaintiff: Then, I decided that I needed an architect, and I was suggested to hire Mr. Dugan.
Judge: All right. Mr. Dugan, how long have you been an architect?
Defendant: I retired this year.
Judge: How long were you in practice before that?
Defendant: 48 years.
Judge: Oh, my goodness. Did you have a written retainer agreement with her?
Defendant: Yes, may I provide it?
Judge: Yes, please. [Examines document] According to the written retainer, you were charging $200 an hour?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: The agreement states that you would inform her in eight-hour increments of your time and bill her accordingly?
Defendant: Yes, meaning I would notify her before accruing additional hours to prevent costs from getting out of hand.
Judge: And you billed her on a monthly basis?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: So what did you do for them?
Defendant: We had an initial meeting where we signed the contract. They paid my retainer.
Judge: So they paid $800?
Defendant: That’s correct. Then they provided some documents for me to use as a base for my plans, which I would then submit to the architectural committee for approval. I’ve done 12 to 15 submissions for different clients in Forest Hills. Only one has ever been approved on the first try.
Judge: Did you explain that to your clients?
Defendant: Yes, of course.
Judge: The denial from the committee stated that they unanimously rejected the design because they felt it made the house look too heavy. They said it exaggerated the asymmetry of the house.
Defendant: That’s correct.
Judge: When did you send them the bill for the remaining amount? Before or after the rejection?
Defendant: Shortly after, I think.
Judge: Wouldn't it have been a better idea to send the bill before the rejection?
Defendant: Not really. Normally, I get paid as the work is done.
Judge: Did you know it wasn’t going to get approved?
Defendant: I suspected they would have objections, but that’s typical. I assumed we would work through their concerns.
Judge: Did you explain that to her?
Defendant: Yes, I did.
Judge: And what happened?
Defendant: We met again, and she started berating my prior work for another client, calling me incompetent and stupid. I told her we couldn’t continue working together if this was the tone.
Judge: So, you sent your bill after she called you incompetent?
Defendant: I don’t recall exactly.
Judge: Ms. Budker, why do you feel that he should return the $800, and you should not have to pay the remaining balance?
Plaintiff: After our first meeting, we signed a contract for the design phase. According to the guidelines, there was supposed to be a review and suggestions from the board before the formal meeting.
Judge: Where is that written?
Plaintiff: In the rules and regulations.
Judge: Show me exactly where it states that the board will meet with him before he submits the design.
Plaintiff: [Hands over documents] Preliminary review.
Judge: [Reads document] That just outlines submission requirements. When did all this happen?
Plaintiff: 2014.
Judge: So, four years ago?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Mr. Dugan, what is the normal procedure after a denial?
Defendant: They set up a subcommittee to discuss the drawings and figure out modifications needed for approval.
Judge: Did you ever get another architect?
Plaintiff: No, because he never did what we wanted. He submitted his own design without discussing it with us.
Judge: He can’t submit anything without your approval. This is what he drew. Someone altered it with a pen. Who did that?
Plaintiff: My husband.
Judge: Okay, so your husband marked changes, and then he followed them?
Plaintiff: No, he never showed us the final submission.
Judge: Mr. Dugan, is there any requirement for a preliminary discussion with the board before submission?
Defendant: No, that’s not how it works.
Judge: Ms. Budker, you seem to believe there was an informal review required. Where is that documented?
Plaintiff: He promised to do it.
Judge: Let me see the letter. [Reads] "At our meeting yesterday, Ann’s words describing my work and me personally made me realize that you have lost confidence in my ability. Therefore, now is the time to end our agreement and go our separate ways." This suggests he left because of how he was treated, not because he refused to continue the work.
Judge: My ruling is as follows: The plaintiff’s lawsuit for the return of the deposit is denied. Work was performed, and just because the outcome was not what the plaintiff wanted does not mean the defendant does not deserve to be paid. The plaintiff is ordered to pay an additional $800 to Mr. Dugan. Case closed.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket, the plaintiff bought an English bulldog from the defendant, but the dog has hip dysplasia, and the plaintiff wants the cost of the surgery. The defendant says there is no hard proof the dog has a problem. It’s the case of the dog that wasn’t hip.
Judge: Okay, Mr. Boey, you are suing Sharon Nunley, doing business as Nunley Ranch and Nunley Ranch Bulldogs, for $1,600—$1,000 of it the amount you paid for the dog, and $600 for missed work and time spent trying to resolve this lawsuit. Tell me what's going on.
Plaintiff: I bought her when she was four months old. I got—bring her out here.
Judge: You brought her all this way? Hi there. What's the name of that dog?
Plaintiff: Bella.
Judge: Bella, yeah. And what kind of dog is that?
Plaintiff: Old English Bulldog.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: I bought her in October. I bought her for a companion because I go over the road.
Judge: Are you a truck driver?
Plaintiff: Yes, I am.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: All 48 states she’s been to—I believe 27.
Judge: Okay. A well-traveled canine.
Plaintiff: Yes, I got travel glasses for every state we’ve been through since I’ve had her.
Judge: Okay. Do you have kids?
Plaintiff: Yes, I do.
Judge: Okay. How old are they?
Plaintiff: 23 and 18. I got a grandson.
Judge: Okay, all right. I ask that question whenever someone is that devoted to a dog, just out of curiosity. So, go ahead.
Plaintiff: No, she’s my life. I bought her in October. She was four months old. I found out that her hips were no good before she was even a year old. I believe I had her for two months. I can’t get rid of a dog after I get attached to a dog.
Judge: Right. And she had hip dysplasia?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay. Do you have the medical records?
Plaintiff: Yes, I do.
Judge: Did you bring that to the attention of Ms. Nunley?
Plaintiff: Yes, I did. I even sent her a copy of it.
Judge: Okay. Can I see that? The copy of—let me see the medical records with the diagnosis. And if you have any emails to her, that would be great. Or did you mail it?
Plaintiff: No, I mailed it.
Judge: Okay. And you deny ever having received the medical records with anything with the vet statement or showing who the vet was? Or you didn't get this vet report that says Foxboro Animal Hospital with the receipt?
Defendant: The one I got—does it have the vet's name or where it came from?
Judge: Bilateral hip dysplasia. What day was the purchase of the dog?
Defendant: It was in October.
Judge: He got the pup in August?
Plaintiff: Yes, in August.
Judge: Okay. This is in August of what year?
Plaintiff: 2011.
Judge: Okay. This vet report is from February 10, 2012. Now, let me ask you a question. Was there a contract between you two for the purchase of the dog?
Defendant: Yes. Our purchase agreement is on our website, and he stated he read it.
Judge: Can I see that?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: Do you—wait, no, no, I’m still on my question. Do you have people sign it, scan it, and send it back to you, or do you have them click something that says—
Defendant: We like for them to print it out and send it back to us, but a lot of people say they don’t have a printer and can’t send it back.
Judge: Okay. How do you record their acknowledging the terms and everything?
Defendant: Well, I ask them. It’s on our website. It’s also where the puppy’s picture is—it states it right here, too.
Judge: Let me see all that. Did he reach out to you and tell you, “Listen, I’ve just found out that the dog has hip dysplasia”?
Defendant: Yes. And I asked him to send me the vet reports and the vet statement stating so.
Judge: When you got stuff and it was missing something you wanted, did you call him to say, “Hey, I still don’t have the name of the vet”?
Defendant: I tracked that down myself. He didn’t send me any.
Judge: Oh, so you got on Google and made a lot of phone calls?
Defendant: Yeah.
Judge: But how could you track it down without the name of the vet?
Defendant: A lot of phone calls and asking if they knew this gentleman and that dog.
Judge: So, you’re the one who tracked down the report?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Okay, so it’s hip dysplasia, right?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: My verdict in this case is for the defendant. Good luck, though. She is a beautiful dog, and I hope she doesn’t end up needing surgery.

Judge: I was just going to ask, but the defense counsel would, of course, know that this witness is going to testify, right?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: And could make appropriate inquiries about the nature of his testimony before.
And so, after he has testified, he could presumably stand up and ask the expert, Mr. Expert, are you saying that in every case, someone knows that the drugs are in the car? He, presumably, would say no.
And are you saying—are you stating an opinion about whether this individual knew she had drugs in the car? He would have to say no, right? So I don't understand—I mean, obviously, you would like a case in which this was a very rare occurrence, but all he's saying is that it's not.
It's whatever he wants to say, in the majority of cases or whatever, and you can challenge that and you can particularly make clear that what the concern was about in drafting this rule, you can make clear that he is not telling you anything about this particular individual.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Let me say two things about that, Your Honor.
First of all, I just want to return to the thing I said in the opening, which is, if the expert were to testify that I think there's an 80 percent chance this particular defendant had the mens rea, the same kind of cross-examination could occur.
You would say, Mr. Expert, you're not sure, are you? It's possible he didn't have it? Only—you know, it's only you're saying probably? And I think it would be very hard to argue Rule 704(b) would not cover that testimony.
Judge: Well, I don't think—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: And the reason why—
Judge: Go ahead.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Oh.
I think the reason why is because what the drafters of the rule cared about was not an absolute versus probabilistic opinion.
What they cared about was the expert assigning a mens rea to the defendant or a class of people like the defendant.
In other words, it's the subject of mens rea that's off limits, not—
Judge: Well, I don't know that—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: —absolute testimony.
Judge: I—I certainly don't have experience in—in trials, but—but I don't know that—could he really say there's an 80 percent chance that this individual had drugs? I think he would have to say more generally that 80 percent of the people that I've studied or whatever have drugs.
I don't know that he can ascribe—maybe he shouldn't be able to ascribe a number to this individual.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well—well—
Judge: Because there may well be situations where—and, again, the defense counsel can bring them up—why this person would not be like the typical individual that the expert—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Right.
Judge: —is testifying about.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, after Rule 704(b), of course, the expert couldn't, but I think, before Rule 704(b), an expert could say—for example, an expert could say, absolutely, as happened in the Hinckley trial, I believe this defendant did not have the capacity to tell right from wrong, so, therefore, he is insane.
And I think after—and I think Congress would have prohibited that in Rule 704(b) just like it would prohibit the expert from saying I think most likely Mr. Hinckley was insane and couldn't tell right from wrong.
And whether you say that in terms of a percentage or just a verbal locution that's "probably," "usually," "most of the time," "almost certainly," any of those things would be covered by the rule because they're all an opinion about whether the defendant had that mens rea.
Judge: But, Mr.—
Judge: Mr.—
Judge: Mr. Lawyer—
Judge: —Mr. Lawyer, the—the Ninth Circuit's rule is clear.
Your rule, to my mind, is unclear.
I still don't understand it.
If you don't think that anything from which the jury could infer mens rea is barred by 704, then I don't know where you draw the line.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, Your Honor, I think it might help to understand what you have to decide in this case if I give you a quick preface to that answer.
Judge: Well, just give me what the rule should be.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: The rule should be that any testimony that the jury would naturally understand as expressing an opinion about the defendant's mens rea is covered by Rule 704(b).
Judge: So that—if it's—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: That raises—
Judge: —if it's relevant—all right.
Suppose the—suppose the—the expert is—is asked, how many cases do you have personal familiarity with in which a person drove across the border with a large quantity of drugs hidden in the car? Eighty-three cases.
And how many of those cases did the person driving the car know that the drugs were there? Eighty-three. Is that barred?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: I think that would be barred because it would be equivalent of saying defendants in this position always have the requisite mens rea.
Judge: Well, it's just a statement of fact about his—about his—what he has personal knowledge of.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, remember, the state—the expert testimony here—and this is at Pet. App. 13A—was admitted as an expert opinion about whether the defendant had the requisite mens rea.
It's opinion evidence—
Judge: All right.
Well, this isn't—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: —not facts evidence.
Judge: Okay.
So it would be okay if it's not opinion evidence?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, I think it functionally would be opinion evidence regardless of whether you characterized it.
But, Your Honor, let me—I think it might help the conversation if I explained the Ninth Circuit's rule raises really two questions.
One is, is the expert testimony about mens rea and is it about the defendant?
And—and—and we agree with the—with the Fifth Circuit rule that if you—if the expert gives testimony that—from which the jury can merely draw an inference about mens rea, that's perfectly fine, and there might be borderline cases about what's mens rea, but that's not this case because the expert used the word "know."
The expert used the word "aware."
Judge: Well, can I test that—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: So the only question—
Judge: —that question about inference?
So suppose that the expert gets on the stand and says: In my experience, drug traffickers always inform their carriers before they head out about the nature of the scheme.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Right.
Judge: Would that come under your rule or would it not?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: I think it would probably barely fall outside of our rule, Your Honor, and that's—but you have identified an edge case, but the reason—
Judge: Yeah, it's—it's—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: —why it wouldn't fall in—
Judge: —very much like—I—I sort of expected—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Yeah.
Judge: —you to say that—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Right.

Defendant: I have no problem because I have thick skin, but once you bring in my children, I think that's highly inappropriate.
Judge: I didn't even know you have children. I don't know what you're talking about your children. What about your children?
Defendant: For them to comment on my children is highly improper before this court to allow that kind of testing.
Judge: There was, I don't remember any comments about any children and if there was, it obviously didn't—it came and went without me noticing it because I can assure you that…

Chief Judge: We'll hear argument next in Case 22-913, Devillier versus Texas.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The question presented in this case is resolved by the text of the Fifth Amendment, which, unlike any other provision of the Constitution, imposes on the government an explicit duty to pay money.
It's also answered by this Court's decision in First English, which holds that the just compensation remedy is mandatory and that the Fifth Amendment itself furnishes a basis on which a court can award just compensation in an inverse condemnation case.
And this right of property owners to sue in inverse condemnation to obtain just compensation for an alleged taking is at the heart of modern American takings law.
It's at the heart of inverse condemnation claims filed against state and local governments nationwide. And it's also at the heart of every takings claim filed against the federal government under the Tucker Act.
The Tucker Act provides no cause of action, no substantive entitlement to a remedy. The cause of action, the substantive entitlement to a remedy, in every Tucker Act takings case is the self-executing Fifth Amendment—the same cause of action recognized in First English, the same cause of action pled here.
To reject that cause of action now is to upend the way lower courts, both state and federal, understand the Takings Clause to work and also to abandon this Court's consistent explanations of that clause—not just in First English but in more recent cases like Knick v. Township of Scott. And there's no reason to make that kind of drastic change.
This Court has already recognized that money-mandating legal obligations logically come along with the right to file a lawsuit to enforce those obligations.
That's true as to statutes, which is what this Court held in Maine Community Health Options.
It should be at least as true as to the Constitution, and this Court's precedents consistently teach that it is.
I welcome the Court's questions.
Judge: In your reply brief, you say that the 19th-century federal courts were faced with a bedrock property right and no way to enforce it directly. Doesn't that seem to be at odds—the fact that the courts there had to resort to extra-constitutional causes of action—isn't that at odds with your argument now?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: I don't think so, Your Honor, because the primary problem facing federal courts in the early part of the 19th century was a lack of jurisdiction.
And I think the question of jurisdiction is just conceptually distinct from the question of whether there's a cause of action, whether there's a right to a remedy.
Congress could tomorrow amend Section 1331 to reimpose an amount-in-controversy limit, and if it did that, that would prevent a number of people from bringing Ex parte Young actions in federal court.
Those claims wouldn't cease to exist. Congress would just have eliminated the jurisdiction over them.
And so I think there's a difference between jurisdictional limits—which limited takings claims—and even pleading requirements like the limits to the forms of action, which also limited plaintiffs' abilities to bring certain kinds of claims, and the core question presented here, which is just whether there is an entitlement to relief.
There's only one modern form of action, which just takes the shape of saying, I'm entitled to this remedy for that reason. The remedy is just compensation.
The reason is the Fifth Amendment as applied through the Fourteenth.
And once the jurisdictional problems and the pleading problems are removed, as they have been in this case, the only question remains whether the Fifth Amendment mandates compensation, whether it mandates that remedy, which this Court has already answered.
First English says that the just compensation remedy is mandatory. And I think contrasting the arguments of the other side with the rule adopted by the California Supreme Court in Agins is actually a useful illustration here.
The California Supreme Court's decision in Agins said, "We as a common law court don't want to recognize a claim for just compensation in a regulatory takings case. We think that intrudes on the legislature's prerogative. We don't recognize that cause of action."
And First English says that doesn't matter. The cause of action, the entitlement to relief, flows directly from the Fifth Amendment.
So too here. The complaint here pleads a cause of action directly under the Fifth Amendment—
Judge: Counsel—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: —that says our property was taken and the Fifth Amendment—
Judge: Counsel, I agree that jurisdiction and a cause of action are distinct, but it's a little bit hard to see how in 1791— I mean, I think your argument is, when the Fifth Amendment was ratified, those who ratified it had to see the Fifth Amendment as itself supplying the cause of action because this was the crucial way to vitiate the takings right, the right to just compensation.
But Congress didn't provide for federal question jurisdiction until 1875, so that kind of languished on the vine for a pretty long time if you're right that the founding generation—or the ratifying generation in 1791—viewed it that way.
Moreover, the historical evidence of private bills runs contrary to your argument because, yes, there was a right to just compensation, but we have all of this time throughout the 19th century of Congress enacting private bills to give just compensation.
