Their only argument is that even the most perfect agency adjudication, if it has an exhaustion rule, is preempted.
And we think that, as Justice Kagan was alluding to with Felder, that that rule has already been rejected by this Court.
But looking at this as-applied challenge to Alabama's law, we don't think that it is the case that it functions as an immunity. Mandamus is still available.
There is still the potential for a futility argument, one that was not raised below.
And so it's simply not the case that it is impossible to get an adjudication here.
Judge: I'm not sure how it is possible given that, as I read the court's decision below, you have to bring the due process argument to the agency, and I don't see any procedure to do that within the agency.
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, Your Honor --
Judge: There's nothing that -- there's no mandamus within the agency. I don't know how the mandamus court would have jurisdiction because it requires exhaustion.
So you're in a loop that I'm not sure how this claimant gets out of.
Lawyer of Defendant: Your Honor, I don't think mandamus would require exhaustion.
The point of mandamus would be to break the sort of logjam that Plaintiffs are complaining about here, but they didn't take advantage of mandamus.
Judge: Well, Alabama -- Alabama ruled very broadly.
It didn't say that Mr. Williams, for example, who had a final judgment that he was attacking, had a claim that could be heard in court.
And you seem to concede in your briefs that he did.
He got his claim thrown out because he didn't file a notice of claim.
Then he brought a due process argument saying that the notice he received was inadequate or the time limits were not proper under due process.
And the Alabama court dismissed his action as unexhausted.
That sounds to me as if the due process claim has to be exhausted within the agency.
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, Your Honor, if -- if he had exhausted it and it was final, he had the ability to file a direct appeal to the circuit court.
Judge: He did.
Lawyer of Defendant: No, Your Honor --
Judge: And the court -- he did.
The court did what it did.
And, all of a sudden, he goes up to the Alabama Supreme Court and it dismisses it as unexhausted.
Lawyer of Defendant: Your Honor, I think it's important to remember how this was actually litigated before the state courts.
Their primary argument, if you go back to their briefs before the state courts, was that this exhaustion requirement didn't apply to sort of procedural challenges whatsoever.
There was no 1983 claim --
Judge: Well, please just tell me what form -- where is it in this process that tells litigants, if we're not acting, you can go to the appeals council and file something and get them to order the agency to do something, and then, if the agency doesn't do it, maybe you've exhausted and you have a futility argument to go seek mandamus or to go do something in state court.
This is a Kafkaesque type of process that's going on here.
Lawyer of Defendant: Your Honor, I don't think so.
Alabama case law makes clear that mandamus is available and that you may be able to raise futility arguments.
Judge: It's discretionary mandamus, isn't it?
Lawyer of Defendant: Correct, but we -- we presume that state courts --
Judge: And I don't know how --
Lawyer of Defendant: -- are going to follow the federal Constitution.
Judge: -- I actually don't know how mandamus operates with respect to the exhaustion requirement.
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, and that just points out the waiver problems that I was addressing earlier.
None of this was briefed up below.
None of this was argued in the blue brief either.
And so what we are dealing with here is this categorical rule.
And we don't think that even Patsy supports this categorical rule.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Well, General, on -- on that question, you started the day but with the -- the text of federal law does not preempt, you know, and that's a fair argument and a good argument.
It's just the argument that was in the dissent in Felder.
Justice O'Connor made exactly those same points, but it was a dissent in Felder.
And in the majority in Felder, it made clear that the exhaustion requirement by Wisconsin's law was problematic. It said it "doesn't involve lengthy or expensive administrative proceedings, but it forces injured persons to seek satisfaction from those alleged to have caused the injury in the first place.
Such a dispute resolution system may have much to commend it, but that is a judgment the current Congress must make." In other words, the language in Felder does seem -- contra Justice O'Connor's dissent, the language in the majority in Felder does seem to suggest that exhaustion requirements generally -- and we can talk about how to define those -- but generally are preempted even though there's no text.
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, a few things on that point, Your Honor.
First is Felder did not just say Patsy ruled and then, in a two-page opinion, end the case.
There was a detailed, nearly 15-page preemption analysis there.
And the Court made clear there were really two key problems with the Wisconsin law.
One was that, in purpose and effect, it minimized liability.
And so there's your clear conflict.
You have a federal law designed to impose liability and a state law designed to minimize it.
I think that is the through line from Felder to Howlett to Haywood, is those are immunity laws.
And, second, the Court said that the law in Wisconsin frequently and predictably defeated the 1983 claim.
Now "defeat" means that you're going to exercise jurisdiction over it and then dismiss it with prejudice because it is an affirmative defense that the defendants were handed by Wisconsin, and that did result in dismissals with prejudice.
Jurisdiction -- like the law at issue in Alabama, jurisdiction's not an affirmative defense.
You would never say that an amount in controversy requirement is an affirmative defense.
And it doesn't defeat the right.
It simply says we're not going to consider it.
And so there's no way for Alabama to alter the 1983 right by merely --

The People's Court Transcript
Judge: Good afternoon. We are here today to hear the case of Mr. Johnson versus Mr. Reynolds regarding the sale of a 1969 Chevy Camaro. Mr. Johnson, you are the plaintiff. Please state your claim.
Plaintiff: Yes, Your Honor. I purchased a 1969 Chevy Camaro from Mr. Reynolds for $25,000. He assured me the car was in excellent condition and had no significant mechanical issues. However, after I took possession of the vehicle, I discovered multiple mechanical problems, including a faulty transmission and significant rust damage that had been covered up.
Judge: Mr. Reynolds, how do you respond to these allegations?
Defendant: Your Honor, I sold the car in good faith. I disclosed everything I knew about its condition at the time of sale. Mr. Johnson inspected the car before purchasing it, and I even allowed him to take it for a test drive. Any issues that arose after the sale are beyond my control.
Judge: Mr. Johnson, did you have a mechanic inspect the car before purchasing it?
Plaintiff: No, I did not. I trusted Mr. Reynolds’ word and the visual inspection I conducted myself.
Judge: Mr. Reynolds, did you provide any written documentation regarding the condition of the car?
Defendant: No, Your Honor. It was a verbal agreement, but I did state that the car was being sold “as-is.”
Judge: Mr. Johnson, were you aware that the car was being sold “as-is”?
Plaintiff: He did not explicitly say “as-is” when we spoke, but there was nothing in writing about it.
Judge: Mr. Reynolds, did you intentionally conceal any issues with the car?
Defendant: Absolutely not, Your Honor. The car was in good running condition when I sold it.
Judge: Do either of you have any evidence to present?
Plaintiff: Yes, I have a report from a mechanic who inspected the vehicle after my purchase. It states that the transmission was in poor condition before I even bought it, and that rust damage had been concealed.
Judge: Mr. Reynolds, do you have any counter-evidence?
Defendant: No, Your Honor. I did not conduct a pre-sale inspection myself, but I was driving the car without issues before selling it.
Judge: Based on the evidence presented, it is clear that the car had pre-existing issues that were not disclosed to the buyer. While the sale was verbal, the seller had an obligation to be transparent about significant defects.
Judge: My ruling is in favor of the plaintiff. Mr. Reynolds, you are ordered to compensate Mr. Johnson for repair costs totaling $4,500. Court is adjourned.

Judge: Thank you, Douglas. Leia Franklin, you are suing James Waller for $5,000. You say you're actually out more than that because, according to you, he sold you a lemon. What happened here?
Plaintiff: I was recommended to him, and, you know, because I knew him, I went to him. I told him about a situation I was having with my previous car, and he told me he had two cars available. I said okay, how much do you want for them? He said one was $1,400 and the other—
Judge: Now, are these cars that he owned, or did he take you to a wholesaler? What happened there?
Plaintiff: He said that he was the dealer, that they were his cars, and that he was selling them. He said he was going to give me a good price because going through him would be a whole lot cheaper.
Judge: Okay, so you picked out your car?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And what did you pick out?
Plaintiff: A 2015 Honda Accord Hybrid for $1,400.
Judge: Okay. And when you met him, what happened?
Plaintiff: The first car he showed me, he said it had a $50 pin missing from the clutch. So I said, "Okay, no, I won’t take this one." Then I asked, "What’s wrong with the hybrid?" He said, "Nothing’s wrong with it. It’s perfectly fine. It just needs a regular tune-up and stuff like that."
Judge: Did you have a mechanic come and look at it to make sure that it was okay?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: So you didn’t get it inspected before purchasing?
Plaintiff: He had a mechanic on—
Judge: No, no. I don’t want to know about his mechanic. I’m asking, did you, the one buying the car, have a mechanic check it?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: Go on.
Plaintiff: I looked at the car. I started it up. It needed gas. And then I said, "Are you sure it’s okay?" He said, "Yes, nothing’s wrong with the car." He said, "If anything’s wrong with it, I’ll fix it."
Judge: Did he put that in writing, that warranty you just mentioned?
Plaintiff: No, he didn’t.
Judge: Because this is a 13-year-old car. So if somebody says, "If anything’s wrong with it, I’ll fix it," that’s really important to be able to prove.
(To Defendant) Did you tell her that if anything was wrong with the 2015 Accord, you would pay to fix it?
Defendant: No, I did not.
Judge: Yeah, watch out. Because when you say something like that, you better be able to prove it, because that kind of amazing warranty on a used car doesn’t usually happen.
(To Plaintiff) So, you took the car home. When did you start having problems?
Plaintiff: To be honest with you, after I put gas in it. I drove it maybe 20 minutes. I told him, "I really hope I didn’t just buy a messed-up car." He said, "No, the car is fine." Then he asked, "What kind of gas did you put in?" I said, "89."
Judge: And what did he say?
Plaintiff: He said, "That’s what made the check engine light come on."
Judge: Where did you get the idea that putting in the wrong octane gas can cause a check engine light to come on?
Defendant: From mechanics, ma’am. From having previous cars and putting bad gas in my own cars.
Judge: Okay, go on.
Plaintiff: I called him, but we were both on the parkway, leaving from Queens back to Long Island, so he didn’t answer. When I got home, he called me back and said, "It’s because you put 89 in it."
Judge: And then what?
Plaintiff: He told me to "drive it out." So I drove the gas out and put in 93, but the check engine light was still on.
Judge: How many days later was this?
Plaintiff: Maybe a day and a half.
Judge: And then?
Plaintiff: I called him and told him the check engine light didn’t go off.
Judge: When did you finally take it to a mechanic?
Plaintiff: Maybe that following Wednesday.
Judge: How long after purchasing the car?
Plaintiff: Three or four days.
Judge: And what did the mechanic tell you?
Plaintiff: He gave me a report and told me I shouldn’t have bought this car.
Judge: Let me see what the mechanic gave you.
(Plaintiff hands over documents.)
Judge: And this bill of sale—when was it prepared?
Defendant: More than a week after she got the vehicle. And actually, I just assisted her in acquiring a vehicle. I took her to A1 Auto Sales.
Judge: That’s not you?
Defendant: No, I’m an independent contractor under A1.
Judge: How did you make money on the $1,400?
Defendant: I didn’t make any money on her because I knew her.
Judge: Why didn’t you make money?
Defendant: She bought a 13-year-old car, didn’t get it checked out, and now wants a refund.
Judge: Why are you signing a bill of sale a week later if you had problems in the first three days?
Plaintiff: Because he told me—
Judge: No, no. Why?
Plaintiff: He said—
Judge: The default position is as-is for a used car.
Plaintiff: But the mileage was wrong!
Judge: Prove that.
(Plaintiff hands over another document.)
Judge: This doesn’t prove mileage fraud. My verdict is for the defendant.
(Bangs gavel.)

Judge: And then you could have brought it to the Fifth Circuit.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: That's what we did.
Judge: Well --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: We did argue --
Judge: -- yeah, but you didn't argue it at the time when -- you -- you didn't bring it.
You didn't argue it at the time that you moved to intervene.
Judge: Did you --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: That's incorrect. That is -- I --
Judge: Did you --did you say --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: -- would like to set the record straight.
That's not correct.
Judge: Go ahead.
Go ahead.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yeah.
We moved to dismiss.
The very first motion we filed said: This is not within your statutory authority.
We moved to intervene for multiple purposes.
Judge: And then you didn't take it up to the Fifth Circuit.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: We argued to the Fifth Circuit that the --
Judge: At -- at the second -- at the second --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Because that was the final order, Your Honor.
We challenged the final order as being outside the scope of the authority --
Judge: But you didn't do it at the first motion to --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: No, because the first motion only go -- went to could we intervene for purposes of bringing in evidence to the Commission.
And -- and the point here is: Are you going to allow agencies to manipulate their rules so that they can decide who gets to challenge them?
Judge: I'm sorry --
Judge: Quite frankly, I'm worried about party manipulation.
I'm trying to understand what basis you now have to say that we should be revisiting the D.C. Circuit's determination that you cannot intervene.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: I'm not asking you to do that.
Judge: Okay.
So, if we believe that the law is such that you had to be a party, do you concede that you were not a party at the lower court proceeding?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: No.
Judge: You do not concede?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: We do not concede that.
Judge: Okay.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: The Atomic --
Judge: And the basis for your part -- your party participation is what?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: The Atomic Energy Act says that if we are affected by the proceedings and we ask for a hearing, the NRC "shall admit" us as a party.
Judge: Well, that -- that says, Mr. Frederick, why you have an argument that you should have been a party.
And maybe you do have a good argument that you should have been a party.
But it -- it's not to say that you were a party.
In fact, you were not a party.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: No, I think that we were not a party in the sense that we were permitted to do the full evidentiary exposition that we might have liked to have done.
And I'm not arguing that we should have -- that that should be revisited.
But we are a party under the meaning -- the plain language of the Atomic Energy Act, which says we shall be a party and we shall have an opportunity to say in a judicial review setting --
Judge: But then you're saying that --that -- I mean, you know, when I look at this, your only participation in the agency proceeding was to be excluded from it.
But then you're saying: Well, if I was excluded wrongly, I'm a party.
I mean, how could that be? I mean, that's -- that's -- that's very much against the way we think of this in a judicial context, right, where we look at somebody and they've tried to intervene, and maybe they've been wronged, maybe the court was wrong to say that they can't intervene, but we don't say: Oh, the court was wrong.
They really should have been there, and so we're going to give them an opportunity to come in at some later point in time and attack the judgment.
It just doesn't work that way.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Well, I would say this is not the normal agency proceeding.
The Pacific Legal Foundation says that they have looked at the various agencies.
This is the only agency that serves as a gatekeeper to its own proceedings.
That's point one.
Point two, there are different scopes of party participation.
One is that you participate as a full party, bring depositions, bring other evidence.
That is not what we are challenging here.
What we are saying is that the plain language of the Act gives us the right to say for the first time in court, because the agency won't allow us to say it in the proceedings, you don't have the statutory authority for what you did.

Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: She should have called the police.
Defendant: Sure.
